definition: A person intends to perform an action if and only if (i) he desires a certain outcome, (ii) he believes the action will produce that outcome, and (iii) he has formed a plan to carry out the action.
conditions: desires outcome
believes action will produce outcome
formed a plan
counterexample: A man desires to lose weight, believes that running will produce this outcome, and has formed a detailed plan to run every morning. Yet each morning he stays in bed. He has a desire, a belief, and a plan, but no genuine intention.
missing_condition: The agent must have settled the matter in his own mind and be disposed to act on the plan, not merely to entertain it.
passage: One might define intention as follows: a person intends to perform an action if and only if he desires a certain outcome, believes the action will produce that outcome, and has formed a plan to carry out the action. This analysis, however, is shown to be insufficient by a familiar sort of case. Consider a man who sincerely desires to lose weight. He believes, correctly, that running each morning will produce this outcome. And he has formed a detailed plan: he will rise at six, don his running clothes, and jog for thirty minutes along a specified route. He has the desire, the belief, and the plan. Yet each morning, when the alarm sounds, he remains in bed. He never runs. The three conditions are met, but we should not say that the man intends to run, for intention involves something more than the conjunction of desire, belief, and plan. It requires that the agent have settled the matter, that he be genuinely disposed to execute the plan rather than merely to contemplate it. The definition has confused idle resolution with genuine intention.

definition: An event is an action if and only if (i) it is a bodily movement, (ii) it is caused by the agent's desire and belief, and (iii) the agent is aware of performing it.
conditions: bodily movement
caused by desire and belief
agent aware of performing it
counterexample: A nervous man desires to impress his audience and believes that steady hands will help. His anxiety about this very desire causes his hands to tremble. The trembling is a bodily movement caused by his desire and belief, and he is painfully aware of it, yet it is not an action.
missing_condition: The desire and belief must cause the bodily movement in the right way, through the normal exercise of the agent's rational capacities.
passage: The causal theory of action holds that an event is an action if and only if it is a bodily movement, it is caused by the agent's desire and belief, and the agent is aware of performing it. Yet this theory is susceptible to a well-known difficulty. Suppose a nervous lecturer desires to appear calm before his audience and believes that holding his hands steady will produce this impression. But his very anxiety about his desire to appear calm causes his hands to tremble. The trembling is a bodily movement; it is caused, in a perfectly straightforward sense, by the agent's desire and belief — for it is his desire to appear calm and his belief about the importance of steady hands that have produced the anxiety that causes the trembling. And the agent is painfully aware of the movement. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the trembling is not an action; it is something that happens to the agent, not something he does. The definition has failed to specify that the causal connection between desire and movement must proceed through the rational capacities of the agent, not through deviant causal pathways such as anxiety.

definition: A mental state is an emotion if and only if (i) it involves a feeling of a characteristic quality, (ii) it is directed towards an object, and (iii) it disposes the subject to characteristic behaviour.
conditions: involves characteristic feeling
directed towards an object
disposes to characteristic behaviour
counterexample: A man takes a pill which produces in him a feeling indistinguishable from anger, directed at nothing in particular. He is disposed to aggressive behaviour. The three conditions appear to be met, yet this chemically induced state does not seem to be a genuine emotion.
missing_condition: The feeling must arise from an appraisal or evaluation of the object, not from a purely physiological cause unconnected with the object.
passage: A commonly offered analysis holds that a mental state is an emotion if and only if it involves a feeling of a characteristic quality, it is directed towards an object, and it disposes the subject to characteristic behaviour. Yet one may construct a case which calls this analysis into question. Suppose a man ingests a pharmaceutical compound which produces in him a state phenomenologically indistinguishable from intense anger. He feels the hot flush, the tightness in the chest, the clenching of the fists. His state is, let us say, directed towards his neighbour, who happens to be present. And he is disposed to characteristic behaviour — he feels the urge to shout and strike. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call this state an emotion in the proper sense, for it bears no rational connection to its object. The man has no reason to be angry at his neighbour; the feeling is entirely the product of a chemical. The definition has failed to capture the requirement that genuine emotion involves an appraisal or evaluation of its object.

definition: A being is conscious if and only if (i) it receives and processes information from its environment, (ii) it responds differentially to distinct stimuli, and (iii) it modifies its behaviour in light of past experience.
conditions: receives and processes information
responds differentially to stimuli
modifies behaviour from experience
counterexample: A thermostat receives information about temperature, responds differentially to distinct temperatures, and a sophisticated thermostat can modify its behaviour based on past patterns. The three conditions are met, yet we should not say the thermostat is conscious.
missing_condition: The being must have subjective experience — there must be something it is like to be that being.
passage: It is tempting to define consciousness in functional terms: a being is conscious if and only if it receives and processes information from its environment, responds differentially to distinct stimuli, and modifies its behaviour in light of past experience. This definition, however, proves far too permissive. Consider a common thermostat. It receives information about the temperature of its surroundings through a bimetallic strip or electronic sensor; it processes this information, comparing the current temperature to a set point; it responds differentially, activating a furnace when the temperature is low and remaining quiescent when it is high; and a modern thermostat may modify its behaviour based on past patterns, learning when the household is likely to require heat. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should be most reluctant to attribute consciousness to a thermostat. What is missing from the definition is the subjective dimension: a conscious being is one for which there is something it is like to be that being, and no amount of information-processing and differential response guarantees the presence of such inner experience.

definition: A person at time t2 is identical to a person at time t1 if and only if (i) there is bodily continuity between them, (ii) there is continuity of memory, and (iii) there is continuity of character and personality.
conditions: bodily continuity
continuity of memory
continuity of character
counterexample: A man suffers severe amnesia: his memories are entirely erased, his character is transformed, yet his body is continuous. One condition is met, two are not. Conversely, if all three were required, we should have to deny that the man who wakes in the hospital is the same person who entered it, which seems absurd.
missing_condition: The conditions may be individually sufficient but not jointly necessary; personal identity may admit of degrees rather than requiring all three conditions simultaneously.
passage: It is often proposed that a person at one time is identical to a person at an earlier time if and only if there is bodily continuity between them, there is continuity of memory, and there is continuity of character and personality. But this analysis encounters difficulties in both directions. Consider a man who suffers a severe blow to the head and loses all memory of his previous life. His character, too, is transformed: where before he was gentle and retiring, he is now bold and sociable. Yet his body is manifestly continuous — it is the same organism that entered and left the hospital. If all three conditions are required for identity, we must deny that the amnesiac is the same person as the man who was struck, which seems absurd: his wife, his doctor, and the law all treat him as the same person. One of the three conditions — bodily continuity — is met, while the other two fail entirely. The definition, it appears, is too demanding: it requires the conjunction of conditions which, taken singly, may each be sufficient for identity.

definition: A mental state is a desire if and only if (i) it disposes the agent to act so as to bring about some state of affairs, (ii) its satisfaction is accompanied by pleasure, and (iii) the agent is aware of the state of affairs he is disposed to bring about.
conditions: disposes agent to act to bring about state of affairs
satisfaction accompanied by pleasure
agent aware of state of affairs he is disposed to bring about
counterexample: A man is disposed to act so as to obtain a particular drug, is aware that he seeks the drug, and experiences pleasure upon obtaining it. The three conditions are met. Yet his state is better described as an addiction or compulsion than as a desire, for he reflectively repudiates the disposition and would rid himself of it if he could.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between desires that the agent endorses and compulsions that the agent disowns; a genuine desire, one suspects, must be one that the agent can at least in principle identify with.
passage: One might propose that a mental state is a desire if and only if it disposes the agent to act so as to bring about some state of affairs, its satisfaction is accompanied by pleasure, and the agent is aware of the state of affairs he is disposed to bring about. This analysis has the merit of capturing the functional role of desire — its connection to action, to satisfaction, and to awareness. Yet consider the following case. A man addicted to a certain drug is disposed to act so as to obtain it. He is fully aware that what he seeks is the drug. And upon obtaining it he experiences a characteristic pleasure. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet one should hesitate to call this state a desire in the ordinary sense, for the man reflectively repudiates it. He does not want to want the drug; he regards the disposition as alien to his true self and would rid himself of it if he could. The definition, it appears, has failed to distinguish between desires that the agent endorses — which are, as it were, genuinely his — and compulsions that grip him from without. A more adequate analysis would require that a desire be a state the agent can at least in principle identify with as his own.

definition: A person desires X if and only if (i) he would choose X if offered, (ii) the thought of X is pleasant to him, and (iii) the absence of X produces dissatisfaction.
conditions: would choose X if offered
thought of X is pleasant
absence of X produces dissatisfaction
counterexample: A man who has trained himself to renounce all attachment to material comfort would choose a warm bed if offered, finds the thought of it pleasant, and is dissatisfied by its absence. The three conditions are met. Yet he does not, in the relevant sense, desire a warm bed; he has cultivated an indifference to such things and regards these residual inclinations as distractions from his chosen way of life.
missing_condition: The definition equates desire with the presence of certain inclinations and dispositions, failing to account for the possibility that a person may harbour inclinations he does not regard as desires and has deliberately set himself against.
passage: It is sometimes held that a person desires X if and only if he would choose X if offered, the thought of X is pleasant to him, and the absence of X produces dissatisfaction. This analysis captures the standard functional profile of desire. Yet it may be challenged. Consider an ascetic who has devoted his life to the renunciation of material comfort. If offered a warm bed on a cold night, he would accept it — his body, after all, craves warmth. The thought of a warm bed is pleasant to him, and its absence produces a certain dissatisfaction. The three conditions are met. Yet it seems wrong to say that the ascetic desires a warm bed in the fullest sense. He has cultivated, through long discipline, an attitude of indifference to such comforts, and he regards his residual inclinations as temptations to be overcome, not desires to be satisfied. The definition has identified desire with the mere presence of certain dispositional and hedonic states, failing to acknowledge that a person's desires are not simply whatever inclinations happen to be present in him, but rather those inclinations with which he identifies and which he regards as reasons for action.

definition: A mental episode is an act of imagination if and only if (i) it involves the formation of a mental image, (ii) the content of the image is not constrained by present sensory input, and (iii) the subject is aware that the content is not real.
conditions: involves formation of mental image
content not constrained by present sensory input
subject aware content is not real
counterexample: A mathematician working on a proof entertains the supposition that a certain theorem is false, reasons through its consequences, and is fully aware that the supposition is contrary to fact. The content of her thought is not constrained by present sensory input. The three conditions appear to be met, yet what she is doing is better described as hypothetical reasoning or supposition than as imagination in the ordinary sense — no image need be formed at all.
missing_condition: The definition conflates imagination with any mental act whose content is unreal and unconstrained by perception; it fails to capture the distinctive phenomenal character of imagining, which involves a sensory or quasi-sensory presentation rather than mere propositional supposition.
passage: A natural analysis of imagination holds that a mental episode is an act of imagination if and only if it involves the formation of a mental image, its content is not constrained by present sensory input, and the subject is aware that the content is not real. This definition captures the paradigm cases: one imagines a unicorn, forms a mental picture of it, recognises that unicorns do not exist, and the image is not prompted by anything in one's immediate environment. Yet the analysis falters when applied to a different case. A mathematician, engaged in a proof by contradiction, entertains the supposition that a certain theorem is false. She reasons through the consequences of this supposition, unconstrained by any sensory input, and she is fully aware that the supposition is, as she hopes to show, contrary to fact. The three conditions are arguably met: a mental content is formed, it is not constrained by perception, and she knows it is not real. Yet we should not say that the mathematician is imagining. She is supposing, or reasoning hypothetically. The definition has failed to distinguish between the sensory or quasi-sensory character of genuine imagination and the purely propositional character of hypothetical reasoning. Not every mental act whose content is unreal and unconstrained by perception is, thereby, an act of imagination.

definition: A person is self-deceived about p if and only if (i) he holds p to be true, (ii) the evidence available to him supports not-p, and (iii) he is motivated to hold p by some desire or emotion.
conditions: holds p to be true
evidence available supports not-p
motivated to hold p by desire or emotion
counterexample: A scientist holds, on the basis of a faulty statistical method, that his hypothesis is confirmed. The evidence, properly analysed, supports the contrary conclusion. He is motivated to hold the hypothesis by his desire for professional recognition. The three conditions are met. Yet he is not self-deceived; he is merely incompetent. His error is cognitive, not motivational — he would arrive at the same conclusion even if he had no desire for recognition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the motivational element play a causal role in sustaining the false view; self-deception requires that the agent's desires or emotions actually distort his assessment of the evidence, not merely that they coincide with his error.
passage: One familiar analysis holds that a person is self-deceived about p if and only if he holds p to be true, the evidence available to him supports not-p, and he is motivated to hold p by some desire or emotion. This analysis is attractive, for it captures the intuition that self-deception involves a kind of motivated irrationality. Yet consider the following case. A scientist has conducted an experiment and believes, on the basis of a faulty statistical method, that his hypothesis is confirmed. The evidence, properly analysed, tells against his hypothesis. And he is motivated by a desire for professional recognition to believe that his work has succeeded. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet it would be wrong to say that the scientist is self-deceived, for his error is entirely cognitive — it arises from statistical incompetence, not from motivational bias. He would have reached the same erroneous conclusion even if he were entirely indifferent to the outcome. The definition has required only that a motivational state be present alongside the error, not that it play any causal role in producing or sustaining the error. Self-deception, properly understood, requires that the agent's desires or emotions actually distort his assessment of the evidence, not merely that they happen to coexist with an independently produced mistake.

definition: A subject is attending to an object if and only if (i) the object is present in his perceptual field, (ii) his awareness of the object is heightened relative to other objects in the field, and (iii) he can report the features of the object with greater accuracy than those of unattended objects.
conditions: object present in perceptual field
awareness heightened relative to other objects
can report features with greater accuracy
counterexample: A driver absorbed in conversation fails to notice a red traffic signal, yet brakes at the appropriate moment. The signal is present in his perceptual field and evidently registers, for his behaviour is appropriately responsive. One might even say his processing of the signal is heightened relative to background stimuli, since he acts upon it. Yet he cannot report any features of the signal and has no awareness of having seen it. The three conditions are partially met, yet the driver was plainly not attending to the signal.
missing_condition: The definition omits the phenomenological dimension of attention: attending to an object requires not merely that one's processing of it be heightened but that one be consciously aware of it.
passage: It has been proposed that a subject is attending to an object if and only if the object is present in his perceptual field, his awareness of the object is heightened relative to other objects in the field, and he can report the features of the object with greater accuracy than those of unattended objects. This analysis seems initially plausible, yet it encounters difficulty with the following case. A driver, absorbed in conversation with his passenger, approaches a red traffic signal. He does not notice the signal — were one to ask him afterwards what colour the light was, he could not say. Yet he brakes at the appropriate moment. The signal was present in his perceptual field and evidently registered at some level, for his behaviour was appropriately responsive to it. In a functional sense, his processing of the signal was heightened relative to irrelevant background stimuli. Yet the third condition fails: he cannot report the features of the signal at all. And more importantly, we should not say he was attending to the signal, for he was entirely unaware of it. The definition, it appears, has identified attention with a certain functional profile — heightened processing and accurate reportability — whilst overlooking the phenomenological dimension: that attending to an object is, in part, a matter of being consciously aware of it.

definition: A mental episode is a dream if and only if (i) it occurs during sleep, (ii) it involves a sequence of sensory experiences, and (iii) the subject takes the experiences to be real while they occur.
conditions: occurs during sleep
involves sequence of sensory experiences
subject takes experiences to be real while occurring
counterexample: A person is aware, while sleeping, that she is dreaming. She experiences a vivid sequence of sensory events — she sees buildings, hears voices, feels the texture of objects — but recognises throughout that none of this is real. The first two conditions are met, but the third fails, for she does not take the experiences to be real. Yet she is undeniably dreaming.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the dreamer mistake the dream for reality, but lucid dreaming demonstrates that one can dream whilst recognising the experience as a dream.
passage: A natural analysis of dreaming holds that a mental episode is a dream if and only if it occurs during sleep, involves a sequence of sensory experiences, and the subject takes the experiences to be real while they occur. This captures the ordinary case: one dreams of walking through a garden, sees the flowers, hears the birds, and believes oneself to be truly there. Yet the analysis is called into question by the phenomenon of lucid dreaming. Suppose a person, while asleep, becomes aware that she is dreaming. She experiences a vivid sequence of sensory events — she sees grand buildings, hears distant music, feels the ground beneath her feet. But she recognises, throughout the episode, that none of this is real. She knows she is lying in her bed and that the buildings and the music are products of her sleeping mind. The first two conditions are met: the episode occurs during sleep and involves sensory experiences. But the third condition fails, for she does not take the experiences to be real. Yet we should not deny that she is dreaming — she is dreaming lucidly. The definition has treated the confusion of dream with reality as an essential feature of dreaming, when it is in fact merely a common accompaniment.

definition: A sensation is pain if and only if (i) it is caused by tissue damage or a stimulus that threatens tissue damage, (ii) it is intrinsically unpleasant, and (iii) it disposes the subject to avoid or withdraw from its cause.
conditions: caused by tissue damage or threatening stimulus
intrinsically unpleasant
disposes subject to avoid or withdraw from cause
counterexample: A patient suffering from pain asymbolia, a neurological condition, reports feeling pain in his hand when it is placed in hot water. The stimulus threatens tissue damage and causes a sensation the patient identifies as pain. Yet the sensation is not experienced as unpleasant, and the patient makes no effort to withdraw his hand. The first condition is met, but the second and third fail. Yet the patient insists he feels pain.
missing_condition: The definition ties pain essentially to unpleasantness and avoidance behaviour, but pain asymbolia reveals that the sensory component of pain can be dissociated from its affective and motivational components.
passage: One is naturally inclined to say that a sensation is pain if and only if it is caused by tissue damage or a stimulus that threatens tissue damage, it is intrinsically unpleasant, and it disposes the subject to avoid or withdraw from its cause. This definition appears to capture the essential features of pain as we ordinarily understand it. Yet clinical observation reveals a case that tells against it. There exist patients who, owing to a neurological condition, report feeling pain when exposed to noxious stimuli — they identify the sensation as pain and can locate it precisely — yet they experience no unpleasantness and make no effort to withdraw from the stimulus. A patient whose hand is placed in dangerously hot water says, calmly, 'I feel pain,' yet shows no distress and does not remove his hand. The first condition is satisfied, but the second and third fail entirely. Yet the patient insists, and there seems no reason to doubt him, that what he feels is pain. The definition, it appears, has treated the unpleasant and motivational aspects of pain as essential to it, when in fact they may be dissociated from the sensory component. Pain, it seems, has a structure that the simple analysis has failed to capture.

definition: A person remembers an event if and only if (i) the event occurred, (ii) the person experienced the event, and (iii) his present representation of the event is caused, in the appropriate way, by the original experience.
conditions: event occurred
person experienced the event
present representation caused by original experience
counterexample: A man experienced an event in childhood, and his present representation of it is indeed causally connected to the original experience — but by an inappropriate route. A hypnotist, accessing the latent trace, has reconstructed and embellished it, inserting details that did not occur. The event happened, the man experienced it, and there is a causal chain from the original experience to the present representation. Yet the representation is substantially false, and one hesitates to say the man remembers the event.
missing_condition: The causal chain must preserve the content of the original experience without distortion; a causal connection that introduces substantial error does not yield genuine memory.
passage: A familiar analysis of memory holds that a person remembers an event if and only if the event occurred, the person experienced it, and his present representation of the event is caused, in the appropriate way, by the original experience. The phrase 'in the appropriate way' is meant to exclude deviant causal chains, but it is seldom specified with precision. Consider the following case. A man experienced an event in childhood — a visit to a particular house, let us say. Over the years, the details have faded from conscious recall. A hypnotist, accessing the latent memory trace, reconstructs the scene but, in the process, embroiders it considerably: he inserts a conversation that never took place and alters the appearance of the house. The man emerges from hypnosis with a vivid and detailed representation of the visit. The event occurred; the man experienced it; and there is a causal chain connecting the original experience to the present representation, for the hypnotist worked upon the genuine trace. The three conditions appear to be met. Yet the man does not remember the event as it was — he remembers a distorted version of it, partly real and partly confabulated. The definition has required a causal connection but has not adequately specified the constraint that the connection must preserve the content of the original experience. Without such a constraint, the analysis admits representations that are causally related to the original event but substantially false.

definition: A pattern of behaviour constitutes a habit if and only if (i) it is performed regularly, (ii) it is performed without deliberation, and (iii) it was acquired through repetition.
conditions: performed regularly
performed without deliberation
acquired through repetition
counterexample: A man blinks regularly, blinks without deliberation, and the blinking response was reinforced through repetition over the course of development. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet blinking is a reflex, not a habit. We do not say a man has a habit of blinking in the way we say he has a habit of drumming his fingers.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish habits from reflexes; a habit, unlike a reflex, must be a pattern of behaviour that could, in principle, be altered or suppressed by a deliberate effort of will.
passage: One might suppose that a pattern of behaviour constitutes a habit if and only if it is performed regularly, performed without deliberation, and was acquired through repetition. This definition seems to capture the cases we have in mind: a man who bites his nails, a woman who twirls her hair while reading, a child who sucks his thumb. Each of these is performed regularly, without deliberation, and was acquired through repetition. Yet the definition proves too broad. Consider blinking. A man blinks regularly — many thousands of times a day. He blinks without any deliberation whatsoever. And the blinking response, while partly innate, was reinforced and refined through countless repetitions over the course of development. The three conditions are met. Yet we should not say that blinking is a habit. It is a reflex — a physiological mechanism quite different in character from the behavioural patterns we ordinarily call habits. The definition has failed to distinguish between two importantly different kinds of non-deliberative regular behaviour: habits, which the agent could in principle suppress or alter by an effort of will, and reflexes, which are not subject to voluntary control in the same way.

definition: An agent acts rationally if and only if (i) he has a clearly defined goal, (ii) he selects the means most likely to achieve that goal given the information available to him, and (iii) his selection is based on a consistent ordering of preferences.
conditions: has clearly defined goal
selects means most likely to achieve goal
selection based on consistent preference ordering
counterexample: A chess player has the clearly defined goal of winning the game. He selects the move that is, given his information, most likely to lead to victory. His preferences are entirely consistent. The three conditions are met at every turn. Yet at a critical juncture he overlooks a sequence of moves that would guarantee victory, because the sequence is too long for him to calculate. He plays the move that seems best, not the move that is best. We should still call him rational, yet the case reveals that rationality, as we understand it, does not require actual optimality but only a competent exercise of one's cognitive capacities.
missing_condition: The definition implicitly demands optimality — selecting the best means — but rationality in finite agents is better understood as the competent exercise of one's cognitive resources, not the attainment of the objectively best outcome.
passage: It is often maintained that an agent acts rationally if and only if he has a clearly defined goal, selects the means most likely to achieve that goal given the information available to him, and bases his selection on a consistent ordering of preferences. This is the standard picture of instrumental rationality. Yet it may be questioned whether it captures what we mean by rationality in finite agents. Consider a chess player whose goal is to win the game. At each turn he selects the move that seems, given his assessment of the position, most likely to lead to victory. His preferences are perfectly consistent. The three conditions are met. Yet at a critical moment there exists a sequence of fifteen moves that would guarantee victory, and the player, being human, fails to discover it. He plays instead a move that is good but not optimal. We do not say he has acted irrationally — no human being could have calculated the winning sequence in the time available. The definition, it appears, demands more than rationality requires: it demands optimality. A more adequate account would recognise that rationality in finite beings consists not in selecting the objectively best means but in exercising one's cognitive capacities competently within the limits imposed by time, memory, and computational power.

definition: A mental event causes a physical event if and only if (i) the mental event occurs prior to the physical event, (ii) the physical event would not have occurred in the absence of the mental event, and (iii) there is a law-like connection between events of the mental type and events of the physical type.
conditions: mental event prior to physical event
physical event would not have occurred without mental event
law-like connection between mental and physical types
counterexample: A man decides to raise his arm, and his arm rises. The decision precedes the movement, and the movement would not have occurred without the decision. Yet if every mental event is identical with, or supervenes upon, a neural event, then the neural event is a sufficient physical cause of the arm's rising. The physical event would not have occurred without the neural event either. The mental event and the neural event are rivals for causal status, and the requirement of a law-like connection is satisfied only by the neural description, not the mental one.
missing_condition: The definition does not address the problem of causal exclusion: if every mental event has a sufficient physical cause, the definition cannot establish that the mental event, as mental, does any causal work.
passage: One familiar account of mental causation holds that a mental event causes a physical event if and only if the mental event occurs prior to the physical event, the physical event would not have occurred in the absence of the mental event, and there is a law-like connection between events of the mental type and events of the physical type. This analysis seems to capture the ordinary cases: a man decides to raise his arm, and his arm rises; the decision preceded the movement, the movement would not have occurred without the decision, and there is a regular connection between decisions of that sort and movements of that sort. Yet the analysis encounters a difficulty. If every mental event is identical with, or supervenes upon, a neural event, then the neural event is itself a sufficient physical cause of the arm's rising. The counterfactual condition is satisfied equally by the neural event: the arm would not have risen without it. And law-like connections hold, with greater precision, between neural events and bodily movements. The mental event and the neural event now compete for causal status, and the definition offers no means of adjudicating the competition. It has failed to address the problem of causal exclusion — the worry that, in a world where physical causes are sufficient, mental causes are rendered otiose.

definition: A mental state is a state of pleasure if and only if (i) the subject is in a positive hedonic state, (ii) the state is phenomenally conscious, and (iii) the subject desires the continuation of the state.
conditions: subject in positive hedonic state
state is phenomenally conscious
subject desires continuation of state
counterexample: A man eating a rich meal is in a positive hedonic state, is phenomenally conscious of the taste, and desires the continuation of the experience. The three conditions are met. Yet suppose he is simultaneously racked with guilt for his indulgence and regards the pleasure as wicked. He experiences pleasure, but he does not welcome it; he is, as it were, pleasured against his will. The definition captures the phenomenal and hedonic aspects but fails to account for the evaluative dimension — the fact that a person may experience pleasure without endorsing it.
missing_condition: The definition treats desire for continuation as sufficient evidence of pleasure's value to the subject, but a person may desire the continuation of a state he nonetheless judges to be bad for him.
passage: It is natural to propose that a mental state is a state of pleasure if and only if the subject is in a positive hedonic state, the state is phenomenally conscious, and the subject desires the continuation of the state. This analysis captures much of what we mean by pleasure. Yet consider the following case. A man sits down to a rich and elaborate meal. He is in an unmistakably positive hedonic state: the food tastes exquisite. He is phenomenally conscious of the taste — the flavours and textures are vivid in his experience. And he desires the continuation of the experience — he eats slowly, savouring each mouthful. The three conditions are satisfied. But suppose the man is, at the same time, deeply troubled by his indulgence. He has resolved to eat simply, and he regards his present gluttony as a moral failing. He takes no satisfaction in the pleasure; indeed, the pleasure itself is a source of distress. He is, so to speak, pleasured against his better judgement. The definition correctly identifies his state as one of pleasure, yet it fails to capture the complexity of the situation: that a person may be in a state of pleasure which he does not endorse, does not welcome, and would prefer not to experience. The analysis has treated pleasure as a simple positive state, overlooking the possibility that pleasure admits of evaluative complications that the three conditions do not address.

definition: A mental state S of a subject P is a conscious state if and only if (i) P is aware of being in S, (ii) S possesses a qualitative character accessible to P through introspection, and (iii) S is integrated into P's unified field of experience at the time of its occurrence.
conditions: P is aware of being in S
S possesses a qualitative character accessible to P through introspection
S is integrated into P's unified field of experience at the time of its occurrence
counterexample: Consider a case in which a subject, under conditions of extreme fatigue, processes a loud auditory stimulus such that it demonstrably alters her behaviour — she flinches and redirects her attention — yet she sincerely reports no awareness of any sound and no qualitative impression thereof. The state possesses integration into her behavioural field but fails conditions (i) and (ii).
missing_condition: The state must be reportable by the subject upon suitable prompting
passage: It has long been supposed that the mark of a conscious mental state is simply that its possessor is, in some suitably direct fashion, aware of being in it. To this one customarily adds that such a state must present a qualitative character — a 'what it is like' — open to the subject's introspective gaze, and further that the state must cohere with the broader experiential field rather than standing in isolation from it. Yet one may observe that certain states, particularly those arising under conditions of severe fatigue or distraction, appear to satisfy the third of these requirements whilst failing the first two quite decisively. A subject who flinches at a sudden noise, whose subsequent conduct is plainly redirected by the auditory event, may nonetheless report with evident sincerity that she was aware of no sound whatever and that no qualitative impression accompanied the stimulus. The state, it would seem, was integrated into her behavioural economy and into the causal structure of her experiential field, yet it lacked both the subject's awareness and any accessible qualitative character. One is therefore inclined to suppose that the proposed conditions, taken jointly, do not perfectly capture what is meant by consciousness, for they permit a state to fail on two counts whilst satisfying the third, and they do not require that the subject be capable of reporting or acknowledging the state when suitably prompted. This suggests that some further condition, perhaps concerning the availability of the state for verbal or reflective report, may be wanted if the definition is to prove adequate.

definition: An agent A intends to perform action φ if and only if (i) A has a desire that φ be brought about, (ii) A believes that φ is within A's power to perform, and (iii) A has settled upon φ as the action to be performed in the relevant circumstances.
conditions: A has a desire that φ be brought about
A believes that φ is within A's power to perform
A has settled upon φ as the action to be performed in the relevant circumstances
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man desires to post a letter, believes himself perfectly able to do so, and has settled upon posting it this afternoon. Yet upon arriving at the post-box he discovers he has already posted the letter earlier in the day without any conscious recollection of having done so — his 'settling' was directed at an action already completed, though he did not know this.
missing_condition: The intended action must not have been already completed by A without A's knowledge
passage: It is natural to suppose that intending to act requires, at a minimum, a desire directed at the relevant outcome, a belief in one's capacity to bring it about, and a settled commitment to the course of action in question. These three conditions have seemed to many philosophers jointly sufficient as well as individually necessary. Yet one may construct a situation which ought to give us pause. Imagine a gentleman who, upon waking, forms the desire to post an important letter, believes himself fully able to walk to the post-box, and settles firmly upon doing so after luncheon. He proceeds through his morning with this intention apparently intact. However, upon reaching the post-box in the afternoon, he discovers that the letter has already been posted — by himself, earlier that morning, during a period of distracted routine of which he retains no conscious memory whatever. Each of the three conditions was satisfied at the time he took himself to be intending: the desire was present, the belief in his capacity was well-founded, and the settlement of plan was genuine. Yet it seems distinctly odd to say that he intended to post the letter in the afternoon, for the action upon which his intention was directed had already been accomplished by his own hand. One is thus led to wonder whether the conditions as stated are truly sufficient, or whether some further requirement — perhaps that the intended action remain as yet unperformed by the agent — must be appended to the analysis.

definition: A person P at time t2 is identical with a person Q at time t1 if and only if (i) P possesses continuous memory of Q's experiences, (ii) P's body is spatiotemporally continuous with Q's body, and (iii) P exhibits the same dispositional character traits as Q.
conditions: P possesses continuous memory of Q's experiences
P's body is spatiotemporally continuous with Q's body
P exhibits the same dispositional character traits as Q
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man suffers a severe cerebral trauma that obliterates all memory of his previous life, yet his body remains spatiotemporally continuous and his dispositional character — his temperament, habits of speech, and emotional responses — persists unchanged. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met, but condition (i) fails entirely, and yet we should ordinarily say without hesitation that the man is the same person.
missing_condition: There must be some continuity of the underlying psychological or neurological substrate, not merely of overt dispositional traits
passage: The question of what makes a person at one time identical with a person at another has exercised philosophers considerably, and it is tempting to suppose that the answer may be given in terms of three jointly sufficient conditions: continuity of memory, spatiotemporal continuity of the body, and persistence of dispositional character. Yet reflection upon certain cases suggests that this tripartite analysis may not be altogether satisfactory. Suppose a man suffers a severe cerebral injury — a blow to the head of the most violent kind — which entirely destroys his capacity to recall any event prior to the injury. His memory, in the relevant sense, is wholly discontinuous; he can recollect nothing of his former life, not even his own name. Yet his body is manifestly the same body, exhibiting perfect spatiotemporal continuity, and his dispositional character remains remarkably intact: he is still irascible, still fond of poetry, still inclined to pace when thinking. His friends and family recognise him without difficulty, and no one supposes for a moment that he has become a different person. The three conditions as stated would require us to deny his identity with his former self, since condition (i) is plainly unsatisfied. But this verdict seems clearly mistaken. One suspects that what truly underwrites personal identity in such a case is not the overt persistence of memories or dispositions but rather some deeper continuity of the psychological or neurological substrate — a continuity that the proposed definition fails to capture.

definition: An action φ performed by agent A is a free action if and only if (i) A could have done otherwise than φ in the same circumstances, (ii) φ proceeds from A's own deliberation, and (iii) no external compulsion determined A to perform φ.
conditions: A could have done otherwise than φ in the same circumstances
φ proceeds from A's own deliberation
No external compulsion determined A to perform φ
counterexample: Consider a case in which a woman deliberates at length about whether to donate to a charitable cause and, after weighing the considerations, freely resolves to donate. Unbeknown to her, a neurologist has implanted a device that would have caused her to donate had she decided otherwise. Since she decided on her own, the device never activates. All three conditions appear satisfied — she deliberated, no external force compelled her, and yet she could not, in the relevant sense, have done otherwise, for the device would have intervened.
missing_condition: The mechanism guaranteeing the outcome must be absent, not merely inactive
passage: It is widely held that a free action must satisfy at least three conditions: the agent must have been able to act otherwise, the action must issue from genuine deliberation, and no external compulsion must have determined the agent's conduct. Taken together, these requirements seem to capture what is ordinarily meant when one says that a person acted of her own free will. Yet there are circumstances which appear to satisfy the second and third conditions whilst rendering the first doubtful in a manner that is, upon reflection, deeply perplexing. Imagine a woman who is deliberating whether to make a charitable donation. She considers the matter carefully, weighs the claims of generosity against those of prudence, and resolves to donate. Her deliberation is genuine and thorough; no one coerces her or subjects her to any external pressure. However, unbeknown to her, a neurologist of considerable skill has implanted a device in her brain which monitors her deliberative processes and which would have intervened, causing her to donate, had she resolved not to do so. As it happens, the device never activates, for the woman arrives at the desired conclusion on her own. Now, the action plainly proceeds from her own deliberation, and no external compulsion determined her choice — the device remained dormant throughout. Yet it seems difficult to maintain that she could have done otherwise, for had she begun to decide against donating, the device would have ensured the same outcome. The case thus suggests that the three conditions may not be jointly sufficient, for a latent mechanism of this sort appears to compromise freedom even when it plays no active causal role.

definition: A bodily movement M of agent A constitutes an action if and only if (i) M is caused by A's intention to perform M, (ii) A is aware of performing M at the time of its occurrence, and (iii) M is under A's voluntary control throughout its execution.
conditions: M is caused by A's intention to perform M
A is aware of performing M at the time of its occurrence
M is under A's voluntary control throughout its execution
counterexample: Consider a case in which a pianist, performing a rapid and technically demanding passage, executes a complex sequence of finger movements with exquisite precision. She intended to play the passage and initiated it voluntarily, yet during the most rapid portion she is not individually aware of each finger movement, nor does she exercise fine voluntary control over each motion — the sequence unfolds automatically through acquired motor habit. The movements satisfy condition (i) at the level of the whole passage but fail conditions (ii) and (iii) at the level of the constituent movements, and yet we should wish to call each such movement an action.
missing_condition: The intention and voluntary control may operate at the level of the encompassing activity rather than each constituent movement
passage: The received view holds that a bodily movement qualifies as an action only if it is caused by the agent's intention, accompanied by the agent's awareness, and sustained under the agent's voluntary control. These conditions have a certain prima facie plausibility, for they appear to distinguish genuine actions from mere reflexes, spasms, and involuntary twitches. Yet the analysis encounters a difficulty when one considers skilled performances of a highly practised character. A pianist who plays a rapid passage — a presto run of considerable technical difficulty — initiates the passage intentionally and, at the outset, is both aware of and in control of her movements. But as the passage unfolds at speed, her awareness of the individual finger movements becomes attenuated to the point of absence; she cannot say, upon reflection, that she was conscious of each particular motion, and her control over the fine details of the sequence is exercised not moment by moment but through the initial intention and the acquired motor habit that carries it forward. The individual movements, taken severally, thus fail conditions (ii) and (iii), for the pianist is not aware of each one and does not exercise voluntary control over each one in isolation. Yet it would be most strange to deny that these movements are actions — they are, after all, precisely what the pianist intended to perform, and they constitute the very substance of her musical achievement. This suggests that the conditions as stated are too demanding, and that what is truly required is that intention and control operate at the level of the encompassing activity, with the constituent movements inheriting their status as actions from the larger purposive whole.

definition: A mental state S of a subject P is an emotion if and only if (i) S involves a felt qualitative experience, (ii) S is directed at an intentional object, and (iii) S disposes P towards characteristic patterns of behaviour.
conditions: S involves a felt qualitative experience
S is directed at an intentional object
S disposes P towards characteristic patterns of behaviour
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man experiences a persistent and pervasive feeling of dread — a felt qualitative state of considerable intensity — which disposes him towards avoidance, withdrawal, and agitation, yet which is directed at no object whatever. He cannot say what he dreads; the feeling is entirely objectless. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied, yet condition (ii) fails, and most would nonetheless classify the state as an emotion, namely, anxiety.
missing_condition: The definition must accommodate emotions that are objectless or diffuse in their intentional directedness
passage: It has been common to analyse emotion in terms of three jointly necessary conditions: a felt qualitative experience, directedness towards an intentional object, and a disposition towards certain characteristic patterns of behaviour. Fear, on this account, involves a distinctive felt quality, is directed at some perceived threat, and disposes the subject towards flight or defensive conduct. Anger involves its own characteristic feeling, is directed at an offending party or situation, and disposes towards aggression or remonstrance. The analysis has a pleasing generality and appears to capture what is essential to the emotions as a class. Yet there exist mental states which we should ordinarily wish to classify as emotions but which resist this tripartite characterisation. Consider a man who finds himself gripped by a persistent, pervasive sense of dread. The feeling is intense and unmistakable — a heavy, constricting qualitative state that colours his entire waking experience. It disposes him powerfully towards avoidance and withdrawal; he is reluctant to leave his rooms, startles easily, and finds himself unable to concentrate upon his work. Yet when pressed to identify the object of his dread, he is entirely unable to do so. He does not fear any particular thing or event; the dread simply pervades his experience without attachment to any identifiable intentional object. The state satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) with considerable clarity, but condition (ii) is plainly unsatisfied. Nevertheless, we should scarcely hesitate to call the state an emotion — it is a case of what is commonly termed free-floating anxiety. This suggests that the requirement of intentional directedness, whilst satisfied by many paradigmatic emotions, is not in fact necessary, and that the definition as given is too restrictive in this respect.

definition: A subject P perceives an object O if and only if (i) O exists and is present in P's environment, (ii) P has a sensory experience as of O, and (iii) P's sensory experience is caused, in the appropriate manner, by O.
conditions: O exists and is present in P's environment
P has a sensory experience as of O
P's sensory experience is caused, in the appropriate manner, by O
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man looks at a red apple on a table. The apple is present, he has a sensory experience as of a red apple, and the apple causes this experience through the normal operation of light upon his retinae. However, the apple is in fact painted white, and the redness of his experience is caused by a red light cast upon the apple by a concealed lamp. His experience as of a red apple is caused by the apple, yet not in the appropriate manner with respect to its colour. All three conditions appear superficially satisfied if 'appropriate manner' is left unspecified.
missing_condition: The causal chain between O and P's experience must not involve deceptive intermediary distortions of O's properties
passage: The causal theory of perception, in its simplest formulation, holds that a subject perceives an object when three conditions are jointly met: the object exists in the subject's environment, the subject enjoys a sensory experience as of that object, and the experience is caused in the appropriate manner by the object itself. This analysis has the virtue of excluding hallucinations, in which no object is present, and of requiring a suitable causal connection between object and experience. Yet the notion of causation in the 'appropriate manner' conceals a difficulty that is not easily resolved. Suppose a man gazes at what he takes to be a red apple resting upon a table. The apple is indeed present in his environment, and he has a vivid sensory experience as of a red apple. Moreover, the apple does cause his visual experience — light reflects from its surface and enters his eyes in the ordinary fashion. However, the apple is in truth painted white, and the redness of his experience is produced not by any property of the apple's surface but by a concealed red lamp whose light bathes the apple, imparting to it an appearance of redness that it does not genuinely possess. The causal chain runs from the apple to the subject's retinae, and in that sense the experience is caused by the apple; yet the chain is contaminated by the deceptive intermediary of the coloured lamp. If one says that the conditions are satisfied, one must accept that the man perceives the apple as red, which seems plainly wrong; if one says they are not satisfied, one must specify more precisely what 'appropriate manner' excludes. The case thus reveals that the definition stands in need of a further condition excluding deceptive distortions within the causal chain.

definition: A subject P remembers an event E if and only if (i) P previously experienced E, (ii) P has a present mental representation of E, and (iii) P's present representation is causally connected to P's original experience of E.
conditions: P previously experienced E
P has a present mental representation of E
P's present representation is causally connected to P's original experience of E
counterexample: Consider a case in which a woman genuinely experienced a particular event in childhood — a visit to a certain garden. She has entirely forgotten this event. Years later, a friend describes the visit in great detail, and from this description the woman forms a vivid mental image of the garden visit. Her present representation is causally connected to her original experience, albeit indirectly through the friend's testimony, and yet we should not say she remembers the visit — she has merely reconstructed it from another's account.
missing_condition: The causal connection must run through P's own memory faculty rather than through external testimony or other non-mnemonic channels
passage: The analysis of memory has commonly proceeded by identifying three conditions that are taken to be jointly sufficient for a subject's remembering a past event. The subject must have experienced the event in question, must presently entertain a mental representation of it, and this representation must be causally connected to the original experience. These requirements appear to exclude cases of mere imagination, which lack the causal connection, and cases of confabulation, in which the representation, though present, was never grounded in actual experience. Yet the conditions as stated admit of a case that one would be reluctant to classify as genuine memory. Consider a woman who, in her childhood, visited a particular garden on a particular occasion. The visit was real and she experienced it fully at the time. However, in the intervening years, all trace of the event has been lost from her memory; she cannot recall the visit at all and would deny, if asked, that it ever took place. Some years later, a friend who was present on the same occasion describes the garden visit to her in considerable and accurate detail. From this description, the woman forms a vivid and detailed mental representation of the event — a representation that is, through the intermediary of her friend's account, causally connected to her original experience, for the friend's testimony derives from the same event. All three conditions are thus satisfied: the woman experienced the event, she has a present representation of it, and the representation is causally linked to the original experience. Yet it would be quite wrong to say that she remembers the garden visit. She has not recalled it; she has reconstructed it on the basis of another person's testimony. This suggests that the causal condition must be refined to require that the connection run through the subject's own retentive faculties, rather than through any external informational channel.

definition: A subject P is self-conscious if and only if (i) P is capable of forming beliefs about P's own mental states, (ii) P can distinguish P's own mental states from those of others, and (iii) P possesses a continuous sense of being the same subject over time.
conditions: P is capable of forming beliefs about P's own mental states
P can distinguish P's own mental states from those of others
P possesses a continuous sense of being the same subject over time
counterexample: Consider a case in which a young child, perhaps two years of age, plainly distinguishes her own pain from that of another child who is crying nearby — she does not clutch her own knee when the other child falls — and she possesses a rudimentary temporal continuity of self, recognising her own belongings from the previous day. Yet she is quite incapable of forming beliefs about her own mental states in any reflective sense; she cannot think 'I believe that I am in pain'. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied but condition (i) fails, and yet the child exhibits what we should ordinarily call a form of self-consciousness.
missing_condition: Self-consciousness may require only a practical or pre-reflective self-awareness rather than the capacity for higher-order belief formation
passage: Self-consciousness has frequently been analysed as requiring three conditions: the capacity to form beliefs about one's own mental states, the ability to distinguish one's own states from those of others, and a continuous sense of identity persisting over time. This analysis captures something important about the mature, reflective self-awareness characteristic of adult human beings. Yet it may be doubted whether these conditions correctly delineate the boundaries of self-consciousness, for there appear to be cases in which a subject exhibits what we should naturally call self-consciousness whilst failing to satisfy the first condition. Consider a young child of perhaps two years of age. Such a child plainly distinguishes her own sensations from those of others: when a companion falls and cries, she does not behave as though she herself were injured, and she shows concern directed outward rather than inward. She also exhibits a rudimentary continuity of self, recognising her possessions, anticipating familiar routines, and displaying distress when separated from her customary surroundings. Yet it would be rash to attribute to her the capacity to form beliefs about her own mental states in the reflective sense that the first condition demands. She does not, and perhaps cannot, entertain thoughts of the form 'I believe that I am feeling thus-and-so.' Her self-awareness is practical and immediate rather than reflective and propositional. If we insist upon condition (i) as stated, we must deny that such a child is self-conscious at all, which seems an implausible verdict. It appears, then, that the definition requires amendment to accommodate a more primitive, pre-reflective mode of self-awareness.

definition: A subject P has a future-directed intention to φ if and only if (i) P represents φ as an action to be performed by P at a later time, (ii) P is disposed to take the preparatory steps necessary for φ-ing, and (iii) P would, in the absence of countervailing reasons, proceed to φ at the appropriate time.
conditions: P represents φ as an action to be performed by P at a later time
P is disposed to take the preparatory steps necessary for φ-ing
P would, in the absence of countervailing reasons, proceed to φ at the appropriate time
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man represents himself as going to resign from his post next month, takes every preparatory step — drafting his letter, informing his family, making alternative arrangements — and would, absent countervailing reasons, resign on the appointed day. Yet he has formed this plan under the influence of a passing mood of resentment that he knows from long experience will dissipate within a week, and he does not, at any level, endorse the plan as reflecting his settled judgement. He satisfies all three conditions yet lacks what we should call a genuine intention.
missing_condition: P must endorse the plan to φ as reflecting P's considered judgement rather than a transient motivational state
passage: Future-directed intentions have been thought to require that the agent represent the intended action as something to be performed at a later time, that the agent be disposed to undertake the necessary preparatory measures, and that the agent would carry through with the action absent the emergence of sufficient countervailing reasons. These conditions jointly attempt to distinguish genuine future intentions from idle wishes, daydreams, and conditional plans that lack motivational force. Yet one may wonder whether they succeed in capturing what is essential. Suppose a man, in a fit of resentment following a disagreeable encounter with his employer, forms what presents itself to him as an intention to resign from his post next month. He represents the resignation as an action to be performed at a definite future time. He is disposed to take, and indeed does take, the preparatory steps: he drafts a letter of resignation, informs his wife of his plans, and begins to investigate alternative employment. Were no countervailing reasons to arise, he would proceed to resign on the appointed day. All three conditions are thus fulfilled. However, the man knows from long experience of his own temperament that such moods of resentment pass within a week, and that he will soon regard the whole plan with equanimity and some embarrassment. He does not, at any reflective level, endorse the resignation as expressing his settled judgement about what he ought to do. It is a product of a transient emotional state, and he is, in a sense, aware of this even as he takes the preparatory steps. We should hesitate to say that he genuinely intends to resign, for his plan lacks the endorsement of his considered will. The conditions as stated fail to capture this distinction between plans that express one's settled judgement and plans that arise from transient motivational states.

definition: An agent A acts of A's own free will in performing φ if and only if (i) A's desire to φ is the proximate cause of A's φ-ing, (ii) A's desire to φ was formed through a process of rational deliberation, and (iii) A identifies with the desire to φ upon reflection.
conditions: A's desire to φ is the proximate cause of A's φ-ing
A's desire to φ was formed through a process of rational deliberation
A identifies with the desire to φ upon reflection
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man is subjected, without his knowledge, to subtle subliminal suggestion over a period of weeks, which implants in him a desire to make a large charitable donation. When the desire arises in consciousness, he subjects it to rational scrutiny, finds it consonant with his values, and reflectively identifies with it. He then donates accordingly. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the desire was not genuinely his own — it was implanted by an external manipulator.
missing_condition: The desire must have originated within A's own psychological economy rather than having been implanted by an external agent
passage: One natural analysis of free will holds that an agent acts freely when his action is caused by a desire that was formed through rational deliberation and with which the agent reflectively identifies. This tripartite account has considerable appeal, for it excludes actions driven by compulsion, by unreflective impulse, and by desires that the agent disowns upon reflection. Yet a case may be described which appears to satisfy all three conditions whilst nonetheless failing to constitute a free action. Imagine a man who, over a period of several weeks, is subjected to a programme of subliminal suggestion — messages presented below the threshold of conscious awareness — designed to implant in him a desire to make a substantial charitable donation. The programme succeeds: the desire gradually takes root in his psychological economy. When it surfaces in consciousness, the man does not simply act upon it unreflectively. He subjects it to careful deliberation, considers whether it accords with his values and financial circumstances, and concludes that it does. He identifies with the desire upon reflection; it seems to him genuinely his own. He proceeds to make the donation, and the desire is the proximate cause of his doing so. All three conditions of the proposed analysis are plainly met. Yet there is something deeply troubling about calling this action free, for the desire at its root was not generated by the agent's own psychological processes but was implanted from without by a manipulator whose purposes the agent knows nothing of. The case suggests that the analysis omits a crucial condition concerning the provenance of the desire — that it must originate within the agent's own mental life rather than being introduced by an external source.

definition: An event E constitutes an action of agent A if and only if (i) E is a bodily movement of A, (ii) E is caused by a mental state of A that rationalises E, and (iii) the causal connection between the mental state and E is non-deviant.
conditions: E is a bodily movement of A
E is caused by a mental state of A that rationalises E
The causal connection between the mental state and E is non-deviant
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man intends to signal to his confederate by raising his arm. His intention causes him to become nervous, and his nervousness causes a sudden involuntary jerk of his arm which, as it happens, constitutes exactly the signal he intended to give. The bodily movement occurred, it was caused by a mental state (the intention, via nervousness) that rationalised it, yet the causal chain was plainly deviant — and specifying 'non-deviant' merely relocates the difficulty without resolving it.
missing_condition: The mental state must cause the movement through the agent's executive control capacities rather than through any intermediary psychological disruption
passage: The causal theory of action, in one of its standard formulations, holds that an event constitutes an action when it is a bodily movement caused by a rationalising mental state through a non-deviant causal chain. The requirement of non-deviance is introduced precisely to exclude cases in which the connection between mental state and movement is, as it were, too indirect or fortuitous to underwrite genuine agency. Yet it has proved remarkably difficult to specify what non-deviance amounts to, and certain cases suggest that the notion may be ineliminably problematic. Consider a man who intends to signal to his confederate by raising his arm at a prearranged moment. His intention, however, causes him to become acutely nervous about the importance of the signal, and this nervousness produces an involuntary muscular spasm that jerks his arm upward at precisely the right moment and in precisely the right manner. The confederate receives the signal; the bodily movement has occurred; and it was caused by the very mental state — the intention to signal — that rationalises it. Yet the causal route passed through an intermediary state, nervousness, which produced the movement not as the controlled execution of a plan but as an involuntary physical reaction. The case satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) straightforwardly, and the difficulty lies entirely in condition (iii): one wishes to say that the causal chain is deviant, but the definition provides no criterion for distinguishing deviant from non-deviant chains beyond the bare stipulation. This strongly suggests that an adequate analysis must replace the negative requirement of non-deviance with a positive characterisation of the manner in which mental states must produce movements — perhaps by requiring that the causation proceed through the agent's executive motor capacities.

definition: A subject P experiences grief if and only if (i) P has suffered a significant loss, (ii) P feels a distinctive painful affect in response to the loss, and (iii) P's cognitive and behavioural functioning is disrupted by the loss.
conditions: P has suffered a significant loss
P feels a distinctive painful affect in response to the loss
P's cognitive and behavioural functioning is disrupted by the loss
counterexample: Consider a case in which a woman loses a close friend to illness. She feels the distinctive painful affect associated with grief and her daily functioning is considerably disrupted — she cannot concentrate, withdraws from social life, and weeps frequently. Yet the loss occurred not recently but fifteen years ago, and she had fully recovered in the interim. The grief has returned, unbidden and with full force, upon encountering an old photograph. She has not suffered a new loss; conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied but condition (i) is not satisfied at the time of the experience.
missing_condition: Grief may recur in the absence of any new or recent loss, triggered by memory or association
passage: Grief is commonly analysed as requiring a significant loss, a distinctive painful affective response to that loss, and a consequent disruption of the subject's cognitive and behavioural functioning. This analysis seems well suited to paradigmatic cases: the death of a loved one produces painful feeling and disrupts the bereaved person's ordinary capacities and routines. Yet the analysis may be challenged by cases in which the temporal relationship between loss and grief does not conform to the expected pattern. Consider a woman who lost a dear friend to illness some fifteen years prior. At the time, she grieved deeply — the affect was painful, her functioning was disrupted, and the loss was recent and significant. In the intervening years, she recovered fully; the grief subsided, and she resumed her ordinary life without persistent impairment. Then, one afternoon, she comes upon an old photograph of herself and her friend, taken during a holiday they shared. The grief returns with sudden and overwhelming force. She feels the same distinctive painful affect, and her functioning is once again disrupted — she cannot attend to her work, she withdraws from company, and she is moved to frequent tears. All this persists for some days. Now, conditions (ii) and (iii) are plainly satisfied. But has she suffered a significant loss? The loss occurred fifteen years ago; she has not lost anything anew. The photograph is not itself a loss. If we insist upon condition (i) as requiring a loss contemporaneous with or proximate to the grief, the definition fails to capture this case. Yet it would be quite wrong to deny that the woman is experiencing grief. One is therefore inclined to conclude that the definition requires supplementation to accommodate the recurrence of grief through memory and association, independent of any fresh loss.

definition: A subject P has a veridical perception of property F of object O if and only if (i) O has property F, (ii) P has a sensory experience representing O as having F, and (iii) P's experience of F is produced by the standard perceptual process for detecting F.
conditions: O has property F
P has a sensory experience representing O as having F
P's experience of F is produced by the standard perceptual process for detecting F
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man looks at a white wall in a room illuminated by ordinary daylight. The wall is indeed white, and his visual experience represents it as white. However, unbeknown to him, two abnormal conditions obtain simultaneously: a strong red light is cast upon the wall, which would make it appear red, and the man is wearing tinted lenses that exactly cancel the red cast, restoring the appearance of whiteness. His experience is veridical — the wall is white and he sees it as white — and yet it is not produced by the standard perceptual process, for the normal path has been disrupted and restored by two compensating aberrations.
missing_condition: The process must not involve the fortuitous cancellation of multiple abnormalities
passage: Veridical perception has been thought to require that the perceived object genuinely possess the property in question, that the subject's experience represent the object as having that property, and that the experience be produced by the standard or normal perceptual process. This last condition is meant to exclude cases in which the experience, though accurate, arrives at its accuracy by some aberrant route. Yet the condition proves difficult to apply in cases involving multiple compensating deviations from the standard process. Suppose a man looks at a wall that is, in fact, white. He has a visual experience as of a white wall, and the wall is white — conditions (i) and (ii) are straightforwardly satisfied. Now suppose that, unbeknown to the man, two abnormal factors are simultaneously at work. A concealed lamp casts a strong red light upon the wall, which would ordinarily cause it to appear distinctly red. At the same time, the man is wearing lightly tinted spectacles — put on for another purpose and forgotten — whose tint happens to filter out precisely the wavelengths added by the red lamp. The two aberrations cancel one another exactly, so that his visual experience accurately represents the wall as white. Is this a veridical perception? The wall is white, the experience is as of whiteness, and the experience is, in a sense, produced by a perceptual process that delivers an accurate result. Yet the process is hardly the standard one, for it involves two deviations from normality whose joint effect is, by coincidence, equivalent to normal perception. If we say that condition (iii) is satisfied, we admit that any process yielding a correct result counts as standard, which empties the condition of content. If we say it is not satisfied, we must explain why an accurate experience produced by a reliable — if accidentally reliable — process does not constitute veridical perception.

definition: A person P has a unified self at time t if and only if (i) all of P's mental states at t are accessible to a single centre of consciousness, (ii) P's mental states at t are mutually consistent, and (iii) P experiences P's mental states at t as belonging to a single subject.
conditions: All of P's mental states at t are accessible to a single centre of consciousness
P's mental states at t are mutually consistent
P experiences P's mental states at t as belonging to a single subject
counterexample: Consider a case in which a perfectly ordinary person simultaneously believes that a certain acquaintance is trustworthy (on the basis of long experience) and believes that the same acquaintance is not trustworthy (on the basis of a recent rumour). These beliefs are mutually inconsistent, so condition (ii) fails. Yet the person experiences her mental states as belonging to a single subject and all her states are accessible to one centre of consciousness. We should not wish to deny that she possesses a unified self merely because she holds contradictory beliefs.
missing_condition: Mutual consistency of mental states is too strong; what is required is merely that the states be capable of being recognised by the subject as jointly her own
passage: The unity of the self at a time has been analysed as requiring that all of a person's mental states be accessible to a single centre of consciousness, that these states be mutually consistent, and that the person experience them as belonging to one subject. This analysis is motivated by the intuition that a genuinely unified self must present a coherent picture — that its states must, as it were, fit together into a harmonious whole.
