Yet the requirement of mutual consistency appears too demanding when one considers the perfectly ordinary phenomenon of holding contradictory beliefs. A woman may believe, on the basis of long acquaintance and many acts of reliability, that a certain friend is trustworthy. She may simultaneously believe, on the basis of a disturbing rumour recently conveyed to her, that the same friend is not trustworthy. If she reflects upon the matter, she will recognise the inconsistency and will endeavour to resolve it; but at the moment in question, both beliefs are present in her mental economy. These beliefs are mutually inconsistent, and condition (ii) is therefore unsatisfied. Yet her mental states are all accessible to a single centre of consciousness — she is not in any pathological condition of dissociation — and she experiences all her states, including the conflicting beliefs, as belonging to one and the same subject, namely herself. It would be extremely peculiar to conclude that she lacks a unified self at this moment merely because she harbours a contradiction in her beliefs. Contradictory beliefs are commonplace, and if every instance of inconsistency destroyed the unity of the self, there would be precious few unified selves in existence. The case suggests that mutual consistency is not required for self-unity, and that what is truly needed is merely that the subject be capable of recognising her various states as jointly her own.

definition: A subject P has phenomenal consciousness of stimulus S if and only if (i) S causally affects P's sensory apparatus, (ii) P undergoes a phenomenal experience with a determinate qualitative character as a result, and (iii) the qualitative character of P's experience corresponds to the objective properties of S.
conditions: S causally affects P's sensory apparatus
P undergoes a phenomenal experience with a determinate qualitative character as a result
The qualitative character of P's experience corresponds to the objective properties of S
counterexample: Consider a case in which a subject with a neurological anomaly sees all blue objects as appearing yellow and all yellow objects as appearing blue — a systematic inversion of these two colour qualia. When she looks at a blue vase, the stimulus causally affects her sensory apparatus, she undergoes a phenomenal experience with a determinate qualitative character (yellowness), and yet the character does not correspond to the objective properties of the stimulus. She is nonetheless phenomenally conscious of the vase; condition (iii) fails but phenomenal consciousness is present.
missing_condition: Phenomenal consciousness requires only that the subject undergo some qualitative experience as a result of the stimulus, not that it correspond to the stimulus's objective properties
passage: It has been thought that phenomenal consciousness of a stimulus requires three things: that the stimulus impinge upon the subject's sensory apparatus, that the subject undergo a qualitative experience in consequence, and that the character of this experience correspond to the objective features of the stimulus. The third condition is intended to ensure that the experience is genuinely 'of' the stimulus in a full-blooded sense — that the phenomenal character tracks the world. Yet this condition may be too restrictive. It has been seriously discussed whether there might exist subjects whose qualitative experiences are systematically inverted relative to those of normal perceivers. Suppose such a subject exists — a woman whose neural constitution is such that blue objects produce in her an experience qualitatively identical to that which yellow objects produce in the rest of us, and conversely. When she looks at a blue vase, the stimulus causally affects her eyes and visual cortex in the normal manner; she undergoes a phenomenal experience of determinate qualitative character — an experience she would describe, using the word as she has learnt to use it, as 'blue', though its intrinsic qualitative character is what others would call 'yellow'. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied without difficulty. But condition (iii) is not, for the qualitative character of her experience does not correspond to the objective properties of the vase — the vase is blue, but her phenomenal experience has the character of yellowness. Yet it would be absurd to deny that she is phenomenally conscious of the vase. She sees it; she is aware of it; there is something it is like for her to look at it. The case demonstrates that phenomenal consciousness does not require correspondence between qualitative character and objective properties, and that the definition therefore includes a condition that is not in fact necessary.

definition: An omission O by agent A is an action if and only if (i) A was aware of the opportunity to act, (ii) A decided not to act, and (iii) A's decision not to act was based on A's reasons.
conditions: A was aware of the opportunity to act
A decided not to act
A's decision not to act was based on A's reasons
counterexample: Consider a case in which a lifeguard sees a swimmer in distress and is aware of the opportunity to intervene. She forms no decision whatever — she is paralysed by sudden fear and simply fails to act. Her omission is not based on her reasons but on her involuntary psychological incapacitation. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are unsatisfied, yet we should ordinarily hold her responsible for the omission as though it were an action of a sort.
missing_condition: An omission may constitute an action-like event for which the agent is responsible even in the absence of a deliberate decision, provided the agent had a duty and the capacity to act
passage: It is sometimes maintained that omissions qualify as actions only when the agent was aware of the opportunity to act, made a deliberate decision not to act, and based that decision upon reasons. This analysis attempts to distinguish culpable omissions — the sort for which we hold agents responsible — from mere non-occurrences that have no agential significance. If a man does not save a drowning stranger in a distant country of whose peril he is entirely ignorant, his failure to act is no omission in the relevant sense. The analysis thus has the virtue of requiring awareness, decision, and rationality. Yet consider the following case. A lifeguard is stationed at her post when she observes a swimmer in evident distress. She is fully aware of the opportunity — indeed the duty — to intervene. Yet at the crucial moment, she is gripped by a sudden and overwhelming fear that renders her unable to move. She forms no decision whatever; the fear simply prevents deliberation from reaching any conclusion. She stands frozen, and the swimmer is rescued by another party. Her omission — her failure to enter the water — was not the product of a decision, and it was not based upon her reasons. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are unsatisfied. Yet we should ordinarily say that her omission was, in a morally and legally significant sense, an action-like event for which she bears responsibility. She was the person whose role it was to act; she had the capacity (in the dispositional sense, absent the fear) and the awareness. The case suggests that the analysis is too narrow, for it excludes omissions that arise from psychological incapacitation rather than from deliberate decision, yet which nonetheless carry the full weight of agential responsibility.

definition: A subject P is in a state of jealousy if and only if (i) P believes that a valued relationship with another person Q is threatened by a rival R, (ii) P experiences a negative affective response directed at R, and (iii) P desires to preserve P's relationship with Q.
conditions: P believes that a valued relationship with Q is threatened by a rival R
P experiences a negative affective response directed at R
P desires to preserve P's relationship with Q
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man believes his friendship with a colleague is threatened by a new arrival at the firm. He experiences a pronounced negative affect — resentment, hostility — yet this affect is directed not at the rival but at his colleague, whom he blames for being too easily charmed. He desires to preserve the friendship. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied, but condition (ii) fails because the negative affect is misdirected, and yet the state is recognisably jealousy.
missing_condition: The negative affect need not be directed specifically at the rival but may be directed at any party to the triangular relationship
passage: Jealousy has been analysed as requiring three conditions: a belief that a valued relationship is threatened by a rival, a negative affective response directed at the rival, and a desire to preserve the threatened relationship. This tripartite account captures the essential triangular structure of jealousy — the subject, the valued other, and the rival — and it plausibly distinguishes jealousy from mere envy, which need not involve a threatened relationship. Yet the account may be questioned on the ground that it too narrowly specifies the direction of the negative affect. Consider a man who enjoys a close friendship with a colleague of long standing. A new person arrives at the firm and begins to form an increasingly intimate friendship with the colleague. The man believes, rightly or wrongly, that his valued relationship is under threat; and he desires strongly to preserve it. So far, conditions (i) and (iii) are clearly met. He does experience a pronounced negative affective response — but it is directed not at the newcomer but at his colleague. He feels resentment towards his friend for being, as he sees it, too easily charmed, too ready to transfer affections, too careless of the established bond. The newcomer he regards with relative equanimity; it is his colleague whose conduct provokes his ire. Condition (ii), which requires the negative affect to be directed at the rival, is thus unsatisfied. Yet the state the man is in is recognisably jealousy — it has the characteristic triangular structure, the characteristic possessiveness, and the characteristic pain of threatened attachment. The direction of the negative affect is merely displaced, not absent. This suggests that the definition errs in specifying the rival as the necessary target of the affective response.

definition: A subject P attends to an object O in P's perceptual field if and only if (i) O is within the scope of P's current sensory experience, (ii) P selectively processes information about O over other objects in the field, and (iii) P is conscious of O as a distinct particular.
conditions: O is within the scope of P's current sensory experience
P selectively processes information about O over other objects in the field
P is conscious of O as a distinct particular
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man is driving along a familiar route whilst absorbed in conversation. He navigates a series of turns, avoids pedestrians, and stops at traffic signals — all of which require that he selectively process visual information about particular objects and respond to them as distinct particulars. Yet he later reports that he was not conscious of any of these objects; his attention was wholly given to the conversation. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied by his successful navigation, but condition (iii) is not, and yet he plainly attended to the relevant objects in some operative sense.
missing_condition: Attention may operate at a sub-personal or non-conscious level, and need not involve the subject's conscious awareness of the attended object
passage: Perceptual attention has been defined as requiring that an object fall within the subject's sensory experience, that the subject selectively process information about it in preference to competing objects, and that the subject be conscious of the object as a distinct particular. This analysis reflects the common-sense view that attending to something is a matter of consciously singling it out from the background of experience. Yet there are familiar cases that strain this account considerably. Consider a man who is driving along a well-known route whilst deeply absorbed in an animated conversation with his passenger. He negotiates several turns, slows for a pedestrian crossing, and halts at a red signal — all without any interruption to the flow of his discourse. When he arrives at his destination, he reports that he has no recollection of any of these events; his conscious attention was entirely occupied by the conversation, and the details of the drive are a blank. Yet his behaviour demonstrates beyond doubt that he selectively processed information about particular objects — the pedestrian, the signal, the turnings — and responded to them appropriately. The objects were within his sensory experience, and he processed information about them in preference to irrelevant features of the visual scene. Conditions (i) and (ii) are therefore satisfied. But condition (iii) is not: he was not, by his own sincere report, conscious of these objects as distinct particulars. If we insist upon condition (iii), we must say that the man did not attend to the traffic signal at which he stopped, which seems plainly false in any operative sense of 'attend'. The case thus suggests that the definition is too restrictive, and that attention may function at a level beneath conscious awareness.

definition: An agent A is morally responsible for action φ if and only if (i) A performed φ intentionally, (ii) A understood the moral significance of φ at the time of acting, and (iii) A was not subject to any compulsion or duress in performing φ.
conditions: A performed φ intentionally
A understood the moral significance of φ at the time of acting
A was not subject to any compulsion or duress in performing φ
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man intentionally and without any compulsion gives false testimony in a legal proceeding. He understands perfectly well that giving false testimony is morally wrong. However, his understanding of the moral significance is based entirely on the fact that it violates a social convention he happens to respect; he has no genuine moral comprehension of why it is wrong — he does not grasp that it undermines justice or harms the accused. All three conditions are superficially satisfied, yet his 'understanding' of moral significance is defective in a way that arguably diminishes his responsibility.
missing_condition: A must possess genuine moral comprehension of why φ is significant, not merely awareness that φ is conventionally regarded as wrong
passage: Moral responsibility has been thought to require that the agent acted intentionally, understood the moral significance of the action, and was free from compulsion or duress. These conditions are meant to ensure that the agent was a fully competent moral actor at the time of the deed — that the action was chosen, that its import was grasped, and that no external force overrode the agent's will. Yet the notion of 'understanding the moral significance' may conceal an important ambiguity. Consider a man who gives false testimony in a legal proceeding. He does so intentionally and without any compulsion; no one has threatened or coerced him. He understands, moreover, that giving false testimony is morally wrong — or so it appears. When questioned about the basis of this understanding, however, it emerges that he regards the wrongness of false testimony as consisting entirely in the fact that it violates a social convention which he has been brought up to respect. He has no appreciation whatever of the deeper moral considerations at stake — he does not understand that false testimony may send an innocent person to prison, that it corrupts the administration of justice, or that it betrays the trust reposed in witnesses by the community. His 'understanding' of moral significance is, in a word, superficial: it extends to the recognition that the act is conventionally disapproved but not to any genuine comprehension of why it is wrong. All three conditions of the proposed analysis are met if we take 'understanding' in a thin sense. Yet one may doubt whether the man is fully morally responsible for his action in the way that a person with genuine moral comprehension would be. The case suggests that the definition requires a richer account of what it means to understand moral significance.

definition: A subject P at time t2 is the same person as subject Q at time t1 if and only if (i) P's psychological states at t2 are causally continuous with Q's psychological states at t1, (ii) P can, at least in principle, recall experiences had by Q, and (iii) P and Q share a common narrative self-understanding.
conditions: P's psychological states at t2 are causally continuous with Q's psychological states at t1
P can, at least in principle, recall experiences had by Q
P and Q share a common narrative self-understanding
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man undergoes a profound religious conversion that entirely transforms his narrative self-understanding. Before the conversion, he understood himself as a worldly, ambitious man of affairs; afterward, he understands himself as a humble servant of the divine, and he explicitly repudiates his former self-narrative. His psychological states are causally continuous with his prior states, and he can recall his pre-conversion experiences. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but condition (iii) fails, and yet no one would deny that he is the same person.
missing_condition: Personal identity does not require continuity of narrative self-understanding, which may change radically whilst identity persists
passage: One influential approach to personal identity over time holds that it requires causal continuity of psychological states, the capacity to recall earlier experiences, and a shared narrative self-understanding between the person at the later time and the person at the earlier time. The third condition reflects the attractive idea that being the same person involves seeing oneself as the same person — that personal identity is, in part, a matter of the story one tells about one's own life. Yet this condition may be too strong. Consider a man who undergoes a profound religious conversion in middle age. Before the conversion, he understood himself as a worldly, ambitious man — a man of affairs, devoted to commercial success and social advancement. His self-narrative was organised around these themes. After the conversion, his self-understanding is wholly transformed. He now regards himself as a humble servant of the divine, and he explicitly and emphatically repudiates his former self-narrative. He does not merely supplement it; he rejects it. He says that the man he was before the conversion was living in error, that his former ambitions were vanity, and that his true life began only with his awakening. His psychological states are causally continuous with those of his earlier self — the conversion did not involve any rupture of causal continuity — and he can recall his pre-conversion experiences vividly and accurately. But his narrative self-understanding is entirely different, indeed opposed, to that of his former self. Condition (iii) is therefore unsatisfied. Yet it would be extraordinary to deny that the man after the conversion is the same person as the man before it. He is, manifestly, the very same individual who has undergone a transformation. The case suggests that narrative self-understanding is not necessary for personal identity and that the definition is too restrictive in this regard.

definition: A subject P has a procedural memory for skill S if and only if (i) P previously acquired S through practice, (ii) P can currently perform S with a degree of proficiency exceeding that of a novice, and (iii) P's current performance of S is causally sustained by the neural traces laid down during the original acquisition.
conditions: P previously acquired S through practice
P can currently perform S with a degree of proficiency exceeding that of a novice
P's current performance of S is causally sustained by the neural traces laid down during the original acquisition
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man learnt to ride a bicycle as a child, acquiring the skill through extensive practice. The neural traces laid down during this original learning have, through some unusual degenerative process, been entirely destroyed. However, he subsequently and independently reacquired the ability to ride through a different mechanism — a neurological compensation that recruited entirely different neural pathways. He can ride with full proficiency, and he did originally acquire the skill through practice, but condition (iii) is not satisfied, since his current performance is not sustained by the original traces. Yet we should be inclined to say he has a procedural memory for cycling.
missing_condition: Procedural memory may persist through neural reorganisation and compensation, and need not depend upon the specific traces laid down during original acquisition
passage: Procedural memory — memory for skills, as opposed to memory for facts or events — has been analysed as requiring that the subject previously acquired the skill through practice, that the subject can currently perform it with proficiency, and that the current performance is causally sustained by the neural traces established during the original learning. These conditions seem to capture what is distinctive about procedural memory: it is acquired through practice, retained over time, and grounded in lasting changes to the nervous system. Yet the third condition may be challenged by cases of neural reorganisation. Suppose a man learnt to ride a bicycle as a boy, through the usual extended process of trial and error. The skill was acquired in the ordinary way, and the relevant neural traces were duly established. Many years later, he suffers a localised neurological insult — perhaps a small stroke — that destroys precisely those neural structures in which the original skill-traces were stored. For a period, he is unable to ride at all. However, over the course of several months, his brain compensates for the damage by recruiting entirely new neural pathways to support the same motor skill. He regains the ability to ride with full proficiency, indistinguishable in quality from his earlier performance. Now, the man originally acquired the skill through practice, and he can currently perform it with more than novice proficiency. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But condition (iii) is not, for his current performance is causally sustained by neural structures that were not laid down during the original acquisition — the original traces have been destroyed. Yet it seems natural to say that he remembers how to ride a bicycle. The skill has been retained, even though the specific neural substrate has changed. This case suggests that procedural memory should be defined in terms of functional continuity of the skill rather than causal dependence upon particular neural traces.

definition: A group of agents G has a collective intention to φ if and only if (i) each member of G individually intends to do their part in φ-ing, (ii) each member of G believes that the other members likewise intend to do their parts, and (iii) these individual intentions and beliefs are common knowledge among the members of G.
conditions: Each member of G individually intends to do their part in φ-ing
Each member of G believes that the other members likewise intend to do their parts
These individual intentions and beliefs are common knowledge among the members of G
counterexample: Consider a case in which three musicians agree to perform a trio. Each intends to play her part, each believes the others so intend, and all of this is common knowledge. Yet each has privately resolved that, should the performance go badly, she will abandon the ensemble without warning. The common knowledge condition is satisfied only with respect to the intention to play, not with respect to the conditional intention to defect. All three conditions are met, yet the collective intention seems defective — it lacks the unconditional commitment characteristic of genuine joint action.
missing_condition: Each member must be committed to performing their part even in the face of difficulties, not merely under favourable conditions
passage: Collective intention has been analysed as obtaining when each member of a group individually intends to do her part, believes that the others share this intention, and when these intentions and beliefs are common knowledge. This analysis attempts to explain how a plurality of agents can share a single purpose — how a group can intend something over and above what each individual intends separately. The conditions of individual intention, mutual belief, and common knowledge seem well chosen to capture the cooperative structure of joint action. Yet one may describe a case which satisfies these conditions whilst falling short of what we should wish to call a genuine collective intention. Suppose three musicians agree to perform a trio at a forthcoming concert. Each intends to play her part — the violinist to play the violin part, the cellist the cello part, the pianist the piano part. Each believes that the others share this intention, and all of this is openly acknowledged and thus common knowledge among them. The three conditions are satisfied. However, each musician has privately resolved — and has not disclosed to the others — that should the performance begin to go badly, she will simply stop playing and leave the stage without warning. Each is committed to the performance only conditionally: she will participate so long as things go well, but will abandon the collective enterprise the moment it encounters difficulty. Now, the individual intentions to play are genuine, the mutual beliefs are accurate, and common knowledge obtains — but only with respect to the intention to perform under favourable conditions. The deeper conditional intention to defect is not common knowledge and indeed contradicts the spirit of collective commitment. Yet all three conditions of the definition are formally met. This suggests that genuine collective intention requires something more — an unconditional commitment to one's part in the joint enterprise that persists through difficulty.

definition: A state S is a pain state of subject P if and only if (i) S involves tissue damage or a stimulus that would cause tissue damage in normal circumstances, (ii) S produces a characteristic aversive phenomenal quality in P, and (iii) S disposes P to engage in protective behaviour directed at the affected bodily region.
conditions: S involves tissue damage or a stimulus that would cause tissue damage in normal circumstances
S produces a characteristic aversive phenomenal quality in P
S disposes P to engage in protective behaviour directed at the affected bodily region
counterexample: Consider a case in which a subject experiences a vivid, intensely aversive phenomenal quality localised in her left hand, and she is strongly disposed to protect her hand — she cradles it, avoids using it, and winces when it is touched. Yet thorough medical examination reveals no tissue damage whatever and no stimulus that would produce tissue damage. The pain is entirely 'functional' or psychogenic in origin. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are fully met, but condition (i) is not, and yet the subject is undeniably in pain.
missing_condition: Pain may occur in the complete absence of tissue damage or noxious stimulation, and the definition must accommodate purely psychogenic pain
passage: Pain has been defined as requiring the conjunction of tissue damage or noxious stimulation, a characteristic aversive phenomenal quality, and a disposition towards protective behaviour. This analysis has the merit of connecting pain to its biological function — the detection and avoidance of bodily harm — and of acknowledging both its phenomenal and its behavioural dimensions. Yet it encounters a difficulty with cases of pain that occur in the absence of any physical damage or noxious stimulus. Consider a woman who reports an intense, sharply localised pain in her left hand. The phenomenal quality is unmistakably aversive — she describes it as burning and throbbing, and her demeanour leaves no doubt of her distress. She is powerfully disposed to protect the hand: she cradles it against her body, avoids any use of it, and winces markedly when it is inadvertently touched. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied in the clearest possible fashion. Yet a thorough medical examination — including imaging, nerve conduction studies, and careful physical inspection — reveals absolutely no tissue damage, no inflammation, no noxious stimulus of any kind acting upon the hand. The pain is entirely psychogenic in origin, produced, perhaps, by some psychological process whose nature remains obscure. If we insist upon condition (i), we must deny that the woman is in pain, which contradicts both her sincere testimony and the manifest behavioural evidence. The aversive quality is real, the protective behaviour is genuine, and the suffering is not feigned. To say that she is not in pain because there is no tissue damage seems to confuse the typical cause of pain with its essential nature. The case thus suggests that the definition requires revision to accommodate pain states that arise without any physical lesion or noxious stimulation.

definition: An agent A performs a mental action in φ-ing if and only if (i) φ-ing is a mental event occurring in A, (ii) A initiates φ-ing by an act of will, and (iii) A can, at least in principle, refrain from φ-ing.
conditions: φ-ing is a mental event occurring in A
A initiates φ-ing by an act of will
A can, at least in principle, refrain from φ-ing
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man deliberately undertakes a mental calculation — he wills himself to compute the sum of two large numbers. In the course of this calculation, various intermediate mental events occur: partial sums arise, digits are carried, and so forth. These intermediate events are mental events occurring in him, and he could in principle refrain from computing (he could abandon the task). Yet he does not initiate each intermediate event by a separate act of will; they arise automatically as consequences of the initial volitional act. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied for the intermediate events, but condition (ii) is not, and yet these events seem to be parts of his mental action.
missing_condition: A mental event may constitute part of a mental action if it arises as an automatic consequence of a voluntarily initiated mental process, without requiring a separate act of will for each constituent event
passage: It has been proposed that mental actions — acts of reasoning, calculating, deliberate imagining, and the like — require that a mental event occur in the agent, that the agent initiate it by an act of will, and that the agent be able, at least in principle, to refrain from it. These conditions are intended to distinguish genuine mental actions from mere mental happenings — from unbidden thoughts, involuntary associations, and the spontaneous play of imagery that occurs without the agent's direction. The analysis has considerable plausibility when applied to the initiation of a mental action: when a man sets himself to calculate a sum, he does so by an act of will, and he could have refrained. Yet the analysis encounters a difficulty when one considers the internal structure of extended mental actions. Suppose a man deliberately undertakes to compute the product of thirty-seven and forty-three in his head. He initiates this task volitionally, and he could in principle have refrained from attempting it. As the calculation proceeds, various intermediate mental events occur: he multiplies seven by three, carries the two, multiplies seven by four, and so on. These intermediate events are mental events occurring in him, and they are plainly parts of his mental action of calculating. Yet he does not initiate each of them by a separate act of will. They arise, as it were, automatically — each following from the preceding step in accordance with the rules of arithmetic that he has internalised. He wills the overall task but not each constituent step. Condition (ii) is therefore unsatisfied for the intermediate events, even though they are integral parts of the mental action. This suggests that the analysis is too demanding in requiring a separate act of will for each mental event that constitutes part of an action, and that some mental events may qualify as components of an action by virtue of arising automatically within a voluntarily initiated process.

definition: A state S of an organism O is a conscious state if and only if (i) O is able to produce a verbal report describing S, (ii) O can direct voluntary attention towards S, and (iii) O can recall S after the fact and incorporate it into an autobiographical narrative.
conditions: O is able to produce a verbal report describing S
O can direct voluntary attention towards S
O can recall S after the fact and incorporate it into an autobiographical narrative
counterexample: Consider the case of a young infant who, upon touching a hot surface, withdraws his hand and cries out in evident distress. The infant is conscious of the pain — the distress is real and the experience is genuine — yet he cannot produce a verbal report, for he has not yet acquired language. He cannot direct voluntary attention towards the pain in any deliberate sense; the pain simply overwhelms him. And he cannot, at this stage of development, recall the experience and incorporate it into an autobiographical narrative, for he possesses no such narrative. Yet the pain is a conscious state.
missing_condition: The definition requires verbal reportability, voluntary attention, and autobiographical recall, yet conscious experience may occur in beings who lack language, who cannot direct attention voluntarily, and who have no capacity for autobiographical memory. What is wanted is an account that does not make the higher cognitive capacities a prerequisite of consciousness.
passage: One might propose that a state of an organism is a conscious state if and only if the organism is able to produce a verbal report describing it, can direct voluntary attention towards it, and can recall it after the fact and incorporate it into an autobiographical narrative. The definition has a certain appeal, for it identifies consciousness with the features that make it most accessible to investigation. The adult who reports a toothache, who can attend to the quality and location of the pain, and who can later recount the experience, is paradigmatically conscious in respect of that pain. One might suppose that verbal reportability, voluntary attention, and autobiographical memory are constitutive of conscious experience. Yet one must attend to the infant who touches a hot surface. A child of six months, crawling upon the hearth, places his hand upon the fender and is burnt. He screams, withdraws his hand, and exhibits every sign of acute distress — tears, facial contortion, protective cradling of the injured hand. The pain is real; no one who observes the infant's reaction can doubt that he is in a state of conscious suffering. But he cannot produce a verbal report of his experience, for he has no language. He cannot direct voluntary attention towards the pain in any deliberate or reflective sense; the pain commands his awareness without his choosing to attend to it. And he cannot recall the episode and weave it into an autobiographical narrative, for he possesses no autobiographical capacity at this stage of development. The three conditions fail entirely, yet the conscious character of the infant's experience is as evident as anything can be. The definition, in requiring verbal report, voluntary attention, and autobiographical recall, confuses the conditions under which consciousness is most easily detected and described with the conditions under which it exists, and thereby excludes the conscious experience of beings who are manifestly sentient but who lack the higher cognitive capacities that the definition demands.

definition: A person P acts with the intention to bring about a result R if and only if (i) P has formed a prior explicit plan to bring about R, (ii) P can articulate the plan in propositional form before acting, and (iii) the plan specifies in detail the means by which R is to be achieved.
conditions: P has formed a prior explicit plan to bring about R
P can articulate the plan in propositional form before acting
the plan specifies in detail the means by which R is to be achieved
counterexample: Consider the case of a skilled batsman who, seeing a ball bowled short of a length, plays a pull shot to the boundary. He intends to hit the ball to the boundary — his action is purposive and directed — but he has formed no prior explicit plan; the shot is a spontaneous response to the delivery. He cannot articulate a plan in propositional form before acting; the interval between the bowler's release and the stroke is far too brief for any propositional thought. And the means are not specified in detail; his body adjusts its position, grip, and swing in a manner that is skilled but unreflective. Yet his action is intentional.
missing_condition: The definition requires a prior, articulable, detailed plan, yet intentional action may be spontaneous, unreflective, and guided by embodied skill rather than by any propositionally structured plan. What is wanted is an account that accommodates the intentionality of skilled, improvisatory action.
passage: One might propose that a person acts with the intention to bring about a result if and only if he has formed a prior explicit plan, can articulate the plan in propositional form before acting, and the plan specifies in detail the means to be employed. The definition has some plausibility when one considers deliberate, premeditated action. The architect who designs a building forms an explicit plan, can articulate it in drawings and specifications, and specifies in detail the materials, dimensions, and methods of construction. His action is intentional in the fullest sense, and the three conditions are plainly satisfied. Yet one must attend to the skilled batsman. A cricketer stands at the crease, facing a fast bowler. The bowler delivers a ball that rises steeply from just short of a length. In a fraction of a second, the batsman shifts his weight, adjusts his grip, swings the bat in a controlled arc, and pulls the ball to the mid-wicket boundary. His action is intentional — he means to hit the ball to the boundary, and he succeeds. But he has formed no prior explicit plan; the shot is a spontaneous response to the particular delivery, shaped by years of practice but not by any act of deliberation preceding this particular stroke. He cannot articulate a plan in propositional form before acting; the interval between the ball's release and the completion of the stroke is measured in fractions of a second, far too brief for any propositional thought to occur. And the means are not specified in detail; the precise adjustments of posture, grip, timing, and swing are governed by embodied skill — by what the body has learnt to do — rather than by any detailed specification that the batsman could set down in words. The definition, in requiring prior explicit planning, propositional articulacy, and detailed specification of means, mistakes the features of deliberate, reflective action for features that are necessary to intentional action as such, and thereby excludes the vast domain of skilled, spontaneous, purposive conduct.

definition: A person P at time t1 is the same person as a person Q at a later time t2 if and only if (i) P and Q share the same continuous physical body, (ii) the body of P at t1 and the body of Q at t2 are connected by an unbroken chain of spatial continuity, and (iii) the body undergoes no abrupt or radical alteration between t1 and t2.
conditions: P and Q share the same continuous physical body
the body of P at t1 and the body of Q at t2 are connected by an unbroken chain of spatial continuity
the body undergoes no abrupt or radical alteration between t1 and t2
counterexample: Consider the case of a man who, as a result of a severe illness, loses both legs, much of his hair, and several stone in weight over the course of a few months. His body has undergone an abrupt and radical alteration. A person who had seen him before his illness might scarcely recognise the emaciated, legless figure in the hospital bed. Yet no one would deny that he is the same person. His character, his memories, his relationships, his legal obligations — all persist through the bodily alteration.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the body undergo no radical alteration, yet personal identity persists through even the most dramatic physical changes — amputation, disfigurement, wasting illness — provided that continuity of mental life and character is maintained. What is wanted is an account that does not make the stability of the body's form a condition of personal identity.
passage: One might propose that a person at one time is the same person at a later time if and only if they share the same continuous physical body, the body is connected by an unbroken chain of spatial continuity, and the body undergoes no abrupt or radical alteration between the two times. The definition has initial plausibility. The man one meets on Tuesday is the same man one met on Monday if he possesses the same body, if that body has been continuously present in space between the two meetings, and if it has not undergone any startling transformation. One might suppose that these conditions capture the essence of personal identity over time. Yet one must attend to the case of the man who falls gravely ill. A robust, vigorous man in his prime is struck down by a wasting disease. Over the course of several months, he loses both legs to gangrene, his hair falls out, and he loses several stone in weight. The transformation of his body is dramatic; a friend who last saw him in health would scarcely recognise the gaunt, altered figure propped in the hospital bed. The body has undergone an abrupt and radical alteration — the very thing that the third condition forbids. Yet no one who knows him has the slightest doubt that he is the same man. His wife addresses him by name, his children call him father, his solicitor continues to act upon his instructions, and his creditors continue to present their bills. His memories, his character, his temperament, his habits of thought — all persist unchanged through the ravages of the illness. The definition, in requiring that the body undergo no radical alteration, mistakes the stability of the body's outward form for a condition of personal identity, when in truth identity persists through even the most dramatic physical changes, sustained by continuities of mental life that the definition fails to mention.

definition: A person P acts freely with respect to an action A if and only if (i) P deliberated among at least two distinct alternatives before performing A, (ii) P's deliberation was conducted in a calm and unperturbed state of mind, and (iii) P could have performed an alternative action had he chosen to do so.
conditions: P deliberated among at least two distinct alternatives before performing A
P's deliberation was conducted in a calm and unperturbed state of mind
P could have performed an alternative action had he chosen to do so
counterexample: Consider the case of a soldier who, in the heat of battle, throws himself upon a grenade to shield his comrades. He does not deliberate among alternatives; there is no time for deliberation. His state of mind is anything but calm — he acts in a moment of extreme agitation and terror. Yet one is inclined to say that his action is free in the deepest sense. It expresses his character, his courage, and his devotion to his comrades. It is not coerced, not compelled by external force, not the product of reflex or automatism. It is, if anything, an exemplary case of free human action.
missing_condition: The definition requires calm, prior deliberation among alternatives, yet some of the most profoundly free actions — acts of spontaneous courage, of moral resolve under extreme pressure — are performed without deliberation and in states of the greatest perturbation. What is wanted is an account that recognises freedom in action that springs from settled character rather than from a calm deliberative process.
passage: One might propose that a person acts freely with respect to an action if and only if he deliberated among at least two distinct alternatives before performing it, his deliberation was conducted in a calm and unperturbed state of mind, and he could have performed an alternative action had he chosen differently. The definition has a certain plausibility when one considers the leisured decision. A man sitting in his study considers whether to accept a new appointment: he weighs the salary against the inconvenience, the prestige against the loss of leisure, and after careful reflection he decides to accept. He is calm, he has alternatives, and his deliberation is thorough. One might suppose that this is what it is to act freely. Yet one must attend to the soldier and the grenade. In the chaos of battle, a grenade lands among a group of soldiers huddled in a trench. One of their number, without a moment's hesitation, throws himself upon the device, absorbing the blast with his own body and saving the lives of his companions. He did not deliberate among alternatives; the interval between the grenade's arrival and his action was too brief for deliberation. His state of mind was not calm — he was in the grip of terror, adrenaline, and the desperate urgency of the moment. Yet it would be perverse to say that he acted unfreely. His action was not coerced; no one compelled him to sacrifice himself. It was not a reflex or an automatism; it expressed something deep in his character — his courage, his loyalty, his willingness to give his life for his comrades. If any action deserves to be called free, it is this one. The definition, in requiring calm deliberation among alternatives, mistakes the features of one familiar form of free action — the considered, unhurried decision — for features that are necessary to freedom as such, and thereby excludes the spontaneous, unreflective, but profoundly voluntary acts that are among the highest expressions of human freedom.

definition: A bodily movement M performed by a person P constitutes an action if and only if (i) M is preceded by a conscious mental event — a volition or act of will — that initiates it, (ii) P is aware of performing M while it is underway, and (iii) M is under P's continuous voluntary control throughout its execution.
conditions: M is preceded by a conscious mental event — a volition or act of will — that initiates it
P is aware of performing M while it is underway
M is under P's continuous voluntary control throughout its execution
counterexample: Consider the case of an experienced pianist performing a rapid and technically demanding passage. Her fingers move with extraordinary speed and precision, executing complex sequences of notes that she could not consciously initiate one by one. She is not aware of each individual finger movement as it occurs; her attention is directed at the musical phrase as a whole, not at the mechanical details of its execution. And the individual movements are not under her continuous voluntary control in any fine-grained sense; she could not, mid-passage, deliberately alter the trajectory of a single finger without disrupting the whole. Yet her playing is an action — indeed, it is a paradigm of skilled human action.
missing_condition: The definition requires a conscious initiating volition, concurrent awareness, and continuous voluntary control, yet skilled action may be initiated without any identifiable act of will, may proceed without fine-grained awareness, and may be governed by trained automaticity rather than by moment-to-moment voluntary control. What is wanted is an account of action that accommodates the role of acquired skill and habitual competence.
passage: One might propose that a bodily movement constitutes an action if and only if it is preceded by a conscious mental event — a volition or act of will — that initiates it, the agent is aware of performing the movement while it is underway, and the movement is under the agent's continuous voluntary control throughout. The definition has a certain appeal. One thinks of the man who raises his arm to signal a cab: he forms the intention to hail the cab, he is aware of his arm rising, and the movement is under his control from beginning to end. The three conditions appear to be satisfied, and the movement is plainly an action. Yet one must attend to the concert pianist. A skilled pianist performing a rapid passage — a Chopin étude, let us say, or a Bach toccata — executes hundreds of precisely coordinated finger movements in the space of a few seconds. These movements are not each preceded by a distinct conscious volition; there is no time for such a thing, and the pianist does not experience a separate act of will for each keystroke. She is not aware of each individual finger movement as it occurs; her awareness is directed at the musical phrase, the harmonic progression, the expressive shape of the line, not at the mechanical details of which finger strikes which key. And the individual movements are not under her continuous voluntary control in any fine-grained sense; she could not deliberately alter the trajectory of a single finger in mid-passage without disrupting the entire performance. Yet her playing is an action in the fullest sense — it is purposive, skilled, expressive, and attributable to her as agent. The definition, in requiring a conscious initiating volition, concurrent awareness, and continuous control, confuses the features of simple, deliberate, unitary actions with the features of action as such, and thereby excludes the rich domain of skilled performance in which agency operates through trained competence rather than through moment-to-moment conscious direction.

definition: A mental state M is an emotion if and only if (i) M involves a characteristic bodily sensation — a felt perturbation of the viscera, the musculature, or the skin, (ii) M has an identifiable object towards which it is directed, and (iii) M arises as an involuntary response to a stimulus and is not subject to voluntary control.
conditions: M involves a characteristic bodily sensation — a felt perturbation of the viscera, the musculature, or the skin
M has an identifiable object towards which it is directed
M arises as an involuntary response to a stimulus and is not subject to voluntary control
counterexample: Consider the case of the grief that a man feels, some years after the death of his wife, when he comes upon her handwriting in an old book. The grief is genuine and deep, but it may involve no characteristic bodily sensation — no quickening of the pulse, no tightening of the chest. It may lack an identifiable present object in the ordinary sense, for the wife is dead and the handwriting is merely a trace. And it is not entirely involuntary, for the man might have chosen not to open the book, and he may, by an effort of will, turn his mind to other things and diminish the feeling. Yet the grief is plainly an emotion.
missing_condition: The definition requires bodily sensation, a present identifiable object, and involuntariness, yet emotions may be quiet and bodily unfelt, may be directed at absent or abstract objects, and may be subject to a degree of voluntary modulation. What is wanted is an account that recognises the diversity of emotional experience, including emotions that are dispositional, diffuse, or partially under voluntary governance.
passage: One might propose that a mental state is an emotion if and only if it involves a characteristic bodily sensation, has an identifiable object towards which it is directed, and arises as an involuntary response not subject to voluntary control. The definition captures something true of many emotional episodes. The man who is startled by a sudden loud noise feels his heart race and his muscles tense; his fear is directed at the source of the noise; and his response is involuntary — he did not choose to be afraid. One might suppose that these features are constitutive of emotion. Yet one must attend to the quiet grief of the bereaved. A man whose wife died several years ago is sorting through old books and comes upon a volume in which she had written her name on the flyleaf. A wave of grief overtakes him — or perhaps it does not so much overtake him as settle upon him, gently and pervasively, like a mist. The grief is real and deep, but it may involve no characteristic bodily sensation. There is no racing of the pulse, no tightening of the chest, no trembling of the hands. The feeling is interior, quiet, and diffuse. Its object is, in a certain sense, absent — his wife is dead, and the handwriting is merely a trace of a person who no longer exists. The grief is directed, if it is directed at anything, towards an absence rather than a presence. And the grief is not entirely involuntary; the man might have left the books unsorted, might have closed the volume without reading the inscription, and might, by a deliberate effort of will, turn his thoughts elsewhere and attenuate the feeling. The emotion admits of a degree of voluntary governance that the definition does not allow. Yet the grief is plainly an emotion — one of the deepest and most characteristically human emotions. The definition, in requiring bodily sensation, a present object, and complete involuntariness, mistakes the features of acute, stimulus-driven emotional episodes for features that are necessary to emotion as such.

definition: A person P perceives an object X if and only if (i) X is physically present in P's immediate environment, (ii) P's sensory organs are stimulated by X in a manner that produces a veridical representation of X, and (iii) P is consciously aware of X as a distinct and determinate object.
conditions: X is physically present in P's immediate environment
P's sensory organs are stimulated by X in a manner that produces a veridical representation of X
P is consciously aware of X as a distinct and determinate object
counterexample: Consider the case of a man walking through a dense fog. He perceives the fog — he is aware of the greyish opacity that surrounds him, he feels the dampness on his skin, he notices that sounds are muffled and distances obscured. Yet the fog is not a distinct and determinate object; it has no boundaries, no definite shape, no clear location in space. His sensory organs do not produce a veridical representation of the fog as a determinate thing, for the fog is inherently indeterminate — a diffuse atmospheric condition rather than an object with fixed properties. Yet he perceives it; to say that he does not would be absurd.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the perceived item be a distinct and determinate object of which the perceiver forms a veridical representation, yet perception may be directed at diffuse, indeterminate, boundary-less features of the environment — fogs, sounds, smells, ambient light — that are genuinely perceived but that do not conform to the model of a determinate object producing a veridical sensory image.
passage: One might propose that a person perceives an object if and only if the object is physically present in his immediate environment, his sensory organs are stimulated by it in a manner that produces a veridical representation, and he is consciously aware of it as a distinct and determinate object. The definition has plausibility when one considers the perception of ordinary middle-sized things. A man sees a red apple on the table before him: the apple is present, his eyes are stimulated by light reflected from its surface, and he is consciously aware of it as a distinct, bounded, coloured object. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet one must attend to the perception of fog. A man walks through a dense fog on a winter morning. He perceives the fog — he is aware of the grey opacity that envelops him, he feels the moisture upon his face, he notices the muffling of sounds and the foreshortening of distances. His perception of the fog is as genuine as any perception can be. But the fog is not a distinct and determinate object. It has no boundaries, no definite shape, no fixed location; it is everywhere and nowhere, a diffuse atmospheric condition that blends imperceptibly into the surrounding air. His sensory organs do not produce a veridical representation of the fog as a determinate thing, for the fog is not a determinate thing — it is inherently vague, shifting, and unbounded. And his awareness of the fog is not an awareness of it as a distinct object set over against him; it is an awareness of being immersed in something that pervades his entire environment. The definition, in requiring that perception be of a distinct and determinate object veridically represented, mistakes the features of the perception of solid, bounded things for features that are necessary to perception as such, and thereby excludes the genuine perception of diffuse environmental conditions that are perceived but that resist characterisation as objects.

definition: A person P remembers an event E if and only if (i) P experienced E at the time of its occurrence, (ii) P can produce a detailed and accurate narrative account of E, and (iii) P's present recollection of E preserves the temporal order in which the constituent elements of E occurred.
conditions: P experienced E at the time of its occurrence
P can produce a detailed and accurate narrative account of E
P's present recollection of E preserves the temporal order in which the constituent elements of E occurred
counterexample: Consider the case of an old man who remembers the day of his christening — not from his own experience, for he was an infant and can have had no experience in the relevant sense, but from the stories told to him by his parents throughout his childhood. He is quite certain that he remembers the event: the church, the font, the vicar, the gathered relations. Yet he did not experience the christening in any sense that the first condition requires; he was a babe in arms. He cannot produce a detailed narrative; his recollection consists of a few vivid but fragmentary images. And the temporal order of the constituent elements is confused — he cannot say whether the hymn preceded or followed the baptism. Yet he remembers his christening, or at any rate he remembers something that stands in a genuine mnemonic relation to it.
missing_condition: The definition requires personal experience, narrative detail, and preserved temporal order, yet what people call remembering frequently involves events not directly experienced, fragmentary and impressionistic recollections rather than detailed narratives, and temporally disordered images. What is wanted is a broader account that accommodates inherited memory, fragmentary recall, and the non-narrative character of much genuine remembering.
passage: One might propose that a person remembers an event if and only if he experienced it at the time of its occurrence, can produce a detailed and accurate narrative account of it, and his recollection preserves the temporal order of its constituent elements. The definition captures something true of the most perspicuous cases of memory. A man who was present at the coronation recalls the procession, the oath, the anointing, and the crowning, in that order, and can produce a detailed and substantially accurate account of the whole ceremony. He experienced the event, his narrative is rich, and the temporal order is preserved. One might suppose that these conditions identify what it is to remember. Yet one must attend to the old man and his christening. An elderly gentleman declares that he remembers the day of his christening. He speaks of the parish church, the stone font, the vicar's white surplice, the faces of aunts and uncles gathered round. The memory is vivid to him and feels entirely genuine. But he did not experience the christening in the relevant sense; he was an infant of a few weeks, incapable of forming the kind of experience that the first condition requires. What he remembers is not the event itself but a reconstruction assembled from stories told to him by his parents over many years, from a photograph he once saw, and from his general knowledge of what christenings are like. His recollection is not detailed; it consists of a handful of fragmentary images — the font, the surplice, a blurred face — rather than a connected narrative. And the temporal order is confused; he cannot say whether the hymn was sung before or after the baptism, or whether the gathered relations were present in the church or only at the reception afterwards. Yet it would be harsh and implausible to say that he does not remember his christening. The definition, in requiring personal experience, narrative detail, and temporal order, mistakes the features of vivid, first-hand, well-structured recall for features that are necessary to memory as such.

definition: An organism O is conscious at a given moment if and only if (i) O is awake and responsive to stimulation from the external environment, (ii) O is capable of forming new memories of its present experience, and (iii) O exhibits purposive behaviour directed towards goals.
conditions: O is awake and responsive to stimulation from the external environment
O is capable of forming new memories of its present experience
O exhibits purposive behaviour directed towards goals
counterexample: Consider the case of a person in the grip of a vivid dream. The dreamer is not awake and is not responsive to external stimulation — he lies inert in his bed, and ordinary sounds and touches do not rouse him. He may or may not form lasting memories of the dream; many dreams are forgotten upon waking. And his behaviour is not purposive in any outward sense; his body is still, and any movements are involuntary twitches. Yet he is conscious. The dream is an experience — it has phenomenal character, it may be terrifying or delightful, and the dreamer undergoes it as a genuine episode of conscious life.
missing_condition: The definition identifies consciousness with waking, responsive, memory-forming, purposive states, yet consciousness may occur during sleep, in the absence of external responsiveness, without the formation of lasting memories, and without any outward purposive behaviour. What is wanted is an account that recognises the consciousness present in dreams and other non-waking states.
passage: One might propose that an organism is conscious at a given moment if and only if it is awake and responsive to external stimulation, capable of forming new memories, and exhibiting purposive behaviour directed towards goals. The definition has considerable plausibility. The alert, active human being who is navigating his way through a crowded street — awake, responsive to the sights and sounds around him, forming memories of his route, and acting purposively towards his destination — is a paradigm of consciousness. One might suppose that wakefulness, responsiveness, memory formation, and purposive behaviour jointly constitute what it is to be conscious. Yet one must attend to the dreamer. A man lies asleep in his bed at three in the morning. He is not awake; he is deeply asleep, his eyes closed, his breathing slow and regular. He is not responsive to external stimulation; the ticking of the clock, the sounds of the street, even a gentle touch upon his shoulder, produce no response. He may well form no lasting memory of what he is experiencing; most dreams are forgotten within moments of waking. And he exhibits no purposive behaviour; his body is still, and his occasional movements are involuntary twitches of no apparent purpose. Yet he is dreaming, and his dream is an episode of conscious experience. He sees a landscape, hears voices, feels fear or joy, undergoes adventures that seem perfectly real to him while they last. The phenomenal character of the dream is as vivid as any waking experience. The definition, in requiring wakefulness, responsiveness, memory formation, and purposive behaviour, identifies consciousness with a particular set of conditions characteristic of alert, active, waking life, and thereby excludes the genuine consciousness that occurs in dreams, which are among the most remarkable and pervasive of all conscious phenomena.

definition: A person P intends a consequence C of his action if and only if (i) C is the primary aim that motivates P's action, (ii) P desires that C should occur, and (iii) P would be disappointed if C failed to materialise.
conditions: C is the primary aim that motivates P's action
P desires that C should occur
P would be disappointed if C failed to materialise
counterexample: Consider the case of a surgeon who amputates a patient's gangrenous leg to save his life. The surgeon intends to amputate the leg — the amputation is not an accidental by-product of some other procedure — yet it is not the primary aim that motivates his action. The primary aim is to save the patient's life; the amputation is a means, and a regrettable one. The surgeon does not desire the amputation; he would much prefer that it were unnecessary. And he would not be disappointed if the amputation failed to materialise — on the contrary, he would be relieved if some alternative treatment rendered it unnecessary. Yet he intends the amputation; he performs it deliberately, skilfully, and with full awareness of what he is doing.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the intended consequence be the primary aim, be desired, and be such that its non-occurrence would disappoint, yet a person may intend a consequence that is a means rather than an end, that he does not desire for its own sake, and whose non-occurrence he would welcome. What is wanted is an account of intention that accommodates the intentional performance of unwanted but necessary intermediate steps.
passage: One might propose that a person intends a consequence of his action if and only if that consequence is the primary aim motivating the action, the person desires it, and he would be disappointed if it failed to occur. The definition has some plausibility. A man who plants a tree intends the tree to grow: the growth is the primary aim, he desires it, and he would be disappointed if the tree withered. The three conditions are satisfied, and the intention is clear. Yet one must attend to the surgeon who performs an amputation. A surgeon determines that a patient's gangrenous leg must be removed to save his life. He performs the operation with deliberation, care, and technical skill. He intends to amputate the leg — the amputation is not an unforeseen accident or an incidental by-product; it is what he sets out to do. But the amputation is not the primary aim of his action; the primary aim is to save the patient's life, and the amputation is merely the means. He does not desire the amputation; given a choice, he would far rather cure the gangrene by some less drastic method. And he would not be disappointed if the amputation failed to materialise — if, at the last moment, some newly discovered treatment rendered the operation unnecessary, he would be relieved rather than disappointed. All three conditions fail, yet the surgeon intends the amputation. The definition, in requiring that the intended consequence be desired, aimed at as a primary goal, and such that its non-occurrence would cause disappointment, confuses intending something with wanting it, and thereby fails to accommodate the large class of cases in which a person deliberately and intentionally performs an action that he regards as regrettable but necessary.

definition: A person P at time t1 is the same person as a person Q at time t2 if and only if (i) P and Q have the same continuous stream of memories linking t1 to t2, (ii) P and Q share the same character traits, dispositions, and temperamental qualities, and (iii) P and Q recognise themselves as the same person — that is, Q has a sense of being identical with P.
conditions: P and Q have the same continuous stream of memories linking t1 to t2
P and Q share the same character traits, dispositions, and temperamental qualities
P and Q recognise themselves as the same person — that is, Q has a sense of being identical with P
counterexample: Consider the case of a man who suffers a severe blow to the head and loses all memory of the preceding ten years of his life. His character, moreover, is altered by the injury — formerly placid and retiring, he is now irritable and impulsive. And he does not recognise himself as the same person; he looks at photographs from before the injury and feels no connexion with the man depicted. Yet no one doubts that he is the same person. His wife, his children, his employer, the law — all treat him as the same man, and rightly so.
missing_condition: The definition requires memory continuity, character stability, and self-recognition, yet personal identity may persist through total amnesia, radical changes in character, and the absence of any sense of continuity with one's former self. What is wanted is an account that does not make the subjective sense of identity a necessary condition of identity itself.
passage: One might propose that a person at one time is the same person at a later time if and only if they share a continuous stream of memories, the same character traits and temperamental qualities, and a sense of being the same person. The definition captures something important about the ordinary experience of personal identity. The man who wakes each morning recalls what he did the day before, recognises his own character and dispositions as continuous with those of yesterday, and has an unbroken sense of being the same individual. One might suppose that these three features constitute personal identity over time. Yet one must attend to the case of the man who suffers a severe head injury. A man in the prime of life receives a violent blow to the skull. Upon his recovery, he discovers that he has lost all memory of the preceding decade. The years of his marriage, the births of his children, the undertakings of his profession — all are gone, as thoroughly as if they had never been. His character, moreover, is altered. Before the injury he was patient, mild, and contemplative; after it he is restless, quick to anger, and impatient of reflection. And he does not recognise himself as the same person. He looks at photographs from before the injury and feels no connexion with the man depicted; he reads old letters in his own handwriting and finds them alien. All three conditions fail. Yet he is the same man. His wife addresses him as her husband, his children call him father, his creditors demand payment of his debts, and the law holds him to his contractual obligations. No court would accept the plea that the man before the injury and the man after it are different persons. The definition, in requiring memory continuity, character stability, and self-recognition, mistakes the subjective sense of identity for identity itself, and thereby generates the absurd conclusion that a man ceases to be himself whenever he loses his memory or undergoes a change of character.

definition: A person P's choice C is free if and only if (i) C is not determined by any antecedent desire, habit, or disposition of P, (ii) C is experienced by P as an origination — as something that begins with the act of choosing itself and is not traceable to prior mental states, and (iii) P could, holding all prior conditions fixed, have chosen otherwise.
conditions: C is not determined by any antecedent desire, habit, or disposition of P
C is experienced by P as an origination — as something that begins with the act of choosing itself and is not traceable to prior mental states
C could, holding all prior conditions fixed, have chosen otherwise
counterexample: Consider the case of a man of settled benevolent character who, seeing a child fall into a river, immediately plunges in to rescue her. His choice to save the child is plainly determined by his antecedent dispositions — his compassion, his courage, his habitual concern for others. He does not experience the choice as an origination independent of his prior character; on the contrary, he would say that he could not have done otherwise given the kind of man he is. Yet his choice is free. It is not compelled by any external force; it flows from his own character, and we should think less of him, not more, if his act of rescue were a random event unconnected with his dispositions.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a free choice be undetermined by character, experienced as a pure origination, and such that the agent could have chosen otherwise with everything else held fixed. Yet a choice that springs from settled character — from the agent's own desires, habits, and dispositions — may be free in the most important sense, precisely because it is the agent's own. What is wanted is an account that recognises that freedom may consist in acting from one's character rather than in acting independently of it.
passage: One might propose that a person's choice is free if and only if it is not determined by any antecedent desire, habit, or disposition, is experienced as an origination that begins with the act of choosing itself, and the person could have chosen otherwise with all prior conditions held fixed. The definition has a certain philosophical attraction, for it locates freedom in the radical independence of the will from everything that precedes it. One might think that a truly free choice must be uncaused by anything in the agent's character — must spring, as it were, from nothing but the bare act of willing. Yet one must attend to the man of settled benevolent character. A man renowned for his kindness and courage is walking along a riverbank when he sees a child tumble into the water. Without hesitation — indeed, without any perceptible interval of deliberation — he plunges into the river and pulls the child to safety. His choice to act is plainly determined by his antecedent character: his compassion, his courage, his ingrained habit of helping others in distress. He does not experience the choice as an origination independent of his prior mental states; if asked, he would say that he acted as his character demanded, and that he could not imagine himself standing by while a child drowned. And it is doubtful that he could have chosen otherwise with all prior conditions held fixed; given his character, his training, and his moral convictions, the rescue was, in the relevant sense, inevitable. Yet his act is free. It is not coerced, not compelled by external force, not the product of manipulation or duress. It flows from his own character — from the very dispositions that make him the man he is — and we praise him precisely because the act expresses who he is. The definition, in requiring that free choice be independent of character, mistakes a philosophical ideal of radical indeterminacy for a necessary condition of freedom, when in truth freedom may consist not in acting independently of one's character but in acting from it.

definition: A being B is conscious if and only if (i) B is capable of verbal report — that is, B can describe its internal states in language, (ii) B exhibits purposive behaviour directed towards goals, and (iii) B responds differentially to stimuli in its environment.
conditions: B is capable of verbal report of its internal states
B exhibits purposive behaviour directed towards goals
B responds differentially to stimuli in its environment
counterexample: Consider a human infant of three months. The infant cannot report its internal states in language; it has no command of words and cannot describe what it sees, hears, or feels. Yet the infant is manifestly conscious. It reacts to pain with distress, gazes at faces with evident interest, and startles at sudden noises. It exhibits purposive behaviour — reaching for objects, turning towards sounds — and responds differentially to its environment. The first condition fails, yet the infant's consciousness is not in doubt.
missing_condition: The definition requires verbal report, but consciousness may exist without the capacity for linguistic expression. What is wanted is a recognition that consciousness is a matter of sentient experience, not of the ability to articulate that experience in words.
passage: One might hold that a being is conscious if and only if it is capable of verbal report of its internal states, it exhibits purposive behaviour directed towards goals, and it responds differentially to stimuli in its environment. The definition has the merit of identifying consciousness with something publicly observable: if a being can tell us what it is experiencing, we have the strongest possible evidence that it is conscious. Yet one must consider the case of a human infant. An infant of three months is incapable of verbal report. It possesses no language; it cannot form a single word, let alone describe the character of its visual experience or the quality of its sensations. It cannot tell us that the light is bright, that the milk is warm, or that the blanket is soft. By the terms of the definition, the infant is not conscious. But this conclusion is difficult to accept. The infant reacts to pain with unmistakable distress — it cries, it grimaces, it withdraws the affected limb. It gazes at faces with what can only be described as interest; it tracks moving objects with its eyes; it startles at sudden noises and turns towards the source of familiar voices. These reactions are not those of an automaton devoid of inner experience. The infant exhibits purposive behaviour — it reaches for objects that attract its attention, it turns towards sounds that interest it, it roots for the breast when hungry — and it responds differentially to its environment in ways that strongly suggest the presence of felt experience. The second and third conditions are satisfied. Only the first fails. The definition has taken a feature of mature human consciousness — the ability to describe one's experience in language — and elevated it to a necessary condition of consciousness as such, when in truth consciousness is a matter of sentient awareness that may exist long before, and perhaps entirely without, the capacity for verbal expression.

definition: A person P acts with intention if and only if (i) P has formed a prior mental plan specifying the action to be performed, (ii) P's action conforms to the plan, and (iii) P is consciously aware of executing the plan at the time of acting.
conditions: P has formed a prior mental plan specifying the action
P's action conforms to the plan
P is consciously aware of executing the plan at the time of acting
counterexample: Consider a skilled pianist performing a familiar sonata. The pianist intends to play the piece; her performance is an intentional action in the fullest sense.
