Yet she has not formed a prior mental plan specifying each note to be struck, each fingering to be employed, each dynamic to be observed. The action unfolds spontaneously from practised habit, guided by the score and by the hands' trained facility. Nor is the pianist consciously aware of executing a plan at the time of playing; her attention is upon the music, the phrasing, the expressive character of the performance, not upon any internal set of instructions. The first and third conditions fail, yet the performance is indisputably intentional.
missing_condition: The definition requires a prior mental plan and conscious awareness of executing it, but skilled intentional action may proceed without any such plan, guided instead by embodied habit and trained facility. What is wanted is an account of intention that accommodates the fluent, unreflective character of skilled performance.
passage: One might propose that a person acts with intention if and only if he has formed a prior mental plan specifying the action to be performed, his action conforms to the plan, and he is consciously aware of executing the plan at the time of acting. The definition captures certain deliberate actions well enough. A man who plans a journey — deciding upon the route, the time of departure, and the mode of conveyance — and then carries out the journey in accordance with his plan, fully aware at each stage of what he is doing and why, acts with intention in the sense the definition requires. Yet one must consider the skilled pianist performing a familiar sonata. The pianist intends to play the piece; she has chosen to perform it, she sits down at the instrument with the purpose of playing, and her performance is an intentional action in the fullest philosophical sense. Yet she has not formed a prior mental plan specifying each note to be struck, each chord to be voiced, each passage to be phrased in this way rather than that. The action unfolds from practised habit; her fingers move with a fluency that owes nothing to the conscious rehearsal of instructions and everything to years of disciplined training. The notes emerge not from the execution of an internal set of directions but from the trained responsiveness of the hands to the score and to the sound. Nor is the pianist consciously aware of executing a plan at the time of playing. Her conscious attention is given over to the music — to its phrasing, its dynamics, its expressive character — not to any internal representation of the movements her fingers are performing. The first and third conditions of the definition fail. The analysis has modelled intentional action upon the case of deliberate, premeditated conduct and has overlooked the vast domain of skilled performance, in which action is intentional without being preceded by an explicit plan or accompanied by conscious awareness of its execution.

definition: A person P at time t1 is the same person as a person Q at time t2 if and only if (i) P and Q share the same continuous body, (ii) P and Q are connected by an unbroken chain of memories — Q remembers the experiences of P, and (iii) P and Q share the same character traits and dispositions.
conditions: P and Q share the same continuous body
P and Q are connected by an unbroken chain of memories
P and Q share the same character traits and dispositions
counterexample: Consider a man who suffers a complete and permanent loss of memory as a result of a severe illness. Before the illness, he was a scholar of classical languages, reserved in temperament, and devoted to solitary study. After the illness, he remembers nothing of his former life — not his scholarship, not his friendships, not a single event of his past. His character, too, has altered: he is sociable where he was reserved, indifferent to learning where he was devoted to it. Yet no one — neither his family, nor the law, nor common sense — doubts that he is the same person. The second and third conditions fail, but the identity is unquestioned.
missing_condition: The definition requires memory continuity and sameness of character, but personal identity may persist through total amnesia and radical character change. What is wanted is a recognition that bodily continuity may suffice for personal identity even when psychological continuity is wholly absent.
passage: One might hold that a person at one time is the same person as a person at a later time if and only if the two share the same continuous body, are connected by an unbroken chain of memories, and share the same character traits and dispositions. The definition has considerable plausibility. When I say that I am the same person who attended school as a child, I appeal to the continuity of my body, to my memories of childhood, and to the persistence of certain traits of character that have accompanied me throughout my life. Yet one must consider the case of a man who suffers a complete and permanent loss of memory following a severe illness — a high fever, let us say, that damages the brain. Before the illness, the man was a scholar of classical languages, a person of reserved and studious temperament, devoted to the quiet pleasures of learning. After the illness, he remembers nothing of his former life. He does not remember his years of study, does not remember his friends or his family, does not remember a single event that occurred before his illness. His character, too, has undergone a profound alteration. He is now sociable and gregarious where he was reserved and solitary; he is indifferent to books and learning where he was once consumed by them. The second and third conditions of the definition both fail. There is no memory connecting the man after the illness with the man before it, and the character traits and dispositions that defined him have been wholly replaced. Yet no one doubts that he is the same person. His family recognises him; the law regards him as the same individual; his property remains his own. The analysis has required too much for personal identity, treating memory and character as necessary conditions when in truth they are contingent accompaniments of a more fundamental continuity — the continuity of the living body.

definition: A person P acts freely with respect to an action A if and only if (i) P deliberates among alternative courses of action before performing A, (ii) P could have done otherwise — that is, it was within P's power to refrain from A, and (iii) P's choice of A is not determined by any antecedent cause.
conditions: P deliberates among alternative courses of action before performing A
P could have done otherwise
P's choice is not determined by any antecedent cause
counterexample: Consider a man who sees a child fall into a river and, without a moment's deliberation, leaps in to save the child. He does not pause to weigh alternatives; he does not consider the possibility of walking past; he acts immediately and unreflectively. The first condition fails — he does not deliberate among alternatives. Yet his action is paradigmatically free. It is an expression of his deepest values and of a character formed by years of moral cultivation. To say that he acted unfreely because he did not deliberate would be to confuse freedom with indecision.
missing_condition: The definition requires deliberation among alternatives, but spontaneous action flowing from settled character may be free in the highest degree. What is wanted is a recognition that freedom of action does not require prior deliberation but may consist in acting from one's own character and values.
passage: One might propose that a person acts freely with respect to an action if and only if he deliberates among alternative courses of action before performing it, he could have done otherwise, and his choice is not determined by any antecedent cause. The definition captures a familiar picture of free action: the agent surveys the alternatives before him, weighs their merits, selects one, and acts — all without being determined by any prior cause to choose as he does. Yet one must consider the case of a man walking along a riverbank who sees a small child fall into the water. The child is in danger of drowning. The man does not pause to deliberate. He does not consider the alternative of walking past, does not weigh the risks of diving in against the inconvenience of wet clothing, does not survey the situation with the detachment of one choosing among options. He leaps into the river immediately and without reflection, grasps the child, and brings it to safety. He acts, as we say, without thinking. The first condition of the definition fails. The man did not deliberate among alternatives before acting. Yet his action is, by any reasonable assessment, paradigmatically free. It is not the action of a man compelled by external force or internal compulsion. It is the spontaneous expression of a character shaped by years of moral formation — of settled dispositions of courage and compassion that have become so deeply ingrained that they issue in action without the intermediary of conscious deliberation. Far from being unfree, the man's unreflective rescue of the child is an instance of freedom at its highest: the freedom of a person whose actions flow naturally from what he most deeply is. The definition has confused freedom with the process of deliberation, overlooking the possibility that the most free of all actions are those that spring from character so settled that deliberation is unnecessary.

definition: A bodily movement M is an action if and only if (i) M is preceded by a distinct mental event — a volition or act of will — that causes M, (ii) M is under the agent's voluntary control throughout its execution, and (iii) the agent can give reasons for performing M.
conditions: M is preceded by a distinct mental event — a volition — that causes it
M is under the agent's voluntary control throughout its execution
the agent can give reasons for performing M
counterexample: Consider the act of breathing. Breathing is, under ordinary circumstances, an action — one can breathe deeply, hold one's breath, or breathe slowly at will. Yet breathing is not preceded by a distinct volition on each occasion; one breathes continuously without forming any mental act of will to initiate each breath. Nor is breathing under voluntary control throughout its execution in the ordinary case; it proceeds automatically, governed by physiological mechanisms, and one is scarcely aware of it. Yet breathing can be brought under voluntary control at any moment, and this capacity distinguishes it from a mere reflex. The first and second conditions fail in the ordinary case, yet breathing is not a mere involuntary occurrence.
missing_condition: The definition requires a distinct preceding volition and continuous voluntary control, but many actions — habitual, automatic, and semi-voluntary actions such as breathing — proceed without either. What is wanted is a recognition that an action may be characterised by the agent's capacity to intervene and control the movement, rather than by the actual exercise of such control on every occasion.
passage: One might hold that a bodily movement is an action if and only if it is preceded by a distinct mental event — a volition or act of will — that causes the movement, the movement is under the agent's voluntary control throughout its execution, and the agent can give reasons for performing it. The definition captures the most deliberate of human actions. A man who raises his arm to signal a cab forms a volition to raise his arm, the arm rises under his control, and he can explain that he raised it in order to attract the cab driver's attention. Yet one must consider the act of breathing. Breathing is, in one important sense, an action. It can be performed deliberately — one can take a deep breath, hold one's breath, or regulate one's breathing in the course of meditation or physical exertion. One can give reasons for breathing in a particular way: 'I breathed deeply because I was preparing to dive,' or 'I held my breath because the air was foul.' Yet in the ordinary course of life, breathing is not preceded by a distinct volition on each occasion. One does not form a mental act of will to initiate each inhalation and exhalation; the process continues without any conscious intention, governed by the automatic mechanisms of the body. Nor is breathing under voluntary control throughout its execution in the ordinary case; one breathes in one's sleep, whilst absorbed in conversation, whilst unconscious of the act entirely. The first and second conditions of the definition fail in the standard case of breathing. Yet breathing is not a mere involuntary occurrence on a par with the beating of the heart; it can be brought under voluntary control at any moment, and this distinguishing feature — the availability of voluntary control, rather than its continuous exercise — marks it as an action of a kind that the definition, with its insistence upon a preceding volition and continuous oversight, cannot accommodate.

definition: A mental state S is an emotion if and only if (i) S involves a felt qualitative experience — a distinctive 'what it is like' character, (ii) S is accompanied by characteristic bodily sensations, such as a quickened pulse or a tightening of the chest, and (iii) S is episodic — that is, S has a definite onset and a definite termination.
conditions: S involves a felt qualitative experience
S is accompanied by characteristic bodily sensations
S is episodic — having a definite onset and termination
counterexample: Consider a mother's love for her child. This love is an emotion — perhaps the most powerful of all emotions — and it involves a felt qualitative experience of great depth. Yet it is not episodic. It does not have a definite onset and a definite termination in the manner of a sudden fright or a burst of anger. It persists over years, indeed over a lifetime, as a steady and enduring affective orientation towards the child. It is not an episode but a standing condition. The third condition fails, yet the mother's love is an emotion by any reasonable standard.
missing_condition: The definition requires that emotions be episodic, but some emotions — notably love, grief, and enduring resentment — persist as standing affective orientations rather than as discrete episodes. What is wanted is an account that accommodates both episodic emotions and enduring emotional states.
passage: One might propose that a mental state is an emotion if and only if it involves a felt qualitative experience, it is accompanied by characteristic bodily sensations, and it is episodic — that is, it has a definite onset and a definite termination. The definition captures many familiar emotions. A sudden fright has a distinctive felt quality — a rush of alarm, a sense of danger — and is accompanied by a quickened pulse, a surge of adrenaline, and a tensing of the muscles. It has a definite onset — the moment of the startling event — and a definite termination — the subsidence of the alarm once the danger has passed. A burst of anger, similarly, has its characteristic felt quality, its bodily accompaniments, and its episodic character. Yet one must consider a mother's love for her child. This love is an emotion in any ordinary sense of the word; it is an affective state of the greatest depth and significance. It involves a felt qualitative experience — a warmth, a tenderness, a fierce protectiveness that the mother recognises and that colours her experience of the world. But it is not episodic. It does not have a definite onset in the manner of a sudden fright; the mother cannot point to a moment at which her love began and before which it did not exist. It does not have a definite termination; it persists over years, over decades, over the whole of a lifetime. It is not an episode but a standing orientation of the whole person towards the child — an enduring affective condition that may wax and wane in intensity but that never wholly ceases. The third condition of the definition fails. The analysis has modelled emotion upon the episodic case — the sudden fright, the burst of anger, the pang of jealousy — and has overlooked the existence of emotions that persist as enduring states rather than as transient episodes.

definition: A person P perceives an object O if and only if (i) O exists independently of P's mind, (ii) O causally affects P's sense organs, producing a sensory experience, and (iii) P's experience accurately represents the properties of O.
conditions: O exists independently of P's mind
O causally affects P's sense organs
P's experience accurately represents the properties of O
counterexample: Consider a person who views a straight stick half-submerged in water. The stick appears bent at the point where it enters the water. The person's experience does not accurately represent the properties of the stick; the stick is straight, but it appears bent. The third condition fails. Yet the person perceives the stick. He sees it; his visual experience is caused by the stick's presence and by the refraction of light at the water's surface. The perception is veridical in that a real object is seen, even though one of its properties is misrepresented.
missing_condition: The definition requires accurate representation, but perception may occur even when the experience misrepresents some properties of the perceived object. What is wanted is a recognition that perception is a matter of causal contact with an object, not of the accuracy of every aspect of the resulting experience.
passage: One might hold that a person perceives an object if and only if the object exists independently of the person's mind, the object causally affects the person's sense organs and thereby produces a sensory experience, and the person's experience accurately represents the properties of the object. The definition captures the ideal case of perception. When I look at a red ball in good light, the ball exists independently of my mind, light reflected from its surface strikes my retina and produces a visual experience, and my experience represents the ball as red, round, and of a certain size — all of which it is. Yet one must consider the familiar case of the stick half-submerged in water. A straight stick is placed in a vessel of clear water so that half of it is above the surface and half below. A person looking at the stick sees it as bent at the point where it enters the water. The stick is not bent; it is perfectly straight; the appearance of bending is produced by the refraction of light at the interface of air and water. The person's experience does not accurately represent the properties of the stick. It represents the stick as bent when it is straight. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet the person perceives the stick. He sees it before him; his visual experience is caused by the stick and by the light it reflects; and if asked what he perceives, he will say, quite correctly, that he perceives a stick in the water. His perception is not an hallucination — there really is a stick there — nor is it a case of seeing nothing at all. It is a genuine perception of a real object, albeit one in which a particular property of the object is misrepresented by the experience. The definition has required too much of perception, treating accurate representation as a necessary condition when in truth perception may be partially illusory and yet remain perception.

definition: A person P remembers an event E if and only if (i) P has a present mental representation of E, (ii) E actually occurred in P's past, (iii) P's present representation of E is caused by P's original experience of E, and (iv) P's representation accurately preserves the details of E as it was originally experienced.
conditions: P has a present mental representation of E
E actually occurred in P's past
P's present representation is caused by P's original experience of E
P's representation accurately preserves the details of E
counterexample: Consider a man who remembers his wedding day. He has a vivid present representation of the event; the event actually occurred; his representation is caused, through the normal processes of memory, by his original experience. Yet after thirty years, many of the details have altered in his recollection. He remembers the sun shining, though in fact it rained; he remembers his bride wearing white, though she wore cream; he remembers the church as large and grand, though it was modest. His representation does not accurately preserve the details of the event. The fourth condition fails, yet he remembers his wedding day. The memory is imperfect, but it is genuine.
missing_condition: The definition requires accurate preservation of details, but memory is characteristically reconstructive and inexact. What is wanted is a recognition that a genuine memory may diverge from the original experience in its details whilst remaining a memory of the event in question.
passage: One might propose that a person remembers an event if and only if the person has a present mental representation of the event, the event actually occurred, the representation is caused by the original experience, and the representation accurately preserves the details of the event as originally experienced. The definition captures an ideal of memory — the faithful mental reproduction of a past experience — that has a long pedigree in philosophical thought. Yet one must consider the common case of a man who remembers his wedding day, an event that occurred some thirty years previously. He has a vivid present representation of the occasion. He can picture the church, the faces of the guests, the exchange of vows. The event actually occurred; he was married on the date in question, in the church he recalls. His present representation is caused, through the normal processes of memory retention and retrieval, by his original experience of the day. But after three decades, his recollection has drifted from the facts. He remembers the sun shining through the windows of the church; in truth, the day was overcast and it rained throughout the afternoon. He remembers his bride wearing a white dress; she wore cream. He remembers the church as large and imposing, with high stone arches and stained glass; the church was, in fact, a modest building of no particular distinction. His representation does not accurately preserve the details of the event. The fourth condition of the definition fails. Yet the man remembers his wedding day. His memory is not an invention; it is not a confabulation; it is not an imagining. It is a genuine, if imperfect, recollection of an event that actually occurred in his past and that is causally connected to his present representation. The definition has demanded of memory a fidelity that memory characteristically lacks, overlooking the well-established fact that human memory is reconstructive rather than reproductive, and that the details of remembered events are routinely altered, embellished, and simplified in the course of retention.

definition: A mental state S is a conscious state if and only if (i) the subject is aware of being in S — that is, the subject has a higher-order representation of S, (ii) S is available for use in reasoning and the rational control of behaviour, and (iii) S is distinguishable by the subject from all other mental states.
conditions: the subject is aware of being in S — has a higher-order representation of S
S is available for use in reasoning and rational control of behaviour
S is distinguishable by the subject from all other mental states
counterexample: Consider the experience of a dull, diffuse anxiety that pervades one's waking hours without one's being able to identify it or distinguish it clearly from other affective states. The subject may be conscious of this anxiety — it colours his experience, affects his mood, and influences his behaviour — yet he may not be aware of being in this particular state. He may not have a higher-order representation of the anxiety as anxiety; he may simply feel uneasy without being able to say what he feels or to distinguish it from general fatigue or low spirits. The first and third conditions fail, yet the anxiety is a conscious state.
missing_condition: The definition requires higher-order awareness and clear distinguishability, but many conscious states — diffuse moods, vague feelings, background anxieties — are experienced without being clearly identified or distinguished. What is wanted is a recognition that consciousness does not require reflective self-awareness of the state one is in.
passage: One might hold that a mental state is a conscious state if and only if the subject is aware of being in that state, the state is available for use in reasoning and the rational control of behaviour, and the state is distinguishable by the subject from all other mental states. The definition captures a certain philosophical picture of consciousness: the conscious mind is transparent to itself, and every conscious state is one of which the subject is reflexively aware and which the subject can identify and distinguish from every other state. Yet one must consider the experience of a dull, diffuse anxiety that pervades one's waking hours. The subject feels uneasy; something is wrong; a cloud of apprehension hangs over his day. The anxiety is a conscious state — it is experienced, it colours perception, it affects mood and behaviour. The subject does not sleep through it; he is not anaesthetised; the anxiety is part of his lived experience. Yet he may not be aware of being in this particular state in the sense the definition requires. He does not have a clear higher-order representation of his state as anxiety. If asked what he feels, he may say merely that he feels out of sorts, or tired, or unwell; he cannot put his finger on the nature of the feeling. Nor can he distinguish this state clearly from other states — from general fatigue, from low spirits, from the residual effects of a poor night's sleep. The first and third conditions of the definition fail. The anxiety is a conscious state that is experienced without being clearly identified, a state that is felt without being reflectively known. The definition has demanded of consciousness a degree of self-transparency that the mind does not always possess, treating reflective self-awareness as a necessary condition of consciousness when in truth many conscious states are experienced in a confused and inarticulate manner.

definition: A person P's will is free if and only if (i) P's desires are not the product of external manipulation or coercion, (ii) P endorses his desires upon reflection — that is, P desires to have the desires that move him to act, and (iii) P's reflective endorsement is itself not the product of manipulation.
conditions: P's desires are not the product of external manipulation or coercion
P endorses his desires upon reflection
P's reflective endorsement is itself not the product of manipulation
counterexample: Consider a person who has been raised from birth within a particular religious tradition. His desires — to worship, to observe the rituals, to live according to the precepts of the faith — are, in a sense, the product of his upbringing, which was not chosen by him and which might, on a strict reading, be regarded as a form of external influence. He endorses these desires upon reflection; he has examined his faith in maturity and affirms it. His endorsement is not the product of manipulation in any ordinary sense. Yet the strict application of the first condition threatens to exclude him, for his fundamental desires are the product of an upbringing he did not choose. The condition, if pressed, would render every person's will unfree, since all desires are shaped by unchosen influences.
missing_condition: The definition requires that desires not be the product of external influence, but all desires are shaped by unchosen factors — upbringing, culture, temperament. What is wanted is a distinction between legitimate formative influences and illegitimate manipulation, not a requirement that desires arise from no external source.
passage: One might propose that a person's will is free if and only if his desires are not the product of external manipulation or coercion, he endorses his desires upon reflection, and his reflective endorsement is itself not the product of manipulation. The definition captures an important aspect of the distinction between free and unfree agency. A person who has been hypnotised or subjected to systematic indoctrination may harbour desires that are not his own in any deep sense, and his will is compromised. Yet one must consider the case of a person raised from birth within a particular religious tradition. He was baptised as an infant, educated in the school of his faith, and brought up in a household where the observance of religious duties was woven into the fabric of daily life. His desires — to worship, to pray, to live according to the precepts of his religion — are shaped by this upbringing. He did not choose to be raised in this tradition; his parents chose it for him. His fundamental desires, the desires that constitute the orientation of his life, are in this sense the product of influences external to his own choosing. In maturity, he reflects upon his faith and affirms it. He examines the tenets of his religion, considers alternatives, and concludes that his faith is sound and that his desires are ones he wishes to have. His endorsement is genuine and unmanipulated. Yet the first condition of the definition, strictly applied, is troublesome. His desires are the product of an upbringing he did not choose — an upbringing imposed upon him by others. If the formation of desires by unchosen external influences counts as the kind of external influence the definition is meant to exclude, then the condition fails, and his will is not free. But this conclusion, if accepted, would render every person's will unfree, for no one chooses the influences — the family, the culture, the language, the temperament — that shape his fundamental desires. The definition has failed to distinguish between the legitimate formative influences that shape every human character and the illegitimate manipulations that genuinely compromise freedom of the will.

definition: An event E is an action performed by a person P if and only if (i) E is a bodily movement of P, (ii) E is caused by P's beliefs and desires in the appropriate way, and (iii) P is the sole cause of E — that is, no other agent or external force contributes causally to E.
conditions: E is a bodily movement of P
E is caused by P's beliefs and desires in the appropriate way
P is the sole cause of E — no other agent or external force contributes
counterexample: Consider two men carrying a heavy table through a doorway. Each man's movements are his own actions — he lifts, he steps, he adjusts his grip. Each man's movements are caused by his beliefs and desires: he believes that the table must be moved, and he desires to move it. Yet neither man is the sole cause of the table's movement through the doorway; each depends upon the other's contribution. The third condition fails for each man's action, yet each man is acting.
missing_condition: The definition requires sole causation, but many actions — indeed, most cooperative and social actions — are performed jointly, with multiple agents contributing causally. What is wanted is a recognition that an action may be an individual's own even when it is performed in concert with others.
passage: One might hold that an event is an action performed by a person if and only if it is a bodily movement of that person, it is caused by the person's beliefs and desires in the appropriate way, and the person is the sole cause of the event. The definition captures the solitary case well enough. A man who lifts a stone from the ground acts alone; the lifting is his bodily movement, caused by his desire to pick up the stone and his belief that he can do so, and no other agent or force contributes to the lifting. Yet one must consider the case of two men carrying a heavy table through a narrow doorway. Each man takes hold of one end of the table, and together they lift it, manoeuvre it through the opening, and set it down on the other side. Each man's movements are his own actions. Each man lifts, steps forward, adjusts his grip, tilts the table to clear the frame — all in response to his beliefs about the dimensions of the doorway and his desire to move the table through it. His movements are caused by his beliefs and desires in the appropriate way. But neither man is the sole cause of the table's passage through the doorway. Each depends upon the other. If either man releases his end, the table falls. The movement of the table is the joint product of two agents acting in concert, and the third condition of the definition fails for each man individually. Yet each man is performing an action — lifting, carrying, manoeuvring — and the fact that his action is performed in cooperation with another does not deprive it of its character as action. The definition has taken the features of solitary action and imposed them as conditions upon action as such, overlooking the pervasive fact that human beings act together, and that cooperative action is no less genuinely action for being jointly caused.

definition: A person P perceives an object O if and only if (i) P has a sensory experience with phenomenal content that represents O, (ii) P can attend to O and discriminate O from its surroundings, and (iii) P can identify O — that is, P can say what O is.
conditions: P has a sensory experience representing O
P can attend to O and discriminate O from its surroundings
P can identify O — P can say what O is
counterexample: Consider a person walking through a dense forest who hears an unfamiliar sound — a low, intermittent rustling from somewhere in the undergrowth. The person has a sensory experience that represents the sound; he can attend to it and discriminate it from the ambient noise of the wind and the birdsong. Yet he cannot identify it. He cannot say what it is — whether it is an animal, the wind in the leaves, or running water. He perceives the sound; he hears it clearly; but he does not know what it is. The third condition fails, yet the perception is genuine.
missing_condition: The definition requires identification, but perception may occur without the perceiver's being able to identify what he perceives. What is wanted is a recognition that perception is a matter of sensory awareness, not of classificatory knowledge.
passage: One might propose that a person perceives an object if and only if the person has a sensory experience that represents the object, the person can attend to the object and discriminate it from its surroundings, and the person can identify the object. The definition captures the case of clear, informed perception. When I see a robin perched upon a branch, I have a visual experience that represents the bird, I can attend to it and distinguish it from the foliage, and I can identify it — I can say that it is a robin. Yet one must consider the case of a person walking through a dense and unfamiliar forest. From somewhere in the undergrowth comes a sound — a low, intermittent rustling that rises and falls without pattern. The person stops and listens. He has a sensory experience that represents the sound; he hears it clearly. He can attend to it and discriminate it from the ambient sounds of the forest — from the wind in the canopy above, from the distant call of a bird, from the creak of a branch. But he cannot identify it. He does not know what is producing the sound. Is it an animal moving through the leaves? Is it the wind disturbing the undergrowth? Is it water flowing over stones beneath the leaf litter? He cannot say. He perceives the sound — he hears it as clearly as he hears his own footsteps — but he does not know what it is. The third condition of the definition fails. The analysis has required of perception a capacity for identification that goes beyond what perception itself provides. Perception gives us sensory awareness of objects and events in our environment; identification — the classification of what is perceived under a concept — is a further cognitive achievement that depends upon knowledge, experience, and conceptual resources that the perceiver may lack. One may perceive what one cannot identify.

definition: A creature C is conscious if and only if (i) C is capable of receiving sensory stimuli from its environment, (ii) C exhibits differential behavioural responses to those stimuli, and (iii) C can report upon or otherwise indicate its internal states to an external observer.
conditions: C is capable of receiving sensory stimuli from its environment
C exhibits differential behavioural responses to those stimuli
C can report upon or otherwise indicate its internal states to an external observer
counterexample: Consider a human infant of three weeks. The infant receives sensory stimuli in abundance — light, sound, warmth, the pressure of being held — and exhibits differential responses: it turns toward a voice, recoils from a bright flash, cries when cold. Yet the infant cannot report upon its internal states in any recognisable sense; it has no language, no gesture system, no capacity for the kind of indication that the third condition demands. One would hesitate to deny that the infant is conscious.
missing_condition: The definition requires the capacity for self-report or indication of internal states, but consciousness may be present in creatures — infants, certain animals — that lack any means of communicating their inner life to others.
passage: One might propose that a creature is conscious if and only if it is capable of receiving sensory stimuli from its environment, it exhibits differential behavioural responses to those stimuli, and it can report upon or otherwise indicate its internal states to an external observer. The definition appears to capture a great deal of what we ordinarily mean when we speak of consciousness. A man who sees a red pillar-box, steps around it, and later tells his companion that he noticed it satisfies all three conditions; and we should not hesitate to call him conscious. An automaton that responds to light by turning a photocell but cannot report upon its internal workings fails the third condition, and we should not wish to call it conscious. Yet one must consider the case of a human infant of three weeks. The infant receives sensory stimuli in abundance — the light of the nursery, the sound of its mother's voice, the warmth of a blanket, the pressure of being held. It exhibits differential behavioural responses with considerable specificity: it turns its head toward a familiar voice, recoils from a sudden bright flash, cries when it is cold or hungry, and relaxes when it is warm and fed. But the infant cannot report upon its internal states in any recognisable sense. It possesses no language, no system of gestures that might serve as a surrogate for language, no capacity for the kind of indication that the third condition demands. The infant's cry, to be sure, is a response to an internal state, but it is not a report upon that state; the infant does not cry in order to communicate its discomfort, for it has no concept of communication at all. The third condition thus fails. Yet it appears absurd to deny that the infant is conscious. The definition has mistaken a sophisticated cognitive achievement — the capacity for self-report — for a necessary condition of consciousness itself, when in truth consciousness may be present wherever there is subjective experience, whether or not the creature possessing it has any means of making that experience available to others.

definition: An agent A intends to do X if and only if (i) A has formed a mental representation of X as a future action, (ii) A believes that X is within A's power to bring about, and (iii) A has deliberated about whether to do X and concluded in favour of doing it.
conditions: A has formed a mental representation of X as a future action
A believes that X is within A's power to bring about
A has deliberated about whether to do X and concluded in favour of doing it
counterexample: Consider a man walking along a pavement who, upon seeing an acquaintance approaching, spontaneously raises his hand in greeting. He intends to greet his acquaintance — the gesture is not a reflex or a twitch — and yet he has not deliberated about whether to raise his hand. The action arises immediately, without any process of weighing reasons or concluding in favour of one course over another. The third condition is not satisfied, yet the intention is plainly present.
missing_condition: The definition requires prior deliberation, but many genuine intentions arise spontaneously and without any process of reasoning, as in habitual or socially conditioned actions that are nonetheless intentional.
passage: It is natural to suppose that an agent intends to do something if and only if the agent has formed a mental representation of the action as a future event, believes the action to be within his power, and has deliberated about whether to perform it and concluded in favour of doing so. This analysis has the merit of distinguishing intention from mere wish — for one may wish to fly without believing it to be within one's power — and from idle fancy, which involves no deliberation. A man who resolves, after careful thought, to resign his position next month satisfies all three conditions, and we should say without hesitation that he intends to resign. Yet one must consider a different sort of case. A man is walking along a pavement in the ordinary way when he sees an acquaintance approaching from the opposite direction. Without any pause for thought, without weighing reasons or considering alternatives, he raises his hand in greeting. The gesture is not a reflex — it is not of the same kind as a knee-jerk or a blink in response to a sudden light. It is directed at a particular person, it conforms to a social convention, and the agent would, if asked, acknowledge it as something he did intentionally. He intended to greet his acquaintance. Yet he did not deliberate. There was no process of considering whether to raise his hand, no weighing of the greeting against the alternative of walking past in silence, no conclusion reached at the terminus of a chain of reasoning. The intention arose spontaneously, as intentions very often do in the course of social life. The third condition of the definition fails. One might observe that a very great proportion of our intentional actions are of this spontaneous character — we reach for a cup of tea, we step aside to let another person pass, we nod in agreement during conversation — and that deliberation, far from being a necessary condition of intention, is the exception rather than the rule.

definition: A person P at time t2 is the same person as P at time t1 if and only if (i) P at t2 has a continuous chain of memories linking back to experiences had by P at t1, (ii) P at t2 possesses the same body — that is, the same spatiotemporally continuous physical organism — as P at t1, and (iii) P at t2 recognises P at t1's commitments, relationships, and projects as his own.
conditions: P at t2 has a continuous chain of memories linking back to experiences had by P at t1
P at t2 possesses the same spatiotemporally continuous physical organism as P at t1
P at t2 recognises P at t1's commitments, relationships, and projects as his own
counterexample: Consider a man who suffers a severe blow to the head and loses all memory of the first thirty years of his life. He cannot recall his childhood, his schooling, his marriage, or the birth of his children. There is no continuous chain of memories linking his present self to the person he was before the injury. Yet his wife, his children, and his colleagues do not doubt that he is the same person; and the law, which has practical reasons for precision in these matters, treats him as the same individual. He retains his body, and in time he may come to recognise certain of his former commitments, but the first condition has failed entirely.
missing_condition: The definition requires a continuous chain of memories, but personal identity may persist through radical amnesia, suggesting that memory is not a necessary condition but at most a typical accompaniment of identity over time.
passage: One might define personal identity over time by stipulating that a person at a later time is the same person as at an earlier time if and only if there exists a continuous chain of memories linking the later self to the experiences of the earlier self, the later self possesses the same spatiotemporally continuous physical organism, and the later self recognises the earlier self's commitments, relationships, and projects as his own. The definition has a certain comprehensiveness, drawing as it does upon the three most prominent traditions in the analysis of personal identity: the psychological, the bodily, and what one might call the narrative or practical. A man who wakes each morning remembering the day before, occupying the same body, and taking up the same responsibilities seems to satisfy all three conditions, and we should not hesitate to call him the same person he was yesterday. But consider a man who suffers a severe blow to the head — a fall from a horse, let us say, or a motor accident — and loses all memory of the first thirty years of his life. He cannot recall his childhood in the village where he was raised, the years at school and university, his courtship and marriage, or the birth of his three children. The memories are not merely difficult to retrieve; they are gone, destroyed by the trauma to his brain. There is no continuous chain of memories linking his present self to the person who married his wife or held his first child. Yet no one who observes this man — not his wife, not his children, not his physician, not the magistrate who must determine his legal obligations — doubts that he is the same person. He has the same body, the same face, the same voice. In time, as he is told of his former life, he may come to accept certain commitments as his own, though this acceptance is not remembering but rather a new act of adoption. The first condition of the definition has failed. The analysis has assumed that memory is a necessary thread in the fabric of identity, when in truth the fabric may hold even when that thread is entirely severed.

definition: An agent A acts freely in doing X if and only if (i) A could have done otherwise — that is, there existed at least one alternative action open to A at the moment of choice, (ii) A's doing X was not the result of external compulsion or coercion, and (iii) A's decision to do X was the product of A's own rational deliberation.
conditions: A could have done otherwise — there existed at least one alternative action open to A
A's doing X was not the result of external compulsion or coercion
A's decision to do X was the product of A's own rational deliberation
counterexample: Consider a woman who, upon hearing a child cry out from a burning building, rushes in without a moment's thought and carries the child to safety. She was not compelled by any external force — no one pushed her, no one threatened her — and she could, in some sense, have done otherwise, for she might have stood frozen or run away. But her decision was not the product of rational deliberation. She did not weigh the risks, consider the alternatives, and conclude that entering the building was the best course of action. She acted from an immediate, unreflective impulse of compassion. Yet we should wish to say that she acted freely — indeed, that her action was among the freest and most admirable actions a person can perform.
missing_condition: The definition requires that free action be the product of rational deliberation, but some of the actions we most readily call free — heroic rescues, acts of spontaneous generosity — arise from immediate impulse rather than reflective reasoning.
passage: It appears natural to say that an agent acts freely in doing something if and only if the agent could have done otherwise, the action was not the result of external compulsion or coercion, and the decision to act was the product of the agent's own rational deliberation. This analysis captures a good deal of ordinary usage. A man who considers two positions of employment, weighs the salary and conditions of each, and chooses the one that better suits his purposes seems to act freely in every sense that matters. A man who hands over his wallet at gunpoint does not act freely, for he acts under coercion. A man locked in a room who cannot leave does not act freely in remaining, for he could not have done otherwise. Yet one must consider a rather different case. A woman is walking along a street when she hears a child cry out from the upper window of a building that is on fire. Without a moment's thought — without any process that could be called deliberation — she rushes through the open door, climbs the stairs through the smoke, finds the child, and carries it to safety. She was not compelled; no one pushed her through the door or threatened her with harm if she failed to act. She could, in some sense, have done otherwise; she might have stood frozen on the pavement, or turned and walked away, or waited for the fire brigade. But her decision — if it can be called a decision — was not the product of rational deliberation. She did not pause to weigh the probability that the staircase would hold her weight, or to consider whether the risk to her own life was justified by the prospect of saving the child's. She acted from an immediate, unreflective impulse of compassion, a movement of the whole person toward the suffering of another. Yet it would be strange in the extreme to say that she did not act freely. Her action was, one might think, freer than most, for it expressed her deepest character without the interference of calculation or self-interest. The definition has made rational deliberation a necessary condition of free action, when in truth some of the actions we most admire — and most readily call free — arise from sources deeper than deliberation.

definition: An event E is an action performed by agent A if and only if (i) E is a bodily movement of A, (ii) E is caused by a prior mental state of A — a desire, intention, or volition, and (iii) A is aware of E while it is occurring.
conditions: E is a bodily movement of A
E is caused by a prior mental state of A — a desire, intention, or volition
A is aware of E while it is occurring
counterexample: Consider a man who, while deeply absorbed in a philosophical argument at his desk, drums his fingers upon the table in a rhythmic pattern. He is not aware that he is doing this; his attention is entirely upon the argument. When a colleague draws his attention to the drumming, he is surprised. The drumming is a bodily movement, and it is presumably caused by some mental state — perhaps restlessness, perhaps a habitual disposition associated with concentration — yet the agent is not aware of it while it occurs. We should nonetheless wish to call it an action, for it is not a reflex or an involuntary spasm; it is a patterned, coordinated movement of the sort that only an agent can produce.
missing_condition: The definition requires concurrent awareness, but agents regularly perform actions — habitual gestures, absent-minded movements — of which they are not aware at the time, and these are still properly called actions rather than mere happenings.
passage: One might propose that an event is an action performed by an agent if and only if it is a bodily movement of the agent, it is caused by a prior mental state — a desire, intention, or volition — and the agent is aware of the event while it is occurring. The definition is designed to exclude mere bodily happenings such as a sneeze or a knee-jerk reflex, which are bodily movements but are not caused by any prior intention and are not under the agent's conscious direction. It is also designed to exclude cases in which the body moves as a result of external force — as when a man is pushed from behind and stumbles forward — for in such a case the movement is not caused by the agent's own mental states. A man who deliberately raises his arm to hail a cab satisfies all three conditions: the arm-raising is a bodily movement, it is caused by his intention to hail a cab, and he is aware of raising his arm while he does so. But consider a man who is seated at his desk, deeply absorbed in a philosophical argument. While his mind is occupied with the intricacies of the problem, his fingers drum upon the table in a rhythmic, repeated pattern. He is not aware that he is doing this. His attention is entirely upon the argument before him, and the drumming proceeds without any conscious direction or monitoring. When a colleague enters the room and remarks upon the noise, the man is surprised; he had not noticed his fingers moving at all. The drumming is a bodily movement, and it is presumably caused by some mental state — perhaps a residual nervous energy associated with intense concentration, perhaps a deeply ingrained habit. But the agent is not aware of it while it occurs. The third condition fails. Yet we should hesitate to classify the drumming with sneezes and knee-jerks. It is a patterned, coordinated, rhythmically complex movement; it is not a mere spasm or twitch. It is the sort of thing that only a living agent — and not a stone or a river — can do. The definition has confused a frequent accompaniment of action — awareness — with a necessary condition, when in truth a great deal of what we do in the ordinary course of life proceeds without our being aware of it.

definition: A mental state M is an emotion if and only if (i) M involves a felt bodily disturbance — an increase in heart rate, a flush of warmth, a tightening of the muscles, or the like, (ii) M is directed at a particular object, person, or state of affairs, and (iii) M disposes the subject to characteristic overt behaviour — flight, approach, aggression, weeping, and so forth.
conditions: M involves a felt bodily disturbance
M is directed at a particular object, person, or state of affairs
M disposes the subject to characteristic overt behaviour
counterexample: Consider a man who experiences a deep, pervasive melancholy that settles upon him gradually over the course of a winter afternoon. He feels a heaviness in his limbs, a dimming of his interest in the things around him, and a vague sadness that colours everything he perceives. Yet his melancholy is not directed at any particular object, person, or state of affairs. He is not sad about anything in particular; he cannot say what has occasioned the feeling, and there is no object upon which it is focused. The second condition fails, yet the melancholy is as much an emotion as any pointed grief or specific fear.
missing_condition: The definition requires that emotions be directed at particular objects, but moods such as melancholy, free-floating anxiety, and generalised elation are undirected emotions that pervade experience without attaching to any specific intentional object.
passage: It might be supposed that a mental state is an emotion if and only if it involves a felt bodily disturbance, is directed at a particular object, person, or state of affairs, and disposes the subject to characteristic overt behaviour. This analysis has the advantage of distinguishing emotions from mere bodily sensations — a pain in one's toe involves a felt disturbance but is not directed at an object in the relevant sense — and from calm judgements, which may be directed at objects but do not involve felt disturbance. A man who sees a snake coiled upon his path experiences an increase in heart rate and a rush of adrenaline; his fear is directed at the snake; and he is disposed to flight. He satisfies all three conditions, and we should not hesitate to classify his state as an emotion. Yet one must consider a rather different kind of case. A man sits by his window on a grey winter afternoon. Gradually, without any precipitating event, a deep melancholy settles upon him. He feels a heaviness in his limbs, a dimming of his interest in the things around him, a vague sadness that colours his perception of the bare trees and the darkening sky. There is a felt bodily disturbance — the heaviness, the fatigue, the constriction in the chest. And there is, perhaps, a behavioural disposition — he is inclined to sit still, to withdraw from company, to let the afternoon pass in silence. But the melancholy is not directed at any particular object, person, or state of affairs. He is not sad about any specific loss, not grieving for any particular person, not distressed by any identifiable circumstance. If one asks him what he is sad about, he can give no answer, for the sadness is not about anything; it pervades his experience without attaching itself to any point. The second condition fails. Yet the melancholy is as genuine an emotion as the sharpest grief or the most specific fear. The definition has taken a feature of what one might call the focused emotions — fear of the snake, anger at the insult, love of the child — and elevated it to a necessary condition of emotion as such, overlooking the entire category of moods, which are felt, which dispose to behaviour, but which float free of any particular intentional object.

definition: A subject S perceives an object O if and only if (i) O exists and is present in S's environment, (ii) S has a sensory experience that is caused, in the normal way, by O, and (iii) S's sensory experience accurately represents O — that is, O has the properties that S's experience attributes to it.
conditions: O exists and is present in S's environment
S has a sensory experience that is caused, in the normal way, by O
S's sensory experience accurately represents O — O has the properties that S's experience attributes to it
counterexample: Consider a man who looks at a white wall upon which a red light is shining. His visual experience represents the wall as red. The wall exists and is present in his environment; his experience is caused, in the normal way, by light reflected from the wall reaching his eyes. But the wall is not red; it is white, and merely appears red because of the illumination. The third condition fails — the experience misrepresents the wall's colour. Yet it seems clear that the man perceives the wall. He sees it, though he sees it under unusual conditions that lead him to misjudge its colour. Perception with inaccuracy is still perception.
missing_condition: The definition requires accurate representation, but perception regularly involves misrepresentation of an object's properties — one sees a white wall as red under unusual lighting — without ceasing to be perception of that object.
passage: One might analyse perception by saying that a subject perceives an object if and only if the object exists and is present in the subject's environment, the subject has a sensory experience that is caused in the normal way by the object, and the subject's experience accurately represents the object — that is, the object has the properties that the experience attributes to it. This analysis is designed to exclude hallucination, in which there is no object; illusion of a non-existent object, in which the experience is not caused by the putative object; and cases of deviant causation, in which the experience is caused by the object but not in the normal way. A man who sees a red pillar-box on a clear day satisfies all three conditions: the pillar-box is there, it causes his visual experience through the ordinary mechanism of reflected light, and his experience accurately represents it as red, cylindrical, and standing on the pavement. Yet consider a man who looks at a white wall upon which a red light is shining. The wall is present in his environment, and his visual experience is caused by the wall in the normal way — light is reflected from the wall's surface, enters his eyes, and produces a visual impression. But his experience represents the wall as red. The wall is not red; it is white. The unusual illumination has caused his experience to misrepresent the wall's colour. The third condition fails; the experience does not accurately represent the object. Yet it would be exceedingly strange to say that the man does not perceive the wall. He sees it; he can point to it; he can walk up to it and touch it. His perception is inaccurate in respect of colour, but it is perception nonetheless. We regularly perceive objects under conditions — fog, dim light, coloured illumination, great distance — that cause our experience to misrepresent their properties, and we do not on that account say that we have failed to perceive them. The definition has confused accurate perception with perception as such, requiring a perfection of representational fidelity that is rarely achieved in the ordinary course of perceptual life.

definition: A subject S remembers event E if and only if (i) S experienced E at some earlier time, (ii) S now has a mental representation of E that is caused by S's original experience of E through a continuous causal chain, and (iii) S's present representation of E is accurate — it preserves the essential features of the original experience.
conditions: S experienced E at some earlier time
S now has a mental representation of E that is caused by S's original experience through a continuous causal chain
S's present representation of E is accurate — it preserves the essential features of the original experience
counterexample: Consider an elderly man who remembers his first day at school. He recalls standing at the gate of a red-brick building, holding his mother's hand, feeling frightened. In fact, the building was of grey stone, not red brick — a detail confirmed by photographs and by his older siblings. His memory has distorted this feature over the decades. He experienced the event, his present mental image is causally connected to the original experience, but his representation is inaccurate in at least one respect. The third condition fails, yet we should not deny that he remembers his first day at school. His memory is imperfect, but it is a memory.
missing_condition: The definition requires accuracy of representation, but memories are routinely distorted by time, subsequent experience, and reconstruction, yet they remain genuine memories provided they are causally connected to the original experience.
passage: It appears reasonable to say that a subject remembers an event if and only if the subject experienced the event at some earlier time, the subject now has a mental representation of the event that is caused by the original experience through a continuous causal chain, and the present representation is accurate — it preserves the essential features of what was originally experienced. This analysis is meant to distinguish genuine memory from confabulation, in which the subject produces a representation that is not caused by any corresponding experience, and from cases of apparent memory that are in fact the product of suggestion or imagination. A man who attended a concert last week and now has a vivid mental image of the orchestra playing, an image that is caused by his perceptual experience at the concert and that faithfully represents what he saw and heard, satisfies all three conditions, and we should say that he remembers the concert. Yet one must consider a more difficult case. An elderly man remembers his first day at school, some sixty years ago. He recalls standing at the gate of a red-brick building, holding his mother's hand, feeling a knot of fear in his stomach. The memory is vivid and detailed. But when his older siblings are consulted, and when a photograph of the school is produced, it emerges that the building was not red brick at all; it was grey stone. The man's memory has, over the course of six decades, altered this feature — perhaps by assimilating the image to later schools he attended, perhaps by the gradual drift that characterises all long-term recollection. He experienced the event; his present representation is causally connected to that experience through the ordinary processes of memory storage and retrieval; but his representation is inaccurate in at least one notable respect. The third condition fails. Yet it would be absurd to deny that the man remembers his first day at school. He remembers it imperfectly — as we all remember the distant past imperfectly — but the imperfection does not annihilate the memory. The definition has demanded a fidelity of representation that human memory simply cannot sustain over long periods, and in doing so it has set a standard that would exclude the greater part of what we ordinarily and rightly call remembering.

definition: A bodily movement M by agent A constitutes a voluntary action if and only if (i) M is preceded by a conscious decision on A's part to perform M, (ii) A has a reason for performing M — that is, M is directed toward some end that A values or desires, and (iii) M is under A's continuous executive control throughout its duration.
conditions: M is preceded by a conscious decision on A's part to perform M
A has a reason for performing M — M is directed toward some end that A values or desires
M is under A's continuous executive control throughout its duration
counterexample: Consider an experienced pianist performing a rapid passage in a Chopin etude. Her fingers move with extraordinary speed and precision across the keys. She decided to play the piece and has a reason — she values the music and wishes to perform it well. But the individual finger movements in the rapid passage are not under her continuous executive control in any meaningful sense. She does not consciously direct each finger to each key; the movements proceed too quickly for that, having been entrusted to motor habit through years of practice. The third condition fails for each individual movement, yet each is a voluntary action — part of a performance she has chosen and shaped through deliberate training.
missing_condition: The definition requires continuous executive control, but skilled actions are largely delegated to trained motor routines, and the absence of moment-by-moment conscious direction does not render them involuntary.
passage: One might propose that a bodily movement constitutes a voluntary action if and only if it is preceded by a conscious decision to perform it, it is directed toward some end that the agent values or desires, and it is under the agent's continuous executive control throughout its duration. The analysis seems initially sound. A man who decides to raise his glass at a dinner, does so in order to propose a toast, and guides the movement of his arm from table to lips satisfies all three conditions, and we should readily call his action voluntary. The definition excludes reflex movements, which are not preceded by decisions; it excludes movements produced by compulsion, where the agent's control is overridden; and it excludes aimless flailing, which is directed toward no end. But consider an experienced pianist performing a rapid passage in a Chopin etude. The passage calls for her fingers to traverse the keyboard at a speed that defies conscious monitoring — thirty-second notes at a tempo that leaves no time for deliberation between one key-stroke and the next. She decided to play the piece; there was a conscious decision at the outset. She has a reason: she values the music and wishes to perform it beautifully. But the individual finger movements in the rapid passage are not under her continuous executive control. She does not consciously direct each finger to each key, any more than a fluent speaker consciously positions his tongue for each phoneme. The movements have been entrusted, through years of practice, to motor habit — to what one might call the body's own intelligence. If she were to attempt continuous executive control of each movement, the performance would collapse; it is precisely the relinquishing of such control that makes the rapid passage possible. Yet no one would say that the pianist's finger movements are involuntary. They are as voluntary as any actions can be — the fruit of deliberate training, performed in the service of an artistic end, and subject to correction if they go awry. The definition has required a mode of control that is appropriate to slow, deliberate actions but not to skilled performance, where voluntary action proceeds through the delegation of executive control to trained sub-personal routines.

definition: A subject S is in a state of fear if and only if (i) S believes that some object or event poses a threat to S's well-being, (ii) S experiences a characteristic felt quality of unease, dread, or alarm, and (iii) S desires to avoid or escape the threatening object or event.
conditions: S believes that some object or event poses a threat to S's well-being
S experiences a characteristic felt quality of unease, dread, or alarm
S desires to avoid or escape the threatening object or event
counterexample: Consider a man standing at the edge of a glass-floored observation deck high above a city. He has been assured, and believes, that the glass is entirely safe — it is thick, reinforced, tested to bear many times his weight. He does not believe that the platform poses any threat to his well-being. Yet he experiences a powerful felt quality of dread, his palms sweat, his legs tremble, and he feels an intense desire to step back from the edge. The first condition fails — he does not believe himself to be in danger — yet his fear is unmistakable.
missing_condition: The definition requires a belief in threat, but fear often occurs in the absence of any such belief, as when one fears heights despite a firm conviction of safety, suggesting that fear has a sub-doxastic or perceptual component that operates independently of what the subject believes.
passage: It might be supposed that a subject is in a state of fear if and only if the subject believes that some object or event poses a threat to his well-being, experiences a characteristic felt quality of unease or dread, and desires to avoid or escape the threatening thing. This analysis captures the paradigm cases tolerably well. A man who sees a bull charging toward him across a field believes himself to be in danger, feels alarm, and desires to escape; we should say without hesitation that he is afraid. The definition also excludes states that resemble fear but are not: a man who believes he is in danger but feels no unease and has no desire to flee may be reckless or foolhardy, but he is not afraid. Yet one must consider a case of a different sort. A man stands upon a glass-floored observation deck cantilevered out from the upper storey of a tall building. He can see the street far below through the transparent floor. He has been assured by the management — and he believes their assurances — that the glass is perfectly safe, having been engineered to bear many times the weight of any number of visitors. He does not believe that the platform poses any threat to his well-being. His reason tells him that he is as safe as if he were standing upon solid ground. Yet he experiences a powerful and unmistakable dread. His palms are damp, his legs are unsteady, his stomach is tight. He feels an intense desire to step back from the edge, to return to the solid floor of the building's interior. The first condition fails: he does not believe himself to be in danger. Yet his fear is as genuine as any fear can be. The definition has assumed that fear is grounded in belief, when in truth fear often operates at a level beneath belief — at the level of perception or instinct — and may persist in direct contradiction to what the subject rationally accepts.

definition: A being X is conscious if and only if (i) X has sensory states that present qualitative features to X, (ii) X is aware of at least some of those sensory states as its own, and (iii) X can distinguish between the presence and absence of such states.
conditions: X has sensory states that present qualitative features to X
X is aware of at least some of those sensory states as its own
X can distinguish between the presence and absence of such states
counterexample: Consider a patient under certain anaesthetic conditions who, upon recovery, reports having felt pain during the operation but having been wholly unable to attend to it or recognise it as her own experience at the time. She possessed sensory states with qualitative character, she could in retrospect distinguish their presence from their absence, yet during the episode she was not aware of those states as belonging to her. One might observe that all three conditions are satisfied only equivocally, for the second condition was met only after the fact, not concurrently with the sensory state.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the awareness specified in condition (ii) obtain concurrently with the sensory states in question; a temporal simultaneity condition is needed to exclude cases of merely retrospective self-attribution.
passage: One might propose that a being X is conscious if and only if X has sensory states that present qualitative features to X, X is aware of at least some of those sensory states as its own, and X can distinguish between the presence and absence of such states. This definition appears, at first blush, to capture the essential features of conscious experience as it is ordinarily understood. The first condition ensures that there is something it is like for the being to be in a given state; the second introduces the element of self-awareness that separates mere sentience from genuine consciousness; and the third demands a capacity for discrimination that rules out beings whose internal states pass without any registering whatever. Yet the definition is not without difficulty. Consider a patient under certain forms of anaesthesia who, upon waking, reports vivid pain experienced during the procedure but declares herself to have been wholly unable to attend to it or to recognise it as her own at the time. The qualitative character was present, the retrospective self-attribution arguably satisfies the second condition, and the patient can certainly distinguish pain from its absence. All three conditions appear to be met, yet we should hesitate to say that the patient was conscious during the operation in the full sense the definition intends. What the definition fails to stipulate is that the awareness of one's own sensory states must be concurrent with those states rather than merely available after the fact.

definition: An agent S intends to perform action A if and only if (i) S has formed a plan that includes A as a step, (ii) S believes that A is within S's power to perform, and (iii) S has not decided to abandon A prior to the occasion for performing it.
conditions: S has formed a plan that includes A as a step
S believes that A is within S's power to perform
S has not decided to abandon A prior to the occasion for performing it
counterexample: Consider a man who plans to post a letter on his way to work and believes himself perfectly able to do so. He has not abandoned the plan. Yet when he reaches the postbox, he walks past it in a fit of absent-mindedness, having entirely forgotten about the letter. He satisfies all three conditions at the moment of passing the postbox, yet it appears wrong to say he intended to post the letter at that moment, for the plan had slipped entirely from his active mental life.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the plan involving A be operative in S's practical reasoning at or near the time of action; mere non-abandonment is insufficient without some degree of active mental engagement with the plan.
passage: It is natural to suppose that an agent S intends to perform an action A if and only if S has formed a plan that includes A as a step, S believes that A is within S's power to perform, and S has not decided to abandon A prior to the occasion for performing it. Such a definition has the merit of connecting intention with planning, competence, and persistence of commitment. The first condition links intention to the broader structure of practical reasoning; the second ensures that the agent is not merely fantasising; and the third rules out cases of explicit retraction. Nevertheless, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account is not difficult to construct. Imagine a man who plans to post a letter on his walk to work and who fully believes himself capable of doing so. He has not at any point decided against posting it. Yet when the moment arrives and he passes the postbox, the plan has so completely faded from his awareness that he walks past without the slightest hesitation. All three conditions are satisfied at the relevant moment, and yet one might observe that it would be peculiar to describe him as having intended to post the letter just then. The difficulty lies in the definition's failure to require that the plan be actively engaged in the agent's practical deliberation at or near the time of action, rather than merely persisting as an unrevoked residue in memory.

definition: A person P at time t2 is the same person as a person Q at time t1 if and only if (i) P has a continuous stream of bodily existence traceable back to Q, (ii) P possesses at least some memories of experiences had by Q, and (iii) P exhibits psychological traits continuous with those of Q.
conditions: P has a continuous stream of bodily existence traceable back to Q
P possesses at least some memories of experiences had by Q
P exhibits psychological traits continuous with those of Q
counterexample: Consider a case of complete retrograde amnesia in which a woman retains her bodily continuity and her fundamental character traits — her temperament, dispositions, and habits of reasoning — yet has lost every memory of her prior life. She satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) but fails condition (ii). Yet it appears deeply counterintuitive to deny that she is the same person, for she is bodily continuous, dispositionally continuous, and recognised by all who know her as the selfsame individual.
missing_condition: The definition errs in treating memory as a necessary condition rather than as one among several sufficient indicators; personal identity may persist through the total loss of episodic memory provided other forms of continuity remain intact.
passage: One familiar proposal holds that a person P at a later time is the same person as a person Q at an earlier time if and only if P has a continuous stream of bodily existence traceable back to Q, P possesses at least some memories of experiences had by Q, and P exhibits psychological traits continuous with those of Q. The appeal of this tripartite definition is considerable. Bodily continuity anchors identity in the physical world; memory provides the subjective thread connecting past and present; and continuity of psychological traits ensures that the person who emerges at the later time is recognisably the same character as the one who existed before. Yet the definition is more restrictive than it ought to be. Consider a woman who suffers a catastrophic injury resulting in complete retrograde amnesia.
