Her body is continuously existent, and her temperament, habits of reasoning, and emotional dispositions remain strikingly unchanged. Her family, her colleagues, and indeed the legal system all treat her as the same person she was before the injury. She satisfies the first and third conditions with perfect clarity but fails the second entirely. If we adhere strictly to the definition, we must conclude that she is not the same person — a conclusion that seems not merely counterintuitive but absurd. The difficulty is that the definition treats memory as a necessary condition of personal identity, whereas it appears to function rather as one possible indicator among others.

definition: An agent S acts freely with respect to action A if and only if (i) S could have done otherwise than A in the circumstances, (ii) A is caused by S's own desires and deliberations, and (iii) no external force compels S to perform A.
conditions: S could have done otherwise than A in the circumstances
A is caused by S's own desires and deliberations
No external force compels S to perform A
counterexample: Consider a man locked in a room who, unaware that the door is locked, decides of his own volition to remain inside and read. He could not have done otherwise, since the door is locked, yet his action of remaining is caused entirely by his own desires and deliberations, and no external force compels him — the lock plays no role in his decision. Condition (i) fails, yet conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied, and it appears entirely natural to say that he remains freely.
missing_condition: The definition's insistence upon the availability of alternative possibilities is too strong; it should be weakened or replaced by a condition requiring merely that the actual causal pathway from deliberation to action be unimpeded by external interference.
passage: A standard account holds that an agent S acts freely with respect to an action A if and only if S could have done otherwise than A in the circumstances, A is caused by S's own desires and deliberations, and no external force compels S to perform A. This definition has the advantage of combining the libertarian intuition that freedom requires open alternatives with the compatibilist insistence that free action must flow from the agent's own mental life. The third condition excludes cases of overt coercion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this tripartite account may be constructed without great difficulty. Imagine a man who is locked inside a room, though he is entirely unaware of this fact. He decides, on the basis of his own inclinations and careful reflection, to remain in the room and continue reading his book. He does not attempt the door. His remaining is caused by his desires and deliberations, and no external force compels him to stay — the lock, being unknown to him, plays no causal role in his decision. Nevertheless, he could not have left the room had he wished to do so. Condition (i) is not satisfied, yet we should hesitate to deny that the man acts freely. He deliberates, he chooses, and his choice issues in action through an entirely unimpeded causal pathway. The definition, it appears, errs in treating the mere availability of alternatives as necessary for freedom, when what matters is rather the character of the actual causal process by which the action is produced.

definition: An event E is an action performed by agent S if and only if (i) E is a bodily movement of S, (ii) E is caused by a mental state of S such as a desire, intention, or decision, and (iii) E is performed voluntarily, that is, not as the result of a reflex or involuntary spasm.
conditions: E is a bodily movement of S
E is caused by a mental state of S such as a desire, intention, or decision
E is performed voluntarily, not as the result of a reflex or involuntary spasm
counterexample: Consider a man who, desiring to signal his friend across the room, raises his arm deliberately. As his arm ascends, it inadvertently knocks a glass from the shelf behind him. The knocking of the glass is a bodily movement of his, it is caused (albeit indirectly) by his desire to signal, and it is not a reflex or spasm. Yet one would not ordinarily describe the knocking of the glass as an action he performed; it is something that happened as a consequence of his action, not an action in its own right.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the mental state which causes the event be directed at that particular event; a condition specifying that the relevant mental state must represent E as its intended or chosen outcome is needed.
passage: It is tempting to define an action as a bodily movement caused by an appropriate mental state and performed voluntarily. More precisely, one might say that an event E is an action performed by agent S if and only if E is a bodily movement of S, E is caused by a mental state of S such as a desire, intention, or decision, and E is performed voluntarily — that is to say, not as the result of a reflex or involuntary spasm. This definition has the merit of distinguishing actions from mere happenings and of connecting them to the mental life of the agent. Yet the account runs into trouble when we consider the full range of bodily movements that may be caused by a mental state. A man who raises his arm deliberately, intending to signal his friend, thereby satisfies all three conditions with respect to the arm-raising. But suppose that as his arm ascends it knocks a glass from a shelf behind him. The knocking of the glass is a bodily movement of his, it is caused by the very desire that caused his arm to rise, and it is not a reflex. All three conditions are met. Yet it appears quite wrong to say that knocking the glass was an action he performed; it was an unintended side-effect. What the definition requires, and lacks, is a condition stipulating that the causative mental state be directed at the event in question — that the desire or intention represent E itself as its object.

definition: A mental state M is an emotion if and only if (i) M involves a felt qualitative character — a way it feels to the subject, (ii) M is directed at an object, situation, or state of affairs, and (iii) M disposes the subject toward characteristic patterns of behaviour.
conditions: M involves a felt qualitative character
M is directed at an object, situation, or state of affairs
M disposes the subject toward characteristic patterns of behaviour
counterexample: Consider a case of what one might call free-floating anxiety, in which a person experiences a pronounced feeling of dread that disposes her toward avoidance behaviour, restlessness, and heightened vigilance, yet the dread is not directed at any particular object or state of affairs. She cannot say what she is anxious about. She satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) but fails condition (ii), and yet it would be perverse to deny that anxiety is an emotion.
missing_condition: The definition's requirement that every emotion be directed at a particular object is too restrictive; it should allow that some emotions may be objectless moods that nonetheless possess the qualitative and behavioural characteristics of genuine emotional states.
passage: One might define an emotion as a mental state that involves a felt qualitative character, is directed at an object or state of affairs, and disposes the subject toward characteristic patterns of behaviour. The first condition ensures that emotions are experiential rather than merely dispositional; the second captures the widely noted intentionality of emotional states — fear is fear of something, anger is anger at someone; and the third connects emotion to the behavioural domain in a manner that permits third-person ascription. This definition has considerable initial plausibility. Yet it encounters a difficulty when confronted with the phenomenon of free-floating anxiety or objectless dread. A person in the grip of such a state experiences a vivid and unmistakable qualitative character — a pervasive unease, a tightening of the chest, a sense of impending misfortune. She is disposed toward avoidance, restlessness, and heightened vigilance. All the hallmarks of emotion are present. But the state is not directed at any object, situation, or state of affairs that the subject can identify, however carefully she reflects upon her condition. If we insist upon the second condition, we must deny that free-floating anxiety is an emotion, which appears to conflict sharply with ordinary usage and with clinical practice alike. The definition, it seems, has made intentional directedness a necessary condition when it is at most a typical feature.

definition: A subject S perceives an object O if and only if (i) O exists in S's environment, (ii) S has a sensory experience that represents O as having certain properties, and (iii) O is causally responsible for S's sensory experience.
conditions: O exists in S's environment
S has a sensory experience that represents O as having certain properties
O is causally responsible for S's sensory experience
counterexample: Consider a man standing before a barn on a misty morning. There is a real barn before him, he has a sensory experience representing a barn with definite shape and colour, and the barn is causally responsible for his visual impression. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the light refracting through the mist causes the barn to appear bright red when it is in fact dull brown. One might argue that he perceives the barn — the object is there, it causes his experience — yet his experience radically misrepresents the barn's properties. The definition says only that the experience represents O as having certain properties, not that these properties correspond to O's actual features.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the properties represented in the sensory experience bear some appropriate correspondence to the actual properties of the object; without a veridicality or accuracy condition, the account counts gross misperceptions as fully satisfactory cases of perception.
passage: A natural definition of perception holds that a subject S perceives an object O if and only if O exists in S's environment, S has a sensory experience that represents O as having certain properties, and O is causally responsible for S's sensory experience. This tripartite account has considerable appeal. The existential condition rules out hallucination of wholly non-existent objects. The representational condition ensures that perception involves a sensory presentation of the object's character. And the causal condition provides the connection between world and mind that distinguishes perception from mere coincidence of experience and reality. Yet the definition is not entirely adequate. Consider a man who stands before a barn on a misty morning. The barn exists, his visual experience represents it as having a definite shape and a vivid red colour, and the barn is causally responsible for his experience — the light reflecting from the barn reaches his eyes through the mist. All three conditions are satisfied without remainder. But the barn is in fact a dull brown; the apparent redness is an artefact of the atmospheric conditions. We should hesitate to say that this is a wholly satisfactory case of perception, for though the man perceives the barn in some attenuated sense, his experience gravely misrepresents its properties. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring that the represented properties bear some appropriate correspondence to the object's actual features.

definition: A subject S remembers an event E if and only if (i) E occurred in the past, (ii) S was a witness to or participant in E, and (iii) S has a present mental representation of E that S takes to be of a past event.
conditions: E occurred in the past
S was a witness to or participant in E
S has a present mental representation of E that S takes to be of a past event
counterexample: Consider a soldier who was present at a battle many years ago. He has a vivid mental representation of charging across a field under heavy fire, which he sincerely takes to be a memory of the battle. However, a careful investigation reveals that the particular episode he represents — the charge across the field — never in fact occurred during the battle he attended; he has confabulated it from films he watched years later, seamlessly integrating it with his genuine experiences. The battle occurred, he was present at it, and he has a mental representation he takes to be of a past event, so all three conditions are met. Yet the specific episode he claims to remember is a fabrication.
missing_condition: The definition requires no appropriate causal connection between the actual past event and the present mental representation; a causal-chain condition linking the original experience to the current representation is needed to exclude confabulated pseudo-memories.
passage: It seems natural to say that a subject S remembers an event E if and only if E occurred in the past, S was a witness to or participant in E, and S has a present mental representation of E that S takes to be of a past event. This definition is attractive because it combines an ontological requirement — the event must have happened — with a biographical one — the subject must have been there — and a phenomenological one — the subject must presently entertain a representation with the right temporal orientation. Yet the definition is susceptible to a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider a soldier who was present at a battle many years ago. He has a vivid mental image of charging across a field under heavy fire, which he takes with complete sincerity to be a memory of the battle. Upon investigation, however, it emerges that no such charge took place during the engagement he attended. The vivid image has been constructed from war films he viewed in the intervening years and has been seamlessly woven into his autobiographical narrative. The event — the battle — occurred; the soldier was a participant in it; and he has a present mental representation that he takes to be of a past event. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should not say that he remembers charging across the field, for no such charge occurred, and his representation is the product of confabulation rather than genuine recollection. The definition omits the requirement of an appropriate causal chain running from the original experience through memory storage to the present representation.

definition: An omission by agent S with respect to action A is a genuine act of refraining if and only if (i) S is aware that A is available as an option, (ii) S possesses the ability to perform A, and (iii) S does not perform A.
conditions: S is aware that A is available as an option
S possesses the ability to perform A
S does not perform A
counterexample: Consider a bystander at the edge of a pond who notices a child struggling in the water. She is aware that swimming out to rescue the child is an available option, she possesses the ability to swim, and she does not perform the rescue. The three conditions are met. Yet suppose she does not rescue the child simply because she is paralysed by shock and is unable to will herself to move. It appears wrong to describe her omission as a genuine act of refraining, for refraining implies a kind of decision or at least a settled non-engagement, whereas she has merely been overtaken by involuntary paralysis.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the agent's non-performance be the outcome of a decision, choice, or at least a deliberate withholding of action; without such a condition, involuntary failures to act are conflated with genuine refraining.
passage: One might define refraining as follows: an omission by agent S with respect to action A is a genuine act of refraining if and only if S is aware that A is available as an option, S possesses the ability to perform A, and S does not perform A. The definition aims to distinguish refraining from mere ignorance or incapacity. The first condition ensures the agent knows the option exists; the second ensures the agent could act; and the third records the simple fact of non-performance. Together, they seem to capture the essential structure of deliberate omission. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account is readily constructed. Consider a bystander at the edge of a pond who sees a child struggling in the water. She is aware that she might swim out to help, and she is a competent swimmer. She does not perform the rescue. All three conditions are satisfied. But suppose her non-performance is not the result of any decision or settled attitude; she is instead wholly paralysed by shock, unable to bring herself to move despite every conscious wish to do so. It would be misleading to describe her as refraining from rescue. Refraining, one might observe, implies at minimum a deliberate withholding — a decision not to act, or at least a composed non-engagement. The definition fails because it does not require that the omission issue from any volitional state of the agent.

definition: A subject S is in a state of grief if and only if (i) S has suffered a significant loss, (ii) S experiences painful affect connected with that loss, and (iii) S's thoughts are frequently occupied by the lost object or person.
conditions: S has suffered a significant loss
S experiences painful affect connected with that loss
S's thoughts are frequently occupied by the lost object or person
counterexample: Consider a woman whose beloved companion animal has died. She experiences no painful affect whatsoever; instead she feels a profound numbness and emotional flatness. Yet her thoughts return constantly to the animal, and she has plainly suffered a significant loss. She satisfies conditions (i) and (iii), but fails condition (ii). Nevertheless, those who observe her recognise her condition as grief — indeed, as a particularly severe form of it. The numbness itself is widely acknowledged in the clinical literature as a characteristic phase of the grieving process.
missing_condition: The definition's requirement of painful affect is too narrow; grief may manifest as emotional numbness or flatness rather than active suffering, and the definition should allow for affective absence as a mode of grieving.
passage: It seems plausible to say that a subject S is in a state of grief if and only if S has suffered a significant loss, S experiences painful affect connected with that loss, and S's thoughts are frequently occupied by the lost object or person. This definition captures three features that are widely associated with grief: the precipitating event, the characteristic suffering, and the cognitive preoccupation with what has been lost. One might take the conjunction of these conditions to be both necessary and sufficient. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty when we consider the phenomenon of what is sometimes called numb grief. Consider a woman whose beloved companion animal has died. She has plainly suffered a significant loss. Her thoughts return to the animal incessantly — she finds herself expecting to hear it at the door, reaching for it in the morning, thinking of it at every quiet moment. Yet she experiences no painful affect. She feels nothing at all: a profound emptiness, a flatness of emotional life that she herself finds disturbing. She satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) with perfect clarity, yet condition (ii) is not met. All the same, anyone acquainted with the varieties of human grieving would recognise her condition as grief, and a particularly deep form of it at that. The emotional numbness is not the absence of grief but one of its most characteristic manifestations. The definition errs in treating painful affect as necessary, when grief may equally express itself through the suspension of feeling altogether.

definition: A creature C is conscious if and only if (i) C is capable of receiving and responding to sensory stimuli from its environment, (ii) C exhibits behaviour that is adaptively modulated by changes in those stimuli, and (iii) C possesses a nervous system of sufficient complexity to integrate information from multiple sensory channels.
conditions: C is capable of receiving and responding to sensory stimuli
C exhibits behaviour adaptively modulated by changes in stimuli
C possesses a nervous system of sufficient complexity to integrate information from multiple sensory channels
counterexample: Consider a Venus flytrap. It receives sensory stimuli — the mechanical pressure of an insect upon its trigger hairs. It responds to those stimuli by closing its lobes, and this response is adaptively modulated: the trap does not close upon a single touch but requires two stimulations within a brief interval, filtering out stimuli unlikely to indicate prey. One might even say that it integrates information from multiple channels, since the two trigger hairs function as independent detectors whose signals must be combined. Yet one would hesitate to say that the Venus flytrap is conscious. Its responses, however adaptive, appear to be purely mechanical, lacking the inner qualitative character that we associate with consciousness.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the creature possess an inner qualitative experience — a felt character to its states — as distinct from merely processing and responding to stimuli in a mechanically adaptive fashion.
passage: One might propose that a creature is conscious if and only if it is capable of receiving and responding to sensory stimuli from its environment, its behaviour is adaptively modulated by changes in those stimuli, and it possesses a nervous system of sufficient complexity to integrate information from multiple sensory channels. The first condition captures the basic receptivity that consciousness seems to require: a creature wholly insensible to its surroundings does not, we should say, enjoy any form of awareness. The second condition adds the requirement of adaptive modulation, ensuring that the creature does not merely receive stimuli but adjusts its behaviour in a manner that serves its interests. The third condition is intended to exclude simple reflex arcs and to require the kind of integrative processing that, in the higher animals, underlies the unity of conscious experience. Yet one might observe that these conditions, though they describe features commonly associated with consciousness, do not capture what is most distinctive about it. Consider the Venus flytrap. It receives sensory stimuli in the form of mechanical pressure upon its trigger hairs. It responds adaptively, closing its lobes only when the stimulation pattern indicates the likely presence of prey. And while it lacks a nervous system in the strict sense, the electrochemical signalling that coordinates its response performs an analogous integrative function. All three conditions are met, or very nearly so. Yet one would hesitate to attribute consciousness to a Venus flytrap. What the definition appears to lack is any reference to the inner qualitative character of the creature's states — the felt experience, the 'what it is like' to undergo them — which distinguishes genuine consciousness from mere mechanical responsiveness, however sophisticated.

definition: An agent A acts with intention if and only if (i) A has formed a prior mental representation of the action to be performed, (ii) A's bodily movements are causally produced by that mental representation, and (iii) the action, once performed, conforms to the content of the mental representation.
conditions: A has formed a prior mental representation of the action
A's bodily movements are causally produced by that representation
The action conforms to the content of the representation
counterexample: Consider a man who intends to shoot a deer. He forms a mental representation of raising his rifle and firing at the deer ahead of him. The representation causes him to become so nervous that his hands tremble, and the trembling causes his finger to pull the trigger prematurely. The bullet, by sheer luck, strikes and kills the deer. The man had formed a prior mental representation of shooting the deer. His bodily movements — the trembling and the pulling of the trigger — were causally produced by that representation, since the nervousness was itself caused by his intention. And the action conforms to the content of the representation: the deer is shot. Yet one would hesitate to say that the man acted intentionally. The causal chain from intention to action passed through an accidental intermediary — the nervous trembling — that was no part of the intended plan.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the causal chain from the mental representation to the bodily movement proceed in the right way — that is, through the agent's rational guidance and motor control rather than through some deviant causal pathway such as nervous trembling or involuntary reflex.
passage: One might suppose that an agent acts with intention if and only if the agent has formed a prior mental representation of the action, the agent's bodily movements are causally produced by that representation, and the completed action conforms to the content of the representation. This definition captures the three elements that seem essential to intentional action: forethought, causal efficacy of the mental state, and correspondence between plan and execution. The requirement of a prior mental representation excludes mere reflexes and involuntary movements; the causal condition ensures that the representation does genuine work in producing the action, rather than being an idle accompaniment; and the conformity condition ensures that the action is the one the agent set out to perform. Yet one might observe that the definition is vulnerable to a class of cases in which the causal connection between representation and action is, in a certain sense, deviant. Consider a man who intends to shoot a deer and forms a detailed mental representation of raising his rifle and firing. The intensity of his intention causes him to become nervous, and the nervousness causes his hands to tremble so badly that the rifle discharges prematurely. By extraordinary luck, the bullet strikes the deer. All three conditions are satisfied: the man had the relevant prior representation, the representation caused his bodily movements via the nervousness it produced, and the action conforms to the content of the representation. Yet it appears wrong to say that the man shot the deer intentionally. The causal chain from intention to execution passed through an accidental intermediary that was no part of the intended mechanism. What the definition lacks is a requirement that the causal chain proceed in the appropriate manner — through the agent's rational control of motor activity, rather than through some deviant route.

definition: A person P at time t1 is the same person as a person Q at time t2 if and only if (i) P and Q share a continuous biological organism — the same living human body persists from t1 to t2, (ii) P and Q share at least some overlapping memories, and (iii) there is a continuous causal chain connecting P's mental states at t1 to Q's mental states at t2.
conditions: P and Q share a continuous biological organism
P and Q share at least some overlapping memories
There is a continuous causal chain connecting P's mental states at t1 to Q's mental states at t2
counterexample: Consider a patient who, following a catastrophic illness, loses all memories of her earlier life. Her body persists continuously; no interruption in biological life occurs. And there is, presumably, a continuous causal chain of neural processes connecting her pre-illness mental states to her post-illness mental states. Conditions (i) and (iii) are met. But condition (ii) is not met, since she retains no overlapping memories with her earlier self. The definition therefore implies that she is not the same person — and yet it would strike most of us as deeply counterintuitive to say that the woman who recovers from the illness is a different person from the woman who fell ill. Her family, her legal identity, and her bodily continuity all point to identity, despite the complete loss of memory.
missing_condition: The definition places too much weight on memory overlap, failing to recognise that personal identity may persist through total memory loss provided that the other conditions — bodily continuity and causal continuity of mental processes — are satisfied. A condition permitting identity to be sustained by bodily and causal continuity alone, in the absence of overlapping memories, is needed.
passage: One might propose that a person at one time is the same person at a later time if and only if the same living body persists from the earlier to the later time, the person at the two times shares at least some overlapping memories, and there is a continuous causal chain connecting the earlier mental states to the later mental states. The bodily continuity condition reflects the commonsense view that a person is, at least in part, a biological organism. The memory condition captures Locke's insight that personal identity is bound up with the capacity to recall one's past. And the causal continuity condition ensures that the later mental states are not merely similar to the earlier ones but are genuinely descended from them through an unbroken causal process. Together, the three conditions appear to provide a robust account of personal identity over time. Yet one might observe that the memory condition creates difficulties in cases of total amnesia. Consider a patient who, following a severe illness, loses all memory of her prior life. Her body persists continuously; the causal chain of neural processes connecting her earlier mental states to her later ones is presumably unbroken; but she possesses no overlapping memories whatsoever. The definition implies that she is not the same person who fell ill. This conclusion seems deeply unsatisfying. Her family recognises her; the law treats her as the same individual; and her bodily and causal continuity are undisturbed. The definition, it appears, has elevated memory to a position of unwarranted necessity, when in fact personal identity may persist through total memory loss, sustained by the remaining conditions of bodily and causal continuity.

definition: An agent A acts freely if and only if (i) A could have done otherwise in the very same circumstances, (ii) A's action is not the result of external compulsion — no force or threat determines the action against A's wishes, and (iii) A deliberates among alternatives before acting.
conditions: A could have done otherwise in the very same circumstances
A's action is not the result of external compulsion
A deliberates among alternatives before acting
counterexample: Consider a man who deliberates at length about whether to donate to a charitable cause. No one compels him; no one threatens him; he weighs the considerations on both sides and finally decides to donate. Unbeknownst to him, a neuroscientist has implanted a device in his brain that would have compelled him to donate had he decided otherwise. Since he decides to donate of his own accord, the device never activates. He could not, in fact, have done otherwise, for the device would have intervened. Yet he deliberated freely and acted without compulsion. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met, but condition (i) is not — and yet the man's action has every appearance of freedom.
missing_condition: The definition requires the ability to do otherwise, but the case reveals that an agent may act freely even when an unactivated backup mechanism renders alternative action impossible. What is needed is a condition focusing on the actual causal history of the action rather than on the mere availability of alternative possibilities.
passage: One might suppose that an agent acts freely if and only if the agent could have done otherwise in the very same circumstances, the action is not the result of external compulsion, and the agent deliberates among alternatives before acting. The first condition expresses the libertarian intuition that freedom requires the genuine availability of alternative courses of action. The second condition excludes cases in which an agent is forced to act against her will — the man who signs a confession under threat of violence, for example, does not act freely. The third condition requires that the agent engage in genuine deliberation, ruling out impulsive or automatic actions that, though uncompelled, do not exhibit the reflective character that we associate with free choice. Together, the three conditions seem to capture the common conception of what it is to act freely. Yet one might observe that the first condition is vulnerable to a class of cases which reveal its insufficiency. Consider a man who deliberates at length about whether to donate to a charitable cause. No force or threat determines his choice; he weighs the reasons carefully and decides to donate. Unbeknownst to him, however, a neuroscientist has implanted a device in his brain that would have compelled him to donate had he decided otherwise. The device never activates, since the man decides to donate on his own. In the strictest sense, he could not have done otherwise — the device would have prevented it. Yet he deliberated freely, acted without compulsion, and his donation was the product of his own reflective judgement. The definition implies that his action was not free, since condition (i) is unsatisfied. But this conclusion seems mistaken: the man's action has all the hallmarks of a free choice. What the definition lacks is the recognition that the freedom of an action may depend on its actual causal history rather than on the mere availability of alternatives that were never in fact pursued.

definition: A bodily movement M constitutes an action if and only if (i) M is caused by a desire of the agent, (ii) the agent believes that M will contribute to satisfying that desire, and (iii) M is a voluntary movement — one that the agent is capable of suppressing at will.
conditions: M is caused by a desire of the agent
The agent believes M will contribute to satisfying that desire
M is a voluntary movement that the agent can suppress at will
counterexample: Consider a man who desires to impress his companions with his calm demeanour during a thunderstorm. He believes that suppressing any flinch when lightning strikes will contribute to satisfying this desire. When lightning flashes, he manages — just barely — not to flinch. His not-flinching is caused by his desire to appear calm; he believes it contributes to satisfying that desire; and it is something he can control. All three conditions are met. Yet his not-flinching is an omission, not a bodily movement. The definition, by requiring a bodily movement, excludes a whole class of cases — deliberate refrainings and omissions — that we should wish to count as actions.
missing_condition: The definition restricts actions to bodily movements, but deliberate omissions and refrainings — cases where the agent intentionally refrains from moving — are also actions in the relevant sense. A broader condition encompassing both movements and deliberate abstentions from movement is needed.
passage: One might propose that a bodily movement constitutes an action if and only if it is caused by a desire of the agent, the agent believes the movement will contribute to satisfying that desire, and the movement is voluntary in the sense that the agent could suppress it at will. This definition reflects the familiar belief-desire model of action: an action is a bodily movement produced by the right combination of motivational and cognitive states. The desire provides the motivational force; the belief provides the cognitive direction; and the voluntariness condition ensures that the movement is under the agent's control rather than being a mere reflex or spasm. Yet one might observe that by defining actions as bodily movements, the definition excludes a significant class of cases that we should wish to count as actions. Consider a man who desires to impress his companions with his composure during a thunderstorm and believes that suppressing any visible flinch when lightning strikes will serve this end. When lightning flashes, he deliberately refrains from flinching. His refraining satisfies the spirit of all three conditions: it is caused by a desire, guided by a belief about what will satisfy that desire, and is under his voluntary control. But his refraining is not a bodily movement — it is the deliberate absence of one. The definition, by requiring a movement, excludes deliberate omissions and refrainings from the category of action. This is too restrictive. When a sentry deliberately refrains from raising the alarm, or a witness deliberately remains silent, these are actions in any sense that matters for the purposes of moral and legal assessment. The definition, it appears, has mistaken one species of action — bodily movement — for the genus.

definition: A mental state S is an emotion if and only if (i) S involves a distinctive felt quality — a characteristic phenomenal character experienced by the subject, (ii) S is directed at an object or state of affairs — it is about something, and (iii) S has a characteristic behavioural expression that is recognisable by others.
conditions: S involves a distinctive felt quality
S is directed at an object or state of affairs
S has a characteristic behavioural expression recognisable by others
counterexample: Consider the case of a diplomat who feels intense anger at a foreign minister's insult during a negotiation. His anger involves a distinctive felt quality — a hot, roiling sensation. It is directed at a specific object — the insult and the person who delivered it. Yet years of professional training have rendered him capable of suppressing every outward sign of anger; his face remains impassive, his voice steady, his gestures composed. No observer, however attentive, could detect any behavioural expression of his anger. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but condition (iii) is not. Yet it would be absurd to say that the diplomat is not experiencing anger. He is experiencing it acutely; he has merely learnt to conceal it.
missing_condition: The definition requires a recognisable behavioural expression, but emotions can be genuinely present even when their behavioural expression is entirely suppressed. A condition permitting the identification of emotions by their phenomenal and intentional characteristics alone, without requiring overt behavioural manifestation, is needed.
passage: One might define an emotion as a mental state that involves a distinctive felt quality, is directed at an object or state of affairs, and has a characteristic behavioural expression recognisable by others. The first condition captures the phenomenal dimension of emotion — the fact that fear feels different from joy, and anger from sadness, in a way that is immediately apparent to the subject. The second condition captures the intentional dimension — the fact that emotions are typically about something: one fears the approaching storm, one is angry at the betrayal. The third condition is meant to connect the inner life of the subject with the public world, reflecting the fact that emotions are typically visible in facial expression, tone of voice, and bodily posture. Together, these conditions seem to provide a tolerably complete account of what an emotion is. Yet one might observe that the third condition is too demanding. Consider a diplomat who, during a tense negotiation, feels intense anger at a deliberate provocation. His anger has a vivid phenomenal character — a hot, roiling sensation in his chest. It is directed at a specific object — the insult and the person who delivered it. But years of professional discipline have taught him to suppress every outward sign of his anger. His face betrays nothing; his voice is perfectly controlled; his gestures are composed and deliberate. No observer, however skilled, could detect any behavioural expression of anger. The definition implies that he is not experiencing an emotion, since condition (iii) is unsatisfied. But this conclusion is plainly wrong. The diplomat is experiencing anger as intensely as anyone has ever experienced it; he has merely learnt to prevent its expression. The definition, it appears, has confused a typical accompaniment of emotion with a necessary condition of it.

definition: A subject S perceives an object O if and only if (i) O exists in S's environment, (ii) S has a sensory experience whose content represents O, and (iii) the sensory experience is caused by O through the normal operation of S's sensory organs.
conditions: O exists in S's environment
S has a sensory experience representing O
The experience is caused by O through normal sensory operation
counterexample: Consider a man walking through a desert who sees, on the horizon, what appears to be an oasis. Suppose there really is an oasis at the location where he sees one — but the visual experience is not caused by the oasis itself. It is caused by a mirage: the refraction of light from the sky creates the appearance of water. The real oasis, which happens to lie just behind the mirage, plays no role in producing his visual experience. The oasis exists in his environment; his experience represents an oasis; but the experience is not caused by the oasis through the normal operation of his visual system. All three conditions are almost met — the first two are satisfied, but the third is not satisfied with respect to the real oasis. Yet the man has a veridical experience of something that is genuinely there.
missing_condition: The definition does not adequately address cases where the object exists and the experience is veridical but the causal connection between object and experience runs through an abnormal intermediary. A condition requiring that the causal connection be of an appropriate kind — not merely that the object cause the experience, but that it do so in a way that constitutes genuine perceptual contact — is needed.
passage: One might propose that a subject perceives an object if and only if the object exists in the subject's environment, the subject has a sensory experience whose content represents the object, and the experience is caused by the object through the normal operation of the subject's sensory organs. The first condition excludes hallucination of objects that do not exist. The second ensures that the subject has a genuine sensory experience, not merely a thought or belief about the object. The third connects the experience to the object by an appropriate causal chain, excluding cases in which the subject's experience, though veridical, is produced by something other than the represented object. These three conditions together constitute a causal theory of perception that has considerable initial plausibility. Yet one might observe that the causal condition admits of difficulties. Consider a man walking through a desert who, on the distant horizon, sees what appears to be an oasis. Suppose — by an extraordinary coincidence — that a real oasis exists at precisely the location where the man sees one. But his visual experience is not caused by the oasis. It is produced by a mirage: the refraction of light from the sky creates the appearance of water on the horizon. The real oasis, hidden behind the shimmer, contributes nothing to the causal production of his experience. The first condition is met — the oasis exists. The second condition is met — his experience represents an oasis at that location. But the third condition fails: the experience is caused by a mirage, not by the oasis. The definition thus denies that the man perceives the oasis, even though his experience is veridical and the oasis is precisely where he sees it. What the definition lacks is a more refined account of the causal connection — one that addresses cases in which the right object exists and the experience is accurate, yet the causal pathway is abnormal.

definition: A subject S remembers an event E if and only if (i) E occurred in the past, (ii) S has a present mental representation that accurately represents E, and (iii) S's present representation is causally connected, through a continuous chain of memory traces, to S's original experience of E.
conditions: E occurred in the past
S has a present mental representation that accurately represents E
S's representation is causally connected through continuous memory traces to the original experience
counterexample: Consider a woman who experienced a particular event in her childhood — say, a family gathering — but has entirely forgotten it. Years later, her mother describes the event to her in vivid detail, and on the basis of this description the woman forms a mental image of the gathering that accurately represents how it occurred. She comes to believe that she remembers the event, and indeed her representation is accurate. The event occurred; her representation is faithful to it. But the causal chain runs not from her original experience through a continuous series of memory traces, but through her mother's testimony. Condition (iii) is not met. Yet from the inside, her mental state is indistinguishable from genuine memory.
missing_condition: The definition does not address cases of accurate representation derived from testimony rather than from memory traces. A condition distinguishing the specific causal pathway of genuine memory — retention of the original experience — from other routes to accurate representation, such as testimony or inference, would clarify the boundary between remembering and merely knowing about the past.
passage: One might define memory as follows: a subject remembers an event if and only if the event occurred, the subject has a present mental representation that accurately represents the event, and the representation is causally connected through a continuous chain of memory traces to the subject's original experience. The first condition ensures that one cannot remember events that never happened. The second ensures accuracy: a distorted or wholly inaccurate representation does not constitute memory, however causally connected it may be to the original experience. The third condition provides the crucial link to genuine remembering: it is not enough that the subject's representation be accurate; it must be accurate because it descends, through an unbroken causal chain of retention, from the original experience. This condition is intended to distinguish memory from other sources of accurate representation, such as inference or testimony. Yet one might observe that the boundary the definition draws is not so clean as it appears. Consider a woman who attended a family gathering as a child but has entirely forgotten it. Years later, her mother recounts the event in vivid detail. On the basis of this narrative, the woman forms a mental image of the gathering that faithfully represents how it occurred. The event happened; her representation is accurate; but the causal chain from event to representation passes not through memory traces but through her mother's testimony. The definition correctly denies that this is memory. But from the woman's own perspective, her mental state is indistinguishable from a genuine memory. She has a vivid, accurate image of a past event and sincerely believes she is remembering it. The definition succeeds in excluding the case but offers no account of why testimony-based representation so closely resembles memory, nor does it provide a criterion that the subject herself could apply to distinguish the two.

definition: A mental state M is an act of imagination if and only if (i) M involves a mental image or representation that does not correspond to any presently perceived object, (ii) M is under the voluntary control of the subject — the subject can initiate, modify, or terminate it at will, and (iii) the subject is aware that the content of M does not represent the actual state of the world.
conditions: M involves a mental image not corresponding to any presently perceived object
M is under the voluntary control of the subject
The subject is aware that the content of M does not represent the actual state of the world
counterexample: Consider a novelist who, while walking through a familiar street, involuntarily experiences a vivid mental image of one of her characters walking beside her. The image does not correspond to any perceived object; the character is fictional. And the novelist is aware that the image does not represent the actual world. But the image is not under her voluntary control — it arises spontaneously and persists despite her attempts to dismiss it. Condition (ii) is not met. Yet the mental state is recognisably an act of imagination: it is creative, representational, and concerned with a fictional content. The definition, by requiring voluntary control, excludes the involuntary imagination that is so characteristic of artistic and literary creation.
missing_condition: The definition requires voluntary control over the imaginative act, but many of the most characteristic acts of imagination — the unbidden images of the novelist, the spontaneous daydream, the sudden flash of creative insight — arise involuntarily. A condition that permits involuntary as well as voluntary imagination is needed.
passage: One might propose that a mental state is an act of imagination if and only if it involves a mental image that does not correspond to any presently perceived object, is under the voluntary control of the subject, and is accompanied by the subject's awareness that it does not represent the actual state of the world. The first condition distinguishes imagination from perception: in perception the image corresponds to a present object, whereas in imagination it does not. The second condition distinguishes imagination from hallucination and dreaming, where images arise beyond the subject's control. The third condition ensures that the subject does not mistake her imagining for reality, as a deluded person might. The three conditions together seem to capture the essential features of the imaginative act. Yet one might observe that the requirement of voluntary control is too restrictive. Consider a novelist who, whilst walking through a familiar street, finds that a vivid image of one of her characters arises unbidden in her mind. The character appears to walk beside her; she can see his face, hear his voice, observe his mannerisms. The image does not correspond to any perceived object; it is wholly fictional. And the novelist is well aware that she is imagining, not perceiving. But the image is not under her voluntary control. It arose spontaneously, and it persists despite her efforts to dismiss it. The definition implies that this is not imagination, since condition (ii) fails. But surely it is imagination of the most vivid and characteristic kind — the kind that has produced the great works of literature and art. The definition has confused the capacity to imagine at will, which is one form the imagination may take, with imagination as such.

definition: A subject S understands a proposition P if and only if (i) S can correctly state P in her own words, (ii) S can draw at least some correct inferences from P, and (iii) S can provide an example or illustration of the state of affairs described by P.
conditions: S can correctly state P in her own words
S can draw at least some correct inferences from P
S can provide an example or illustration of P
counterexample: Consider a student of mathematics who can state Gödel's incompleteness theorem in her own words, draw certain correct inferences from it (for instance, that no consistent formal system of sufficient power can prove its own consistency), and provide a standard textbook example illustrating the theorem. She satisfies all three conditions. Yet further questioning reveals that she has no grasp of why the theorem is true. She has memorised the statement, learnt the standard inferences by rote, and recalled the textbook example. Her capacity is entirely reproductive; she cannot apply the theorem to a novel case, explain the proof strategy, or recognise situations in which the theorem is relevant. One would hesitate to say that she understands the incompleteness theorem; she merely knows about it.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the subject grasp the reasons or grounds underlying the proposition — the capacity to explain why it holds, to apply it in novel contexts, and to recognise its relevance beyond memorised examples. Understanding, it appears, requires not merely the capacity to reproduce information but the capacity to deploy it flexibly.
passage: One might suppose that a subject understands a proposition if and only if she can correctly state it in her own words, draw at least some correct inferences from it, and provide an example or illustration of the state of affairs it describes. The first condition ensures that the subject has not merely memorised a formula but can express the proposition's content independently. The second condition tests inferential competence: someone who understands a proposition can reason from it to its consequences. The third condition connects the abstract proposition to the concrete world, ensuring that the subject can recognise what the proposition amounts to in practice. These three conditions seem to capture the common notion of understanding tolerably well. Yet one might observe that they can be satisfied by a subject who lacks genuine understanding. Consider a diligent student of mathematics who can state Gödel's incompleteness theorem in her own words, draw certain standard inferences from it, and provide the textbook example. All three conditions are met. But further inquiry reveals that her competence is entirely derivative: she memorised the paraphrase from a textbook, learnt the inferences as routine exercises, and recalled the example from a lecture. She cannot explain why the theorem holds, cannot apply it to a case she has not previously encountered, and cannot recognise situations in which it is relevant unless they closely resemble the textbook example. One would hesitate to say that she understands the theorem. She has acquired a set of verbal and inferential dispositions that mimic understanding without possessing the genuine article. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring the subject to grasp the grounds or reasons underlying the proposition — the capacity to explain, to apply in novel contexts, and to recognise relevance beyond rehearsed cases.

definition: A creature C is conscious if and only if (i) C exhibits discriminative responses to stimuli in its environment, (ii) C is capable of reporting its own internal states when suitably prompted, and (iii) C modifies its behaviour in light of past experience.
conditions: C exhibits discriminative responses to stimuli in its environment
C is capable of reporting its own internal states when suitably prompted
C modifies its behaviour in light of past experience
counterexample: Consider a thermostat connected to a speech synthesiser that has been programmed to announce 'I am too warm' when the ambient temperature exceeds a threshold. The thermostat discriminates between temperatures, reports an internal state, and modifies its behaviour by activating the cooling system. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one would hesitate to say the thermostat is conscious; there is, as it were, nothing it is like to be the thermostat.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that C possess a subjective, qualitative dimension of experience — that there is something it is like, from the creature's own perspective, to be in the states it reports.
passage: One might propose that a creature is conscious if and only if it exhibits discriminative responses to stimuli, is capable of reporting its own internal states, and modifies its behaviour in light of past experience. This proposal has much to recommend it: it excludes mere reflexive systems by requiring reportability, it captures the adaptive character of conscious organisms, and it provides behavioural criteria that are, in principle, publicly observable. Yet the proposal admits of a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider a thermostat fitted with a simple speech synthesiser. The device discriminates between temperatures — it responds differently to warmth and cold. When the temperature exceeds a programmed threshold, the synthesiser announces 'I am too warm,' thereby reporting an internal state. And the thermostat modifies its behaviour in light of past readings, adjusting its activation cycles according to previous patterns. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet it would be absurd to attribute consciousness to a thermostat. What is missing is a requirement that the creature possess a subjective, qualitative character to its experience — that there is something it is like, from the creature's own perspective, to occupy the states it reports. Without this requirement, the definition confuses the behavioural indicators of consciousness with consciousness itself.

definition: An agent A intends to perform an action X if and only if (i) A has a representation of X as a future state of affairs to be brought about, (ii) A believes that X is within A's power to perform, and (iii) A has not formed a conflicting intention that would preclude X.
conditions: A has a representation of X as a future state of affairs to be brought about
A believes X is within A's power to perform
A has not formed a conflicting intention that would preclude X
counterexample: Consider a man who represents to himself the prospect of climbing Mont Blanc next summer, believes it is within his power to do so, and has formed no conflicting intention. He entertains the idea with pleasure and tells his friends he might do it. Yet he has never resolved to climb Mont Blanc; the prospect remains a mere wish or aspiration rather than a settled intention. All three conditions are met, but the man does not intend to climb Mont Blanc.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that A have formed a settled resolution or commitment to bring about X, as opposed to merely entertaining X as an attractive possibility.
passage: It is natural to analyse the concept of intention by requiring that the agent represent a future action, believe it to be within his power, and harbour no conflicting intention. This analysis appears to capture the essential features of intending: the forward-looking character of intention, the requirement that the intended action be believed feasible, and the consistency of the agent's practical attitudes. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a man represents to himself the prospect of climbing Mont Blanc next summer. He believes it is within his power — he is fit, has the time, and can afford the journey. He has formed no conflicting intention for the same period. He speaks of the climb with evident enthusiasm and tells his friends he might do it. All three conditions are met. Yet it would be quite wrong to say that he intends to climb Mont Blanc. The prospect remains a pleasing fancy, an aspiration he has not yet resolved to pursue. There is a difference between entertaining a possibility with favour and committing oneself to its realisation. What is missing from the definition is the element of settled resolution or commitment — the practical determination that transforms a mere wish into a genuine intention.

definition: A person P at time t2 is the same person as a person Q at time t1 if and only if (i) P has a continuous bodily existence traceable from Q, (ii) P shares psychological characteristics — memories, dispositions, temperament — with Q, and (iii) P occupies a single unbroken causal chain extending from Q.
conditions: P has a continuous bodily existence traceable from Q
P shares psychological characteristics with Q
P occupies a single unbroken causal chain extending from Q
counterexample: Consider a man who suffers a catastrophic brain injury that obliterates all his memories, alters his temperament entirely, and destroys his former dispositions. His body persists without interruption; the causal chain connecting his earlier self to his later self is unbroken. The first and third conditions are met. But the second condition is not — he shares no psychological characteristics with his former self. Yet the law, his family, and common sense all regard him as the same person. The definition would force us to deny his identity, which appears absurd.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that bodily continuity alone may be sufficient for personal identity in cases where psychological continuity has been destroyed, suggesting the conditions are too conjunctive.
passage: One might propose that personal identity over time requires continuous bodily existence, shared psychological characteristics, and an unbroken causal chain. This proposal is attractive in its comprehensiveness, for it combines the bodily criterion favoured by materialists with the psychological criterion favoured by those in the empiricist tradition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this conjunctive analysis is readily at hand. Consider a man who suffers a catastrophic injury to the brain — a severe trauma that obliterates every memory, transforms his temperament, and extinguishes his former habits and dispositions. He awakens as, psychologically speaking, a stranger to his former self. His body, however, persists without interruption, and the causal chain linking his present state to his past is unbroken. The first and third conditions are satisfied, but the second is not — he shares no psychological characteristics with the person he was. Yet it seems plainly wrong to say that a new person has come into existence. His wife recognises him; the hospital records identify him; the law holds him responsible for his former debts. The definition, by requiring the conjunction of all three conditions, denies identity where identity plainly obtains. What is wanted is a more flexible analysis that permits bodily continuity alone to ground identity when psychological continuity is absent.

definition: An agent A acts freely with respect to an action X if and only if (i) A could have done otherwise than X, (ii) A's performance of X was not the result of external compulsion, and (iii) A deliberated about whether to perform X before performing it.
conditions: A could have done otherwise than X
A's performance of X was not the result of external compulsion
A deliberated about whether to perform X
counterexample: Consider a mother who, seeing her child stumble into traffic, rushes into the road to pull the child to safety. She was not externally compelled; she could, in some notional sense, have remained on the pavement. But she did not deliberate — the action was immediate, unreflective, and spontaneous. The third condition is not met. Yet we should be most reluctant to say she did not act freely; indeed, one might argue that her action expressed her deepest values with a directness that deliberation would only have attenuated.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that freely willed action may proceed from deeply held values or settled character without the mediation of conscious deliberation, and thus that the absence of deliberation does not vitiate freedom.
passage: A plausible analysis of free action might require that the agent could have done otherwise, was not externally compelled, and deliberated before acting. This analysis captures much of what we ordinarily mean by free will: it excludes actions performed under duress, it insists upon the availability of alternative courses, and it demands the involvement of rational reflection. Yet there is a case which reveals its insufficiency. Suppose a mother is walking with her young child along a busy road. The child stumbles and falls into the path of an oncoming vehicle. Without a moment's hesitation, without weighing alternatives or calculating consequences, the mother darts into the road and pulls the child to safety. She was not externally compelled — no one forced her hand. And she could, in some abstract sense, have remained on the pavement. But she did not deliberate. The action was immediate, instinctive, and unreflective. The third condition of the definition is not satisfied. Yet it would be deeply counterintuitive to deny that she acted freely. Her action expressed her character, her values, and her love with a directness that deliberation might only have impeded. What the definition overlooks is that free action may spring from settled character and deeply held commitments without the intervening step of conscious deliberation.

definition: A bodily movement M counts as an action if and only if (i) M is caused by a mental state of the agent — a desire, intention, or volition, (ii) M is under the voluntary control of the agent, and (iii) M is directed toward some end or goal.
conditions: M is caused by a mental state of the agent
M is under the voluntary control of the agent
M is directed toward some end or goal
counterexample: Consider a man who, while asleep, rises from his bed, walks to the kitchen, opens the refrigerator, and consumes a quantity of food before returning to bed. The sleepwalker's movements are caused by mental states — neural processes that generate motor commands — and are directed toward a goal, namely the procurement of food. One might even say they are under a rudimentary form of control, since the sleepwalker navigates obstacles. Yet we should hesitate to call sleepwalking an action in the full sense; the agent is not aware of what he is doing and would disclaim responsibility.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the agent be aware of what he is doing at the time of the movement, such that the causal connection between mental state and bodily movement passes through conscious awareness.
passage: One might analyse the concept of action by requiring that a bodily movement be caused by a mental state, be under voluntary control, and be directed toward some end. This analysis appears to capture the paradigmatic cases of action — reaching for a glass, signing a document, walking to the shops — in which the agent's desires or intentions cause purposive, controlled movements. Yet the analysis admits of a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider a man who walks in his sleep. He rises, navigates the corridor, opens the refrigerator, selects food, eats, and returns to bed. His movements are caused by mental states — the neural processes that issue in motor commands. They are directed toward an end — obtaining and consuming food. And they exhibit a form of voluntary control, inasmuch as the sleepwalker avoids obstacles and adjusts his movements to the environment. All three conditions appear to be met, at least in a minimal sense. Yet it would be strange to regard sleepwalking as genuine action. The man is not aware of what he is doing; he will have no memory of it; he would repudiate the description of his conduct as something he did. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring that the agent be conscious of his movements at the time they occur, so that the causal link between mental state and bodily movement is mediated by awareness.

definition: A mental state S of an agent A is an emotion if and only if (i) S involves a characteristic bodily feeling — a felt perturbation or agitation, (ii) S is directed toward an intentional object, and (iii) S disposes A to act in certain characteristic ways.
conditions: S involves a characteristic bodily feeling
S is directed toward an intentional object
S disposes A to act in characteristic ways
counterexample: Consider a seasoned soldier who has been in combat many times. He reports that he feels fear in battle — he recognises the situation as dangerous, his behaviour is cautious and alert, and he would describe himself as afraid. Yet he reports no felt bodily agitation; years of exposure have extinguished the trembling, the racing heart, the churning stomach. His fear is directed at the enemy, it disposes him to take cover and return fire, but it no longer involves a characteristic bodily feeling. The first condition is not met, yet it seems wrong to deny that he experiences fear.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that an emotion may persist in the absence of felt bodily perturbation, as when habituation or training attenuates the somatic component while leaving the evaluative and dispositional components intact.
passage: A natural analysis of emotion requires a characteristic bodily feeling, directedness toward an intentional object, and a disposition to act in certain ways. This analysis captures many paradigmatic cases: the pounding heart and trembling hands of the frightened man, directed at a perceived threat, disposing him to flee; the warmth and expansion of joy, directed at a welcome event, disposing one to smile and share one's happiness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider the emotions of those who have undergone extensive habituation. Consider a seasoned soldier who has spent years in combat. He reports that he experiences fear in battle — he judges the situation to be dangerous, his behaviour is appropriately cautious, and he would without hesitation describe himself as afraid. But he no longer experiences the bodily agitation that once accompanied his fear. The trembling, the racing heart, the nausea — all have been extinguished by repeated exposure. His fear is directed toward the enemy and disposes him to take cover and return fire, but the felt bodily component has faded away. The first condition of the definition is not met. Yet to deny that the soldier fears seems wrong in the extreme. What the definition overlooks is that the somatic component of emotion may be attenuated by habituation without the emotion itself disappearing, provided the evaluative and dispositional components remain.

definition: An agent A perceives an object O if and only if (i) O exists in A's environment, (ii) O causally affects A's sense organs in an appropriate manner, and (iii) A forms a sensory representation that corresponds to O's actual properties.
conditions: O exists in A's environment
O causally affects A's sense organs appropriately
A forms a sensory representation corresponding to O's actual properties
counterexample: Consider a man who looks at a white wall upon which a red light is being projected. He sees the wall as red. The wall exists and causally affects his sense organs. His sensory representation — of a red surface — does not correspond to the wall's actual colour, which is white. The third condition is not met. Yet it seems wrong to say the man does not perceive the wall; he perceives it, but perceives it as having a colour it does not possess. Perception under misleading conditions is still perception.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate cases of perception under non-standard conditions, where the agent perceives a genuine object but forms a representation that misattributes some of its properties, and thus conflates perceiving an object with perceiving it veridically.
passage: One might analyse perception by requiring that the perceived object exist, that it causally affect the agent's sense organs, and that the agent form a sensory representation corresponding to the object's actual properties. This analysis captures the paradigmatic case of veridical perception admirably: when one sees a red apple in good light, the apple exists, it causally stimulates one's retinae, and one's visual experience corresponds to its actual colour and shape. Yet the analysis admits of a case which reveals its insufficiency. Suppose a man looks at a white wall upon which a red light is being projected by a concealed lamp. The wall exists in his environment and causally affects his visual system in the normal manner — photons reflect from its surface and stimulate his retinae. But his sensory representation is of a red surface, which does not correspond to the wall's actual property of being white. The third condition is not met. Yet it would be peculiar to deny that the man perceives the wall. He perceives it — he is visually aware of a surface before him, he can point to it, he would walk around it rather than through it. What he fails to do is perceive it veridically. The definition conflates two things that ought to be kept apart: the perception of an object and the veridicality of that perception. A more adequate analysis would allow that perception may occur even when the representation misattributes some of the object's properties.

definition: An agent A remembers an event E if and only if (i) E occurred in the past, (ii) A was a witness to or participant in E, and (iii) A's present representation of E is causally connected to A's original experience of E.
conditions: E occurred in the past
A was a witness to or participant in E
A's present representation of E is causally connected to A's original experience
counterexample: Consider a man who participated in a childhood event of which he has utterly no recollection. Years later, he reads a vivid description of the event in his mother's diary. He forms a detailed representation of the event that happens to be accurate. The event occurred, he participated in it, and one might argue that a causal chain connects his present representation to his original experience — after all, his mother's account was itself caused by the same event. Yet the causal connection is deviant: it runs through the diary rather than through his own memory system.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the causal chain from original experience to present representation run through the agent's own cognitive-retentive processes rather than through external intermediaries.
passage: A natural analysis of memory holds that an agent remembers an event if the event occurred, the agent witnessed it, and a causal connection links the agent's present representation to the original experience. This analysis is attractive in its simplicity and captures many standard cases: I remember yesterday's lecture because the lecture occurred, I attended it, and my present recollection is causally sustained by the traces laid down during the experience. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is near at hand. Suppose a man was present at a family gathering in his early childhood but has completely forgotten it — no trace of the event remains in his memory. Decades later, he discovers a detailed account of the gathering in his mother's diary. Reading it, he forms a vivid and accurate representation of the event. The event occurred; he was a participant; and a causal chain of sorts connects his present representation to the original event, since the diary entry was itself caused by the gathering he attended. All three conditions are formally met. Yet it would be wrong to say he remembers the event. His representation derives from the diary, not from his own retentive capacities. What the definition overlooks is the requirement that the causal chain run through the agent's own cognitive system — through the processes of encoding, storage, and retrieval that constitute memory in the proper sense — rather than through some external intermediary.

definition: A mental state S of an agent A is an act of imagination if and only if (i) S represents a state of affairs that is not presently perceived, (ii) S is under the voluntary control of A, and (iii) A does not take S to represent an actual state of affairs.
conditions: S represents a state of affairs not presently perceived
S is under the voluntary control of A
A does not take S to represent an actual state of affairs
counterexample: Consider a man who, upon being told that a close friend has died, involuntarily pictures the friend's face and imagines scenes from their shared past. The mental imagery represents states of affairs not presently perceived, and the man does not take the images to represent his current surroundings. But the imagery is not under his voluntary control — it arises unbidden and persists despite his efforts to dismiss it. The second condition is not satisfied, yet we should not hesitate to describe this as an act of imagination, albeit an involuntary one.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate involuntary imagination — cases in which imagery arises spontaneously and irresistibly, yet possesses all the phenomenal and representational characteristics of imagination proper.
passage: One might analyse imagination as a mental state that represents a state of affairs not presently perceived, is under the agent's voluntary control, and is not taken to represent actuality. This analysis appears to distinguish imagination from perception (which represents what is presently before the senses), from belief (which is taken to represent the actual), and from passive mental events (which are not under voluntary control). It captures the paradigmatic cases of creative reverie — the novelist who constructs scenes at will, the child who invents an imaginary companion. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a man receives the news that a close friend has died. At once, and entirely against his will, he is assailed by vivid mental images: the friend's face, scenes from their years together, imagined versions of the friend's last moments. These images represent states of affairs not presently perceived. The man does not confuse them with present reality. But they are not under his voluntary control — they arise unbidden and resist his efforts to banish them. The second condition of the definition is not met. Yet it would be odd to deny that the man is imagining. His mental states have precisely the representational and phenomenal character of imagination; they differ from voluntary imagination only in their aetiology. What the definition overlooks is that imagination may be involuntary and still be imagination.

definition: An agent A believes that p if and only if (i) A assents to p when the question whether p is put to A, (ii) A is disposed to act in ways that would be appropriate if p were true, and (iii) A's assent to p is not the result of pretence or insincerity.
conditions: A assents to p when the question is put
A is disposed to act as though p were true
A's assent is not the result of pretence or insincerity
counterexample: Consider a man who sincerely assents to the proposition that aeroplanes are safe when asked, and who is disposed to purchase tickets and arrive at the airport. Yet when the moment comes to board the aircraft, he is seized by terror and cannot bring himself to step onto the plane. His verbal assent is sincere; his prior behaviour is consistent with the belief that flying is safe; yet his conduct at the critical moment suggests a deep-seated conviction that it is not. The definition, which requires only dispositions and sincere assent, cannot accommodate this divided state.
