missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent's dispositions be consistent across the full range of contexts in which p is practically relevant, rather than merely in those contexts where compliance is easy or costless.
passage: One might propose that an agent believes a proposition if he sincerely assents to it and is disposed to act in ways appropriate to its truth. This proposal captures many ordinary cases of belief: the man who assents to the proposition that it will rain and carries an umbrella; the woman who assents to the proposition that the bridge is sound and walks across it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises in connexion with what one might call divided or fragmented assent. Consider a man who, when asked, sincerely affirms that aeroplanes are safe modes of transport. He purchases tickets without hesitation, packs his bags, and arrives at the airport in good time. All his behaviour up to this point is consistent with his professed belief. But when the moment comes to board the aircraft, he is overcome by terror. He cannot bring himself to pass through the door. His feet will not carry him forward. His verbal assent was sincere, and his prior behaviour was appropriate to the truth of the proposition. Yet his conduct at the decisive moment reveals a disposition quite at odds with his professed belief. The definition, which looks only for sincere assent and appropriate dispositions in general, cannot account for this fragmentation. What is needed is a requirement that the agent's dispositions be consistent across the full range of circumstances in which the proposition is practically relevant, not merely in those where the cost of acting on it is low.

definition: A mental state M is conscious if and only if (i) the subject is aware of being in M, (ii) M has a qualitative character — there is something it is like for the subject to be in M, and (iii) M is available for verbal report by the subject.
conditions: The subject is aware of being in M
M has a qualitative character — there is something it is like for the subject to be in M
M is available for verbal report by the subject
counterexample: Consider a patient who, following damage to the primary visual cortex, sincerely reports that she cannot see anything in a portion of her visual field. Yet when forced to guess the location or orientation of objects presented in that region, she performs well above chance. There is a mental state that guides her behaviour and that has some qualitative character — she responds differentially to colour and motion — but she is not aware of being in it and cannot voluntarily report upon it. The state is conscious by condition (ii) but fails conditions (i) and (iii), yet it is not merely a reflex: it exhibits sensitivity to features that are characteristically the province of conscious perception.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the possibility of dissociations between awareness, qualitative character, and reportability, and thus cannot accommodate states that possess some but not all of these features in a theoretically principled manner.
passage: One might propose that a mental state is conscious if and only if the subject is aware of being in it, it has a qualitative character, and it is available for verbal report. This proposal has the merit of combining three features that are widely associated with consciousness: self-awareness, phenomenal quality, and reportability. In the central cases — seeing a vivid red, feeling a sharp pain, hearing a trumpet — all three features are present simultaneously and without tension. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the phenomenon sometimes called blindsight, observed in patients with damage to the primary visual cortex. Such a patient sincerely reports that she sees nothing in the affected region of her visual field. She is not aware of any visual state; she cannot voluntarily report upon it. Yet when compelled to guess whether a stimulus has been presented, and to indicate its position, orientation, or colour, she performs well above chance. Her responses are sensitive to features — shape, motion, chromatic contrast — that are characteristically the province of conscious visual perception. It appears that a mental state is guiding her behaviour that possesses some qualitative character, inasmuch as it discriminates among perceptual features, but of which she has no awareness and which she cannot report. The definition requires all three conditions to be jointly satisfied, but this case suggests that the features may dissociate, and that a state may possess phenomenal sensitivity without self-awareness or reportability. One might observe that consciousness, rather than being a unitary property, may admit of degrees and dimensions that no conjunctive definition can adequately capture.

definition: A subject S perceives an object O if and only if (i) O exists in S's environment, (ii) S has a sensory experience that represents O, and (iii) S's sensory experience is caused by O in the appropriate manner.
conditions: O exists in S's environment
S has a sensory experience that represents O
S's sensory experience is caused by O in the appropriate manner
counterexample: Consider a man who stands before a white wall upon which a powerful projector casts the image of a red square. He has a sensory experience as of a red square; the red square, qua projected image, exists in his environment; and his experience is caused by the projected image in the standard causal manner — light from the projector strikes the wall and is reflected into his eyes. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to say that he perceives a red square in the sense in which he perceives the wall itself. The projected image is not a physical object but an artefact of the projection; perceiving it is not perceiving a thing but perceiving an appearance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that O be a mind-independent physical object rather than an artefact produced by the arrangement of light, shadow, or other medium, and thus cannot distinguish perception of things from perception of mere appearances.
passage: It appears natural to propose that a subject perceives an object when the object exists in the subject's environment, the subject has a sensory experience representing it, and the experience is caused by the object in the appropriate manner. This formulation captures the core of perception in the paradigmatic cases: one sees a tree because the tree is there, one has a visual experience of it, and the tree causes that experience through the normal operation of light and the visual system. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed with the aid of a powerful projector. A man stands before a blank white wall upon which a projector casts an image of a red square. He has a sensory experience as of a red square before him. The red square — understood as the projected image — exists in his environment in the sense that it occupies a definite region of the wall. His experience is caused by the projected image in the ordinary causal manner: light from the projector strikes the wall and is reflected into his eyes. All three conditions of the definition are met. But one might observe that the man does not perceive a red square in the robust sense in which he perceives the wall behind it. The wall is a physical object; the red square is an artefact of the projection, a pattern of light that vanishes the moment the projector is switched off. The definition, by requiring only that the object exist in the environment and cause the experience appropriately, cannot distinguish between perceiving a genuine physical object and perceiving a mere appearance. One might conclude that the definition requires a further condition specifying that the perceived object be a mind-independent physical entity rather than an artefact of some intervening medium.

definition: A mental state M is an emotion if and only if (i) M involves a felt bodily disturbance — a change in heart rate, muscular tension, visceral sensation, or the like, (ii) M is directed towards an object or situation that the subject takes to be its cause, and (iii) M disposes the subject towards characteristic behaviour — approach, avoidance, aggression, and so forth.
conditions: M involves a felt bodily disturbance
M is directed towards an object or situation taken to be its cause
M disposes the subject towards characteristic behaviour
counterexample: Consider a man who learns, upon reading a letter, that a distant relative whom he never met has left him a modest inheritance. He forms a calm judgement that this is good fortune and is disposed to write a letter of thanks, but he experiences no felt bodily disturbance — no quickening of the pulse, no warmth, no visceral sensation. He would describe himself as pleased, and his behaviour confirms this: he smiles faintly, speaks well of the deceased relative, and proceeds to accept the bequest. His state is directed towards an object, and it disposes him to characteristic behaviour, yet it lacks any felt bodily component. Common usage would class his state as an emotion — mild gratitude or quiet pleasure — but the definition excludes it.
missing_condition: The definition requires a felt bodily disturbance in every case of emotion, but mild or dispositional emotions may lack any salient bodily phenomenology while still constituting genuine emotional states.
passage: One might propose that a mental state is an emotion if and only if it involves a felt bodily disturbance, is directed towards an object taken to be its cause, and disposes the subject towards characteristic behaviour. This definition has the advantage of combining three features that are conspicuous in the paradigmatic emotions: fear involves trembling and a racing heart directed towards a perceived threat, and it disposes one to flee; anger involves muscular tension and heat directed towards an offending party, and it disposes one to aggression. The three conditions work in concert to identify these states as emotions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises with the mild, dispositional emotions that pervade ordinary life. Consider a man who learns from a solicitor's letter that a distant relative whom he never knew has bequeathed him a modest sum. He judges calmly that this is agreeable news. He is disposed to write a courteous letter of acknowledgement, to speak favourably of the deceased, and to accept the bequest. But he experiences no felt bodily disturbance — no quickening of the pulse, no flush of warmth, no visceral flutter. His state is directed towards an object and disposes him to characteristic behaviour, but it lacks the bodily phenomenology that the definition requires. Common usage would not hesitate to call his state an emotion — a mild gratitude or quiet pleasure — yet the definition excludes it. One might observe that the definition has been constructed from the dramatic emotions and does not accommodate the cooler, more dispositional states that are no less genuinely emotional for their lack of bodily tumult.

definition: A mental state M is an act of imagination if and only if (i) M involves the formation of a mental image that is not derived from current sensory input, (ii) M is under the voluntary control of the subject, and (iii) M does not purport to represent an actual state of affairs.
conditions: M involves the formation of a mental image not derived from current sensory input
M is under the voluntary control of the subject
M does not purport to represent an actual state of affairs
counterexample: Consider a novelist who sets herself to imagine the drawing room of a Victorian house in order to describe it accurately for her readers. She forms a vivid mental image of heavy curtains, dark furniture, and a coal fire. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied: she forms images not derived from current perception, and she does so voluntarily. But condition (iii) fails, for her imagining does purport to represent an actual state of affairs — she is attempting to imagine what Victorian drawing rooms were actually like, and she would revise her image if shown evidence that it was historically inaccurate. Her state is plainly an act of imagination, yet it violates the third condition.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly assumes that imagination never purports to represent actuality, but a significant class of imaginative acts — historical reconstruction, architectural visualisation, empathic imagining — are directed towards accuracy and would be revised in the light of evidence.
passage: It appears reasonable to define an act of imagination as a mental state involving the formation of a mental image not derived from current sensory input, under the voluntary control of the subject, and not purporting to represent an actual state of affairs. This definition captures the imaginative acts most commonly discussed in philosophical psychology: daydreaming, fantastical invention, the construction of fictional scenarios. In these cases, one forms images voluntarily, the images are not drawn from present perception, and one does not take them to depict reality. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the historical novelist who deliberately imagines a Victorian drawing room. She closes her eyes and forms a detailed mental image: heavy velvet curtains, a dark mahogany sideboard, a coal fire in a cast-iron grate. The image is not derived from current sensory input — she is sitting at a modern desk — and it is under her voluntary control. But her imagining does purport to represent an actual state of affairs. She is not inventing a fantasy; she is attempting to reconstruct what a certain kind of room actually looked like. If a historian were to inform her that Victorian drawing rooms of the period in question typically had gas lighting rather than coal fires, she would revise her image accordingly. Her imaginative act is answerable to the facts in a way that the definition does not permit. One might observe that a considerable range of imaginative activity — historical reconstruction, architectural visualisation, the empathic imagining of another person's situation — is precisely directed towards representing actuality, and that the definition, by excluding such cases, confuses one species of imagination with the genus.

definition: A subject S remembers an event E if and only if (i) S previously experienced E, (ii) S now has a mental representation of E that is causally connected to the original experience, and (iii) S's present representation of E is accurate — it represents E as it actually occurred.
conditions: S previously experienced E
S now has a mental representation causally connected to the original experience
S's present representation is accurate
counterexample: Consider a woman who attended a concert twenty years ago at which a celebrated pianist performed a particular sonata. She has a vivid recollection of the event: the hall, the pianist's posture, the particular sonata performed. Her representation is causally connected to the original experience through the normal processes of memory retention. However, the pianist actually performed a different sonata that evening, and her memory has been contaminated by a later conversation in which someone mentioned the wrong piece. She would insist that she remembers the concert, and in an important sense she does — she experienced the event, and her representation is causally linked to it. Yet her representation is inaccurate in a significant detail. The definition excludes her state from the category of memory, but common usage and psychological theory would both recognise it as a genuine, if partially distorted, memory.
missing_condition: The definition requires perfect accuracy, but genuine memories are characteristically imperfect, and a representation may constitute a memory even when it contains errors, provided the errors are incidental rather than wholesale.
passage: One might propose that a subject remembers an event if and only if the subject previously experienced it, now has a mental representation causally connected to the original experience, and the representation is accurate. This definition captures what we might call the ideal case of memory: the subject experienced the event, retains a trace of it, and the trace faithfully represents what occurred. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is readily constructed from the ordinary imperfections of human recollection. Consider a woman who attended a concert twenty years ago at which a celebrated pianist performed. She has a vivid and detailed recollection: the grand hall with its gilded balconies, the pianist's characteristic posture, and the particular sonata that was performed — let us say it was Mozart's Sonata in A major. Her representation is causally connected to the original experience through the ordinary processes of memory consolidation and retention. She satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) without difficulty. But the pianist actually performed a different sonata that evening — Beethoven's Waldstein, as the programme confirms — and her memory of the piece has been contaminated by a conversation, some years after the event, in which a friend mentioned the Mozart sonata in a related context. Her representation is inaccurate in a significant particular. The definition therefore excludes her state from the category of memory. Yet she would insist, and we should be inclined to agree, that she remembers the concert. Her memory is imperfect, but it is genuinely a memory: it is causally derived from the experience, it preserves the setting and the occasion, and the error is confined to one detail. One might observe that perfect accuracy is not a condition of genuine memory but an idealisation, and that the definition mistakes the ideal for a necessary condition.

definition: A mental state M is a desire if and only if (i) M represents a state of affairs that does not currently obtain, (ii) M motivates the subject to act so as to bring about that state of affairs, and (iii) the subject would experience satisfaction if the state of affairs were to obtain.
conditions: M represents a state of affairs not currently obtaining
M motivates action to bring about that state of affairs
The subject would experience satisfaction upon the state of affairs obtaining
counterexample: Consider a reformed smoker who, upon smelling tobacco, experiences a powerful craving for a cigarette. The craving represents a state of affairs that does not currently obtain — his smoking a cigarette — and it motivates him, insofar as he feels a strong pull towards purchasing a packet. But he would not experience satisfaction if he were to smoke; he knows from past relapses that he would feel disgust and self-reproach. The craving satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) but fails condition (iii). Yet it would be perverse to deny that the craving is a desire. It is precisely the kind of state — urgent, motivating, directed at a definite object — that the concept of desire is meant to capture.
missing_condition: The definition requires that fulfilment bring satisfaction, but compulsive and addictive desires may motivate action towards outcomes that the subject expects to find aversive, and the definition cannot accommodate these cases.
passage: It appears natural to define a desire as a mental state that represents a state of affairs not currently obtaining, motivates action to bring it about, and is such that the subject would experience satisfaction upon its realisation. The paradigmatic desires — a thirsty man's desire for water, a lonely person's desire for companionship — conform to this pattern: the desired state of affairs is absent, the subject is moved to act, and attainment brings satisfaction. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises with compulsive desires, of which addiction provides the most striking instance. Consider a reformed smoker who has abstained for several years. Upon entering a room where someone is smoking, he experiences a powerful craving. The craving represents a state of affairs that does not obtain — his smoking a cigarette — and it motivates him powerfully: he feels a nearly irresistible urge to go to the nearest shop and buy a packet. Conditions (i) and (ii) are amply satisfied. But the man knows, from the experience of past relapses, that smoking would not bring satisfaction. He would feel disgust at the taste, shame at his weakness, and anxiety about his health. The craving is directed at an outcome that the subject himself expects to find aversive. Condition (iii) fails. Yet it would be perverse to deny that the craving is a desire; indeed, it is a desire of considerable intensity and motivational force. One might observe that the definition has been constructed upon the model of appetitive desires that aim at pleasure, and that it cannot accommodate the compulsive desires whose characteristic feature is precisely that fulfilment brings not satisfaction but regret.

definition: A person P at time t1 is identical with a person Q at a later time t2 if and only if (i) P and Q have the same continuous body, (ii) P and Q share a continuous stream of consciousness, and (iii) Q possesses memories of P's experiences.
conditions: P and Q have the same continuous body
P and Q share a continuous stream of consciousness
Q possesses memories of P's experiences
counterexample: Consider a man who undergoes a severe epileptic episode that renders him unconscious for several days and, upon waking, has no memory of the preceding two years of his life. His body is continuous — he is the same biological organism throughout. But his stream of consciousness was interrupted by the period of unconsciousness, and he lacks memories of P's recent experiences. Conditions (ii) and (iii) fail. Yet no one — neither his family, nor the law, nor the man himself upon reflection — would deny that he is the same person. He has lost memories and suffered an interruption of consciousness, but he has not become a different person.
missing_condition: The definition requires unbroken continuity of consciousness and memory, but personal identity can survive interruptions in both, provided the underlying psychological and biological continuities are preserved in sufficient degree.
passage: One might define personal identity over time by requiring that the person at the later time have the same continuous body as the person at the earlier time, share a continuous stream of consciousness, and possess memories of the earlier person's experiences. This definition combines the bodily and psychological criteria that have dominated philosophical discussion and has the advantage of requiring both: it is not satisfied by mere bodily continuity without psychological connection, nor by psychological connection without bodily continuity. In the central cases, it yields the correct verdicts: a man who goes about his daily life from one year to the next has the same body, an unbroken stream of consciousness (save for the familiar interruptions of sleep, which we set aside as benign), and memories of his past experiences. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is provided by the phenomenon of severe amnesia following a neurological crisis. Consider a man who suffers a prolonged epileptic episode that leaves him unconscious for several days. Upon waking, he has no memory of the preceding two years. His stream of consciousness was interrupted — not merely by sleep but by a prolonged period of complete unconsciousness. His memories of the recent past are gone. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are violated. Yet his wife recognises him, the law holds him to his contracts, and the man himself, upon reflection, does not doubt that he is the person who signed those contracts and made those promises. The definition is too stringent: it makes personal identity hostage to the contingencies of memory and consciousness, when what matters is a broader pattern of continuity — biological, dispositional, characterological — that can survive local interruptions. One might observe that the definition mistakes the normal accompaniments of identity for its necessary conditions.

definition: A subject S intends to perform an action A if and only if (i) S has formed a plan to perform A, (ii) S believes that A is within S's power to perform, and (iii) S's plan to perform A is the product of S's deliberation about what to do.
conditions: S has formed a plan to perform A
S believes A is within S's power
S's plan is the product of deliberation
counterexample: Consider a man who, while walking along a familiar route, suddenly turns left at a junction and enters a bookshop. He did not deliberate about whether to enter the bookshop; the decision arose spontaneously, prompted perhaps by a glimpse of a book in the window. He has a plan — he is entering the shop to look at the book — and he believes the action is within his power. But condition (iii) fails: the intention was not the product of deliberation but of a spontaneous impulse that he immediately endorsed. Yet it would be wrong to say that he entered the bookshop unintentionally. He intended to go in; his intention was simply not formed through deliberation.
missing_condition: The definition requires that intentions arise from deliberation, but many genuine intentions arise spontaneously — from habit, impulse, or sudden recognition — and are no less intentional for lacking a deliberative history.
passage: It appears reasonable to propose that a subject intends to perform an action if and only if the subject has formed a plan, believes the action to be within his power, and the plan is a product of deliberation. This definition captures the structure of the paradigmatic cases of intention: one deliberates about whether to accept a position, forms a plan to do so, and believes the acceptance is within one's power. The three conditions work together to distinguish intention from mere wish (which lacks a plan), from idle fantasy (which lacks the belief in one's power), and from compulsion (which lacks deliberation). Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the many intentions that arise without deliberation. A man walks along a familiar street, and his eye is caught by a book displayed in the window of a shop he is passing. Without pausing to deliberate, he turns and enters the shop. He has a plan — to examine the book — and he believes the action to be within his power. But the plan was not formed through deliberation; it arose spontaneously from a sudden interest, and he endorsed it immediately without weighing alternatives. Condition (iii) is not satisfied. Yet his entry into the shop is plainly intentional: he meant to go in, he went in on purpose, and if asked why he entered he would cite the book in the window as his reason. One might observe that a great many of our daily intentions — to pick up a dropped pen, to answer a question, to step aside for a fellow pedestrian — arise without any deliberation whatever, and that the definition, by insisting on deliberation, excludes the spontaneous intentions that constitute the bulk of intentional action.

definition: A sensation S is a pain if and only if (i) S is experienced as unpleasant by the subject, (ii) S is located in a determinate region of the subject's body, and (iii) S motivates the subject to seek the cessation of S.
conditions: S is experienced as unpleasant
S is located in a determinate bodily region
S motivates the subject to seek its cessation
counterexample: Consider the phenomenon of referred pain, in which a patient with a diseased gall bladder experiences a sharp pain in the right shoulder. The pain is experienced as unpleasant and motivates the patient to seek its cessation. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. But condition (ii) is satisfied only in a misleading sense: the pain is located in the shoulder, yet the pathology is in the abdomen. The pain is located in a determinate bodily region, but the wrong one — the location of the sensation does not correspond to the location of the disorder. The definition counts this as a pain in the shoulder, but in a medically and philosophically important sense the pain belongs to the gall bladder, not the shoulder. The definition cannot accommodate the distinction between felt location and source.
missing_condition: The definition locates pain where it is felt but does not require that the felt location correspond to the site of bodily disturbance, and thus cannot distinguish veridical pain localisation from referred pain, in which the felt location is systematically misleading.
passage: One might propose that a sensation is a pain if and only if it is experienced as unpleasant, located in a determinate bodily region, and motivates the subject to seek its cessation. This definition captures the central features of the paradigmatic pains: a burn on the hand is felt as unpleasant in the hand and prompts one to withdraw the hand. The three conditions cooperate to distinguish pain from other unpleasant states — nausea, for instance, is unpleasant but is not always located in a determinate region, and a loud noise is unpleasant but is not experienced as bodily. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the well-documented phenomenon of referred pain. A patient with inflammation of the gall bladder may experience a sharp, insistent pain in the right shoulder. The pain is experienced as unpleasant — intensely so — and the patient is motivated to seek its relief. It is located in a determinate bodily region, namely the shoulder. All three conditions are met, and the definition classifies it as a pain, which it certainly is. But the definition treats the shoulder as the relevant locus of the pain, when in fact the pathological source is the gall bladder. The felt location of the pain does not correspond to the site of the bodily disturbance that produces it. A physician who treated the shoulder rather than the gall bladder would be treating the wrong organ. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only that pain be located somewhere in the body, fails to distinguish between pains whose felt location accurately indicates the site of disturbance and pains whose felt location is systematically misleading. This distinction is of both medical and philosophical importance, and the definition is silent upon it.

definition: A subject S attends to an object O if and only if (i) S is perceptually aware of O, (ii) S selectively focuses on O to the relative exclusion of other objects in the perceptual field, and (iii) S's focusing on O is under voluntary control.
conditions: S is perceptually aware of O
S selectively focuses on O to the exclusion of other objects
S's focusing is under voluntary control
counterexample: Consider a man at a crowded dinner party who is engaged in conversation with his neighbour. He is not voluntarily attending to the conversation at the far end of the table. Yet when someone at that distant conversation mentions his name, he immediately becomes aware of it — his attention is captured involuntarily. Prior to the capture, he was not perceptually focused on the distant conversation, and the capture was not under his voluntary control. His attention was seized, not directed. Condition (iii) fails, yet the phenomenon is universally recognised as a case of attention — indeed, it is one of the most studied phenomena in the psychology of attention.
missing_condition: The definition requires voluntary control, but a significant class of attentional phenomena — involuntary capture by salient or personally relevant stimuli — are characterised precisely by the absence of voluntary control, and the definition cannot accommodate them.
passage: It appears natural to define attention as a state in which the subject is perceptually aware of an object, selectively focuses on it to the relative exclusion of other objects, and does so under voluntary control. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases — a reader attending to a difficult passage, a birdwatcher scanning the hedgerow for a particular warbler — in which the subject deliberately directs her focus. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the phenomenon of involuntary attentional capture, of which the most familiar instance occurs at noisy social gatherings. A man is engaged in conversation with his dinner companion and is not voluntarily attending to any other conversation in the room. The babble of the other guests is a mere background noise. Then someone at the far end of the table mentions his name. His attention is immediately seized: he becomes aware of the distant conversation, its content, and the speaker, all without any voluntary act of focusing. Prior to the capture, he was not selectively focused on the distant conversation — it was part of the undifferentiated background. The capture itself was involuntary — he did not choose to attend to the distant voice; it commandeered his attention. Condition (iii) is not met. Yet the phenomenon is universally recognised as a case of attention; indeed, it is one of the most extensively studied phenomena in experimental psychology. One might observe that attention is not always a voluntary act of the subject but is sometimes an involuntary response to the salience of the stimulus, and that a definition which insists upon voluntary control captures only one species of attention while excluding another that is equally genuine.

definition: A mental state S constitutes an act of attention if and only if (i) S is directed toward a particular object or feature in the subject's experiential field, (ii) S involves an increase in the clarity or vividness of the subject's awareness of that object or feature, and (iii) S is under the voluntary control of the subject.
conditions: S is directed toward a particular object or feature in the subject's experiential field
S involves an increase in the clarity or vividness of the subject's awareness of that object or feature
S is under the voluntary control of the subject
counterexample: Consider a man walking quietly through a forest who is startled by the sudden crack of a breaking branch. His awareness is immediately and vividly directed toward the source of the sound; the sound fills his consciousness with a clarity that his previous auditory experience of birdsong and wind did not possess. Yet this redirection of awareness is wholly involuntary — the man did not choose to attend to the sound, and indeed could not have prevented the sudden seizure of his consciousness by the noise. The definition, requiring voluntary control, would exclude this paradigmatic case of involuntary attention.
passage: One might propose that an act of attention consists in a mental state directed toward a particular object or feature in the experiential field, involving an increase in the clarity of the subject's awareness of that object, and falling under the subject's voluntary control. This proposal captures a good deal of what we ordinarily mean by attention: when one deliberately concentrates upon a passage of music, one's awareness of that passage becomes vivid and one exercises a species of voluntary control over one's mental focus. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the proposal arises when we consider involuntary or reflex attention. A man walking through a forest is startled by the sudden crack of a breaking branch. His awareness is immediately and vividly directed toward the source of the sound; the auditory experience possesses a clarity that his prior experience of ambient noise did not. But this redirection of awareness is entirely involuntary. The man did not choose to attend to the sound, nor could he have prevented the sudden capture of his consciousness by so startling a stimulus. One might observe that this is a perfectly good instance of attention — we should not hesitate to say that the man attended to the sound — and yet it fails the third condition of the proposed definition. The difficulty suggests that the definition has conflated one species of attention, namely the voluntary, with the genus as a whole.

definition: A creature C is conscious at time t if and only if (i) C is responsive to stimuli from its environment at t, (ii) C exhibits discriminative behaviour with respect to those stimuli at t, and (iii) C is capable of modifying its behaviour in light of past experience at t.
conditions: C is responsive to stimuli from its environment at t
C exhibits discriminative behaviour with respect to those stimuli at t
C is capable of modifying its behaviour in light of past experience at t
counterexample: Consider a thermostat connected to a heating system. The thermostat is responsive to stimuli from its environment, namely changes in temperature. It exhibits discriminative behaviour, switching the heating on when the temperature falls below a threshold and off when it rises above. And if fitted with a simple learning algorithm, it may modify its behaviour in light of past patterns. Yet we should hesitate to say that the thermostat is conscious. Its responsiveness, discrimination, and adaptability appear to be merely mechanical operations devoid of any inner experiential character.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the creature's responsiveness involve subjective experience — that there be something it is like for the creature to undergo its responses — as opposed to merely mechanical or functional processing.
passage: One might define consciousness as a state in which a creature is responsive to environmental stimuli, exhibits discriminative behaviour with respect to those stimuli, and is capable of modifying its behaviour in light of past experience. This definition has the advantage of being anchored in observable behaviour and of avoiding appeal to obscure metaphysical notions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers a thermostat of the more sophisticated variety. Such a device is responsive to temperature changes in its environment. It discriminates between different temperature ranges, activating or deactivating the heating system accordingly. And if equipped with a simple adaptive mechanism, it may modify its responses in light of past temperature patterns. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it appears plainly false that the thermostat is conscious. One might observe that what is missing is any suggestion that there is something it is like for the thermostat to register changes in temperature — that its internal states possess an experiential or phenomenal quality. The definition, by confining itself to behavioural responsiveness and adaptability, has omitted the very feature that we most centrally associate with consciousness.

definition: A mental state D is a desire if and only if (i) D represents some state of affairs as to be brought about, (ii) D disposes the subject to act in ways conducive to the realisation of that state of affairs, and (iii) D is accompanied by a felt sense of lack or longing in the subject.
conditions: D represents some state of affairs as to be brought about
D disposes the subject to act in ways conducive to the realisation of that state of affairs
D is accompanied by a felt sense of lack or longing in the subject
counterexample: Consider a man who desires that his estranged daughter should flourish, but who has long since accepted that he will play no part in her life. He represents her flourishing as a state of affairs to be brought about. He is disposed to act on her behalf should the occasion ever arise. Yet he experiences no felt sense of lack or longing — he has made his peace with the situation and feels only a quiet, settled wish for her welfare. We should not deny that this is a genuine desire merely because it lacks the phenomenological character of longing.
passage: It is tempting to define desire as a mental state that represents some state of affairs as to be brought about, that disposes the subject to act in ways conducive to its realisation, and that is accompanied by a felt sense of lack or longing. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases well: a thirsty man's desire for water involves a representation of drinking as desirable, a disposition to seek water, and a palpable feeling of thirst. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition arises when we consider what might be called a settled or tranquil desire. A man who desires that his estranged daughter should flourish represents her flourishing as something to be brought about; he is disposed, should occasion arise, to act on her behalf. But he has long since made his peace with their separation and experiences no felt sense of lack or longing. His desire is genuine — we should not wish to deny that he desires her welfare — yet it lacks the phenomenological accompaniment that the third condition requires. One might observe that the definition has taken a feature that is often present in desire, namely the subjective feeling of longing, and elevated it to a necessary condition. The error lies in confusing what is characteristic of a mental state with what is essential to it.

definition: A psychological state E is an emotion if and only if (i) E involves a characteristic bodily feeling or sensation, (ii) E is directed toward an intentional object — some person, event, or state of affairs, and (iii) E is of relatively brief duration, arising in response to a precipitating event.
conditions: E involves a characteristic bodily feeling or sensation
E is directed toward an intentional object — some person, event, or state of affairs
E is of relatively brief duration, arising in response to a precipitating event
counterexample: Consider a woman who has for many years felt a quiet but persistent resentment toward a colleague who was promoted in her place. Her resentment is directed at the colleague and the circumstances of the promotion; it involves a characteristic, if mild, bodily tension when she thinks of the matter. Yet it is not of brief duration — it has persisted for years without any fresh precipitating event. We should not hesitate to call this resentment an emotion, and yet it fails the third condition.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly requires that emotions be brief and tied to a precipitating event, failing to accommodate the class of enduring or dispositional emotions that persist without continual re-elicitation.
passage: One might propose that an emotion is a psychological state that involves a characteristic bodily feeling, is directed toward an intentional object, and is of relatively brief duration arising in response to a precipitating event. This definition captures the everyday cases admirably: a sudden fright upon encountering a snake involves bodily sensations of alarm, is directed at the snake, and arises briefly in response to the encounter. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the proposal concerns enduring emotions. Consider a woman who has felt a quiet but persistent resentment toward a colleague who was promoted in her place some years ago. Her resentment is directed at the colleague and at the circumstances of the promotion; it involves a characteristic, if subdued, bodily tension when the matter comes to mind. Yet it has persisted for years without any fresh precipitating event — it is not a brief episode but a settled feature of her affective life. One might observe that resentment of this kind is as much an emotion as sudden fright, and that a definition which excludes it by requiring brevity and a precipitating event has mistaken the features of one species of emotion — the acute, reactive kind — for the necessary conditions of the genus.

definition: A mental process M is an act of imagination if and only if (i) M produces or sustains a mental image — a quasi-perceptual representation in the absence of the relevant external stimulus, (ii) the subject is aware that the content of M does not correspond to present reality, and (iii) M is initiated and sustained by the subject's voluntary effort.
conditions: M produces or sustains a mental image — a quasi-perceptual representation in the absence of the relevant external stimulus
The subject is aware that the content of M does not correspond to present reality
M is initiated and sustained by the subject's voluntary effort
counterexample: Consider a novelist who, whilst walking through a crowded market, finds unbidden images of a fictional scene forming in her mind — characters speaking, a room taking shape, a plot unfolding — without any voluntary effort on her part. She is aware that the content does not correspond to present reality, and the process produces vivid quasi-perceptual representations. Yet the images arise spontaneously, not through voluntary effort. We should not deny that this is imagination; indeed, writers frequently report that their most creative imaginative work occurs in precisely this involuntary fashion.
passage: It is natural to define imagination as a mental process that produces a quasi-perceptual representation in the absence of the relevant external stimulus, that is accompanied by the subject's awareness that the content does not correspond to present reality, and that is initiated and sustained by voluntary effort. This definition captures the deliberate exercise of imagination well: when one closes one's eyes and pictures a mountain landscape, one produces a visual image voluntarily and with full awareness that no mountain is present. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises when one considers the spontaneous imagination of creative artists. A novelist walking through a crowded market finds that images of a fictional scene form unbidden in her mind — characters speak, a room takes shape, a plot unfolds — without any voluntary effort on her part. She is aware that the content does not correspond to present reality, and the process produces vivid quasi-perceptual representations. All conditions save the third are met. Yet one might observe that this spontaneous imaginative activity is not merely an instance of imagination but is often regarded as its most characteristic and valuable form. The definition, by requiring voluntary effort, would exclude the very cases that most clearly exemplify the creative power of the imaginative faculty.

definition: A mental state I is an intention to perform an action A if and only if (i) I represents A as an action to be performed by the subject, (ii) I includes a commitment or resolution on the part of the subject to perform A, and (iii) I is causally efficacious in bringing about A.
conditions: I represents A as an action to be performed by the subject
I includes a commitment or resolution on the part of the subject to perform A
I is causally efficacious in bringing about A
counterexample: Consider a man who intends to post a letter on his way to work. He represents the posting of the letter as an action to be performed; he is firmly committed to posting it. Yet, absorbed in thought during his walk, he passes the postbox and arrives at work with the letter still in his pocket. His intention was never causally efficacious in bringing about the posting, for the posting never occurred. We should not, however, deny that he had an intention to post the letter. The third condition confuses the successful execution of an intention with the intention itself.
passage: One might define an intention to perform an action as a mental state that represents the action as to be performed by the subject, includes a commitment or resolution to perform it, and is causally efficacious in bringing about the action. The first two conditions seem unexceptionable: to intend to do something is surely to represent it as something one will do and to be committed to doing it. The third condition, however, introduces a difficulty. Consider a man who intends to post a letter on his way to work. He represents the posting of the letter as something he will do; he is firmly resolved to post it. Yet, absorbed in thought during his walk, he passes the postbox without pausing and arrives at work with the letter still in his pocket. His intention was never causally efficacious, for the action it represented was never performed. One might observe, however, that we should not deny on these grounds that the man had an intention to post the letter. He clearly did intend to post it; he merely failed to act on his intention. The definition, by requiring causal efficacy, has confused the fulfilment of an intention with the intention itself, and in consequence it would deny the name of intention to every unsuccessful resolve — a result that is plainly unacceptable.

definition: A mental state M constitutes a genuine memory of a past event E if and only if (i) M represents E as having occurred in the past, (ii) the subject experienced E at the time of its occurrence, and (iii) M is accompanied by a feeling of pastness — a subjective sense that the represented event belongs to the subject's personal past.
conditions: M represents E as having occurred in the past
The subject experienced E at the time of its occurrence
M is accompanied by a feeling of pastness — a subjective sense that the represented event belongs to the subject's personal past
counterexample: Consider a man who was involved in a minor motor accident some years ago. He has no recollection of the event whatever; it has been entirely forgotten. Yet his wife has described the accident to him so often and so vividly that he now represents the event as having occurred in his past, and this representation is accompanied by a strong feeling of pastness — it seems to him as though he remembers the accident. All three conditions are satisfied, yet we should hesitate to say that he genuinely remembers the event, for his mental state is not causally connected to his original experience of the accident in the appropriate way.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require an appropriate causal connection between the subject's original experience of the event and the present mental state, so that representations acquired through testimony or suggestion are not distinguished from genuine memories.
passage: It appears natural to define a genuine memory of a past event as a mental state that represents the event as having occurred in the past, that derives from the subject's own experience of the event at the time of its occurrence, and that is accompanied by a feeling of pastness — a subjective sense that the event belongs to the subject's personal history. This definition captures the ordinary cases well: when one remembers one's first day at school, one represents that day as past, one did indeed experience it, and one has a characteristic feeling that the event belongs to one's own life. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when we consider what might be called a confabulated memory. A man involved in a minor motor accident some years ago has entirely forgotten the event. But his wife has described the accident to him so often and so vividly that he now has a mental representation of the event as past, complete with a feeling of pastness. He seems to himself to remember the accident. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied: the event is represented as past, the subject did originally experience it, and the feeling of pastness is present. Yet one might observe that this is not a genuine memory, for the present mental state is not causally connected to the original experience in the appropriate manner. It is a reconstruction from testimony, not a preservation from experience.

definition: A sensation S is a pain if and only if (i) S is a bodily sensation localised in some region of the subject's body, (ii) S possesses a negative hedonic quality — it is intrinsically unpleasant, and (iii) S motivates the subject to engage in avoidance behaviour with respect to its cause.
conditions: S is a bodily sensation localised in some region of the subject's body
S possesses a negative hedonic quality — it is intrinsically unpleasant
S motivates the subject to engage in avoidance behaviour with respect to its cause
counterexample: Consider the phenomenon of pain asymbolia, a condition in which a patient reports feeling a sharp sensation localised in the hand when it is pricked with a needle, and identifies the sensation as a pain, yet experiences no unpleasantness and makes no effort to withdraw the hand. The sensation is localised in a bodily region, but it lacks both the negative hedonic quality and the motivational force that the definition requires. Yet the patient insists, and the clinician agrees, that the sensation is a pain — it is simply a pain stripped of its usual affective and motivational accompaniments.
passage: One might propose that a pain is a bodily sensation localised in some region of the subject's body, possessing a negative hedonic quality, and motivating the subject to engage in avoidance behaviour. This definition accords well with the paradigmatic cases: a burn on the hand is localised, unpleasant, and prompts withdrawal. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal arises from the clinical phenomenon of pain asymbolia. Patients suffering from this condition report feeling a sharp, localised sensation when pricked with a needle. They identify the sensation as a pain. Yet they experience no unpleasantness — the sensation has lost its negative hedonic character — and they make no effort to withdraw the affected limb. Both the second and third conditions of the definition fail to be satisfied. One might observe, however, that the clinician and the patient agree in calling the sensation a pain; it is a pain of which the affective and motivational dimensions have been stripped away by the neurological condition. The definition, by making unpleasantness and avoidance behaviour necessary conditions, would exclude this case and thereby reveal that it has confused the typical accompaniments of pain with its essential character as a type of sensation.

definition: A subject S perceives an object O if and only if (i) O exists in S's environment, (ii) S has a sensory experience that represents O as being present, and (iii) S's sensory experience is caused by O in the normal way.
conditions: O exists in S's environment
S has a sensory experience that represents O as being present
S's sensory experience is caused by O in the normal way
counterexample: Consider a man in a desert who sees what appears to be an oasis ahead. As it happens, there is a genuine oasis at that location, and so the first condition is satisfied. His sensory experience represents the oasis as being present, satisfying the second. But his experience is not caused by the oasis in the normal way — it is caused by a mirage, a refraction of light from the sky, which happens by coincidence to appear at the exact spot where the real oasis lies. We should hesitate to say that the man perceives the oasis, for the causal connection between object and experience, though present in a deviant sense, does not follow the normal perceptual pathway.
missing_condition: The definition's requirement that the experience be caused by the object 'in the normal way' is insufficiently precise; it needs to specify the particular causal chain characteristic of veridical perception so as to exclude cases where a deviant causal route accidentally produces a matching experience.
passage: A natural definition of perception holds that a subject perceives an object if and only if the object exists in the subject's environment, the subject has a sensory experience representing the object as present, and the experience is caused by the object in the normal way. This definition has evident appeal: it requires both the existence of the object and an appropriate causal connection, thereby excluding hallucinations and illusions. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the following scenario. A man in a desert sees what appears to be an oasis ahead. There is, as it happens, a genuine oasis at that location. His visual experience represents the oasis as present. But his experience is caused not by the oasis itself but by a mirage — a refraction of light from the sky — which happens by coincidence to appear at exactly the location of the real oasis. One might say that the oasis does causally contribute to his experience in some remote sense, for it is part of the environment that produces the overall visual scene. But the causal route does not follow the normal perceptual pathway. The definition, by specifying only that the experience be caused by the object 'in the normal way,' fails to provide a sufficiently precise account of the required causal chain, and thereby admits cases in which the match between experience and object is accidental rather than genuinely perceptual.

definition: A person P at time t2 is the same person as a person P at time t1 if and only if (i) P at t2 has a continuous stream of consciousness connecting t1 and t2, (ii) P at t2 can remember experiences had by P at t1, and (iii) there is a continuity of character and personality between P at t1 and P at t2.
conditions: P at t2 has a continuous stream of consciousness connecting t1 and t2
P at t2 can remember experiences had by P at t1
There is a continuity of character and personality between P at t1 and P at t2
counterexample: Consider an elderly man who suffers from severe amnesia. He cannot remember any experiences from his youth; the first condition is broken by the nightly interruptions of dreamless sleep over decades; and his character has changed profoundly from the reckless young adventurer he once was. Yet we do not hesitate to say that he is the same person as the young man who bore his name. His wife recognises him; the law holds him responsible for contracts signed in his youth; and he bears the same bodily identity throughout.
missing_condition: The definition omits any requirement of bodily or physical continuity, which in ordinary judgement is often sufficient for personal identity even when psychological continuity has been disrupted.
passage: One might define personal identity over time by the following conditions: a person at a later time is the same person as a person at an earlier time if and only if there is a continuous stream of consciousness connecting the two times, the later person can remember experiences had by the earlier person, and there is a continuity of character and personality. This definition captures an important strand of our thinking about personal identity — the sense in which our identity is bound up with our psychological continuity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when we consider an elderly man who suffers from severe amnesia. He cannot recall any experience from his youth. His stream of consciousness has been broken innumerable times by dreamless sleep and periods of unconsciousness during surgery. His character has changed profoundly: the reckless young adventurer has become a cautious and retiring old man. All three conditions fail. Yet one might observe that we should not hesitate to say that this man is the same person as the young man who bore his name decades ago. His wife recognises him; the law holds him responsible for obligations incurred in his youth; and the bodily continuity that links the young man to the old is unbroken. The definition, by confining itself to psychological criteria, has neglected the role of bodily continuity in grounding our ordinary judgements of personal identity.

definition: A system S is conscious if and only if (i) S processes information from its environment and generates appropriate behavioural responses, (ii) S exhibits adaptive behaviour that is not fully predictable from its initial programming or design, and (iii) S is capable of reporting on its own internal states when prompted.
conditions: S processes information from its environment and generates appropriate behavioural responses
S exhibits adaptive behaviour that is not fully predictable from its initial programming or design
S is capable of reporting on its own internal states when prompted
counterexample: Consider a sophisticated thermostat equipped with a learning algorithm that adjusts its temperature regulation on the basis of occupants' past preferences. It processes environmental information and produces appropriate behavioural responses. Its behaviour is adaptive and not fully predictable from its initial design. When queried, it can report its current settings, sensor readings, and recent adjustments. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to say that such a thermostat is conscious in any sense that we should find philosophically interesting.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S possess phenomenal or qualitative experience — that there be something it is like to be S — as opposed to merely exhibiting the functional hallmarks of information-processing and self-report.
passage: One might attempt to characterise consciousness by appeal to the functional capacities that conscious beings typically display: the processing of environmental information, adaptive behaviour, and the ability to report on one's own internal states. This proposal has the considerable advantage of being empirically tractable, for each of its conditions can, in principle, be tested by external observation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed without great difficulty. Suppose we encounter a sophisticated thermostat equipped with a learning algorithm that adjusts its regulation of room temperature on the basis of the occupants' past preferences. It receives information from temperature sensors and motion detectors and produces appropriate heating and cooling responses. Its behaviour is adaptive — it gradually learns patterns of occupancy — and not fully predictable from its initial design. When queried via its interface, it can report its current settings, its recent sensor readings, and the adjustments it has made. All three conditions of the definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that nothing about this thermostat suggests the presence of phenomenal experience. There is, it appears, nothing it is like to be this thermostat. The definition, by attending only to functional capacities, cannot distinguish genuine consciousness from the mere simulation of its outward manifestations.

definition: An agent A intends to bring about a state of affairs S if and only if (i) A has formed a plan of action directed towards bringing about S, (ii) A believes that the plan has a reasonable prospect of success, and (iii) A has not abandoned the plan prior to attempting its execution.
conditions: A has formed a plan of action directed towards bringing about S
A believes that the plan has a reasonable prospect of success
A has not abandoned the plan prior to attempting its execution
counterexample: Consider a chess player who forms a detailed plan to execute a particular combination of moves, believes the plan will succeed in checkmate, and has not abandoned the plan. Yet she is playing a simultaneous exhibition match and has formed identical plans against six opponents. Midway through execution, she realises that on board four the position has changed, so the plan there would now achieve something quite different from what she originally envisaged, yet she continues mechanically. All three conditions are satisfied for board four, yet we should hesitate to say she genuinely intends to bring about the original state of affairs there, for she no longer represents the plan as directed towards that end.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that A currently represent the plan as directed towards S at the time of acting, rather than merely not having formally abandoned a plan that was once so directed.
passage: It is natural to suppose that intention may be characterised by the conjunction of a plan of action, a belief in its likely success, and the absence of any decision to abandon it. This tripartite analysis captures the forward-looking, action-guiding character of intention that many philosophers of mind have regarded as central. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the context of complex, multi-tracked deliberation. Consider a chess player engaged in a simultaneous exhibition, playing six boards at once. Against each opponent she has formed a plan directed towards a particular combination of moves, believes the plan has a reasonable prospect of success, and has not formally abandoned any of these plans. On board four, however, the position shifts unexpectedly during her attention to other boards. When she returns to board four, she continues to execute the same sequence of moves mechanically, but the position has changed in such a way that the moves no longer tend towards the checkmate she originally envisaged. She has not abandoned the plan — she has simply failed to notice the change. All three conditions remain satisfied. Yet one might observe that her action at board four is no longer genuinely intentional with respect to the original state of affairs. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the formation of a plan and the absence of explicit abandonment, fails to capture the requirement that the agent currently represent the plan as directed towards the intended outcome at the time of acting.

definition: A person P at time t2 is identical with a person Q at an earlier time t1 if and only if (i) P possesses continuous memories linking back to Q's experiences, (ii) P's body is spatiotemporally continuous with Q's body, and (iii) P exhibits the same fundamental character traits and dispositions as Q.
conditions: P possesses continuous memories linking back to Q's experiences
P's body is spatiotemporally continuous with Q's body
P exhibits the same fundamental character traits and dispositions as Q
counterexample: Consider a case of severe retrograde amnesia following a traumatic brain injury. The patient retains no memories of her life before the injury. Her body is spatiotemporally continuous with that of the person she was before, and after recovery she happens to exhibit the same fundamental character traits — the same temperament, the same generosity, the same dry humour. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied, but condition (i) is not, since there are no continuous memories linking the two stages. Yet it would be extraordinary to deny that she is the same person.
passage: A natural proposal for the analysis of personal identity through time holds that a person at a later time is identical with a person at an earlier time if and only if there is continuous memory, spatiotemporal bodily continuity, and continuity of fundamental character. This proposal draws together the three strands that have figured most prominently in the philosophical literature. Yet one might observe that cases arise in which the conditions come apart in ways that put pressure on the analysis. Consider a patient who suffers severe retrograde amnesia as a consequence of a traumatic brain injury. She retains no memories of her life before the event — her childhood, her education, her relationships are all lost to her. Her body, however, is spatiotemporally continuous with the body she possessed before the injury. And as she recovers, she happens to exhibit the same fundamental character traits and dispositions: the same temperament, the same generosity, the same dry humour. Two of the three conditions are met, yet the first — continuous memory — is absent. Nevertheless, it appears that we should say without hesitation that she is the same person. Her family, her friends, and the law all treat her as such. It appears that the definition, by requiring continuous memory as a necessary condition, is too demanding: it would compel us to deny personal identity in cases where common sense and practice emphatically affirm it.

definition: An action A performed by an agent S is free if and only if (i) S could have done otherwise than A in the circumstances, (ii) A was not the product of external coercion or compulsion, and (iii) S performed A on the basis of deliberation among alternatives.
conditions: S could have done otherwise than A in the circumstances
A was not the product of external coercion or compulsion
S performed A on the basis of deliberation among alternatives
counterexample: Consider a recovering addict who deliberates carefully between accepting and declining an offered drink, is subject to no external coercion, and could — in the libertarian sense — have chosen otherwise. He declines the drink. Yet his deliberation, unbeknown to him, was entirely driven by a neurological implant placed during a surgical procedure, which stimulated the regions of his brain responsible for aversion to alcohol. All three conditions are met: he could have done otherwise, he was not externally coerced, and he deliberated among alternatives. Yet we should hesitate to call his action free, for the deliberation itself was manipulated.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the deliberative process itself be free from covert manipulation or bypassing of the agent's rational capacities, even when external coercion is absent.
passage: One might characterise a free action as one in which the agent could have done otherwise, was not externally coerced, and acted on the basis of deliberation among alternatives. This proposal is attractive in that it combines the indeterminist intuition — the open-alternatives condition — with the rationalist intuition that free action involves genuine deliberation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be described as follows. Suppose a recovering addict is offered a drink at a social gathering. He deliberates carefully, weighing the pleasure of acceptance against the risks of relapse. He is subject to no external coercion — no one threatens or compels him. And let us suppose, with the libertarian, that he genuinely could have chosen otherwise. He declines the drink. Yet unbeknown to him, a neurological implant placed during a recent surgical procedure has been stimulating the aversion centres of his brain throughout his deliberation. The implant does not bypass his deliberative processes — he genuinely considers alternatives — but it covertly tilts the balance of his reasoning. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should be reluctant to call this action free. The deliberation was manipulated from within, and the agent's rational capacities were not functioning as they would have done in the absence of the implant. It appears that the definition, by attending only to external coercion and the availability of alternatives, cannot detect covert manipulation of the agent's own deliberative processes.

definition: A bodily movement M constitutes an action if and only if (i) M is caused by the agent's desire to bring about some end, (ii) M is caused by the agent's belief that M will contribute to bringing about that end, and (iii) the desire and belief jointly cause M.
conditions: M is caused by the agent's desire to bring about some end
M is caused by the agent's belief that M will contribute to bringing about that end
The desire and belief jointly cause M
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer who desires to lighten his load and believes that releasing his companion's rope would contribute to that end. This desire and belief make him so anxious that his hands begin to tremble uncontrollably, and the trembling causes him to release the rope. The desire and belief jointly caused the movement. Yet the release was not an action in the relevant sense — it was a nervous tremor brought about by the psychological states in a deviant manner, not through the exercise of the agent's motor control.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the desire and belief cause the bodily movement in the right way — that is, through the agent's exercise of rational motor control — rather than through some deviant causal chain.
passage: The causal theory of action proposes that a bodily movement constitutes an action if and only if it is jointly caused by a relevant desire and a relevant belief. This analysis has considerable appeal: it connects action to the agent's reasons for acting and provides a naturalistic framework within which action can be understood. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency has become something of a classic in the philosophy of mind. Consider a mountaineer who desires to lighten his load and believes that releasing his companion's rope would contribute to that end. The desire and the belief, taken together, make him so anxious that his hands begin to tremble uncontrollably. The trembling causes him to release the rope. It is true that the bodily movement — the release of the rope — was caused by the desire to lighten the load. It is true that it was caused by the belief that releasing the rope would achieve this end. And it is true that the desire and belief jointly caused the movement. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the release of the rope was not an action at all, but an involuntary tremor. The causal chain ran from the mental states to the movement through anxiety and loss of motor control, rather than through the normal exercise of rational agency. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the desire and belief jointly cause the movement, cannot distinguish genuine actions from bodily movements that are caused by the right mental states in the wrong way.

definition: A mental state E experienced by a subject S is an emotion if and only if (i) E involves a characteristic bodily feeling or sensation, (ii) E is directed towards an intentional object, and (iii) E disposes S towards characteristic patterns of behaviour.
conditions: E involves a characteristic bodily feeling or sensation
E is directed towards an intentional object
E disposes S towards characteristic patterns of behaviour
counterexample: Consider a person suffering from a severe case of clinical depression. She has a characteristic bodily heaviness and fatigue. Her depression is directed towards her life circumstances — it is about something. And she is disposed towards characteristic behavioural patterns such as withdrawal and inactivity. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that clinical depression is typically classified as a mood rather than an emotion. It pervades the subject's entire mental life in a way that discrete emotions such as anger or fear do not, and it persists regardless of changes in its ostensible intentional object.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that E be a discrete, episodic mental state that arises in response to a particular occasion and subsides when that occasion passes, rather than a pervasive and enduring affective condition.
passage: One might characterise emotion by appeal to three features that paradigmatic emotions appear to share: a characteristic bodily feeling, directedness towards an intentional object, and a disposition towards characteristic behaviour. This proposal has the merit of combining the somatic, the cognitive, and the behavioural dimensions that various theories have emphasised. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be found in the phenomenon of clinical depression. Consider a subject who experiences a characteristic bodily heaviness and fatigue. Her condition is directed towards her life circumstances — she is depressed about her work, her relationships, her future. And she is disposed towards characteristic patterns of withdrawal and inactivity. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to classify clinical depression as an emotion in the ordinary sense. Depression is a mood — a pervasive, enduring affective condition that colours the subject's entire mental life. It does not arise in response to a particular occasion and subside when that occasion passes, as anger at an insult or fear of a dog characteristically does. It persists through changes in the subject's circumstances and resists the kind of rational reassessment that can dissolve a discrete emotion. It appears that the definition, by attending only to feeling, intentionality, and behavioural disposition, cannot distinguish the discrete and episodic character of genuine emotions from the diffuse and chronic character of moods.

definition: A subject S perceives an object O if and only if (i) O exists in S's environment, (ii) S has a sensory experience as of O, and (iii) S's sensory experience is caused by O.
conditions: O exists in S's environment
S has a sensory experience as of O
S's sensory experience is caused by O
counterexample: Consider a subject standing in a hall of mirrors. A vase sits behind her, and a mirror arrangement causes light from the vase to reach her eyes by an extraordinarily indirect path involving multiple reflections. She has a sensory experience as of a vase. The vase exists in her environment. And her experience is caused by the vase, for it is the vase's light that reaches her — albeit after numerous reflections that misrepresent the vase's location and orientation. All three conditions are met, yet one might observe that she does not perceive the vase in any straightforward sense; she perceives an image of the vase in a location where no vase stands.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the causal process connecting O to S's sensory experience preserve the spatial and structural information about O, rather than merely originating from O through a chain that distorts its apparent properties.
passage: A natural analysis of perception holds that a subject perceives an object if and only if the object exists in the subject's environment, the subject has a sensory experience as of the object, and the experience is caused by the object. This causal theory of perception has been influential and captures the intuition that perception involves genuine contact with the external world. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by considering the peculiarities of indirect vision. Suppose a subject stands in a hall of mirrors. Behind her sits a porcelain vase. Through an elaborate arrangement of reflective surfaces, light from the vase reaches her eyes after numerous reflections. She has a sensory experience as of a vase — she seems to see a vase. The vase exists in her environment. And her experience is caused by the vase, for the light originates from it. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that what the subject sees is not the vase itself but a reflected image of the vase appearing at a location where no vase stands. The image may be inverted, distorted, or displaced. We should hesitate to say that she perceives the vase in the ordinary sense.
