It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the object cause the experience, fails to distinguish veridical perception from cases in which the causal chain so distorts the spatial and structural information that the resulting experience misrepresents the object's actual properties and location.

definition: A subject S remembers an event E if and only if (i) S has a present mental representation of E, (ii) S experienced E at some earlier time, and (iii) S's present representation of E is caused by S's original experience of E.
conditions: S has a present mental representation of E
S experienced E at some earlier time
S's present representation of E is caused by S's original experience of E
counterexample: Consider a man who witnessed a minor traffic accident in his youth. Over the years, his genuine memory fades, but a family member repeatedly tells him a vivid but substantially inaccurate version of the event. He now has a mental representation of the accident; he did experience it at an earlier time; and his present representation is caused — in part — by the original experience, since the family member's narrative was itself prompted by the original event. All three conditions are met. Yet the content of his present representation is largely the product of suggestion, and it misrepresents the event in significant respects.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the content of S's present representation accurately preserve the content of the original experience, rather than merely being causally descended from it through a chain that may introduce substantial distortion.
passage: One might propose that remembering an event consists in having a present mental representation of it that is caused by one's original experience of the event. This causal-preservative account captures the intuition that memory involves a genuine link to the past. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the well-documented phenomenon of memory distortion through suggestion. Suppose a man witnessed a minor traffic accident as a child. His genuine memory of the event gradually fades over the years. Meanwhile, a family member who was also present repeatedly recounts a vivid but substantially inaccurate version of the incident — embellishing certain details, omitting others, and introducing elements that were not part of the original scene. The man now possesses a present mental representation of the accident. He did experience the event at an earlier time. And his present representation is causally connected to the original experience, since the family member's narrative was itself prompted by the actual event. All three conditions of the definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the content of his present representation bears little resemblance to what actually occurred. It is a confabulation shaped by years of suggestion, and we should hesitate to say that he remembers the accident as it happened. It appears that the definition, by requiring only a causal link to the original experience, cannot distinguish genuine memory from cases in which the causal chain has introduced such distortion that the content of the representation has been substantially corrupted.

definition: A subject S has a pain if and only if (i) S is in a functional state that typically results from tissue damage, (ii) S is disposed to avoid the stimulus causing the state, and (iii) S is disposed to report that something hurts when asked.
conditions: S is in a functional state that typically results from tissue damage
S is disposed to avoid the stimulus causing the state
S is disposed to report that something hurts when asked
counterexample: Consider a patient who has undergone a procedure in which nerve signals are rerouted so that stimulation of a particular area of undamaged skin produces the functional state that typically results from tissue damage. She withdraws from the stimulus and reports that something hurts. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet no tissue damage has occurred or is occurring, and one might question whether the functional state, produced by an entirely artificial rerouting of nerve signals in the absence of any harmful stimulus, constitutes genuine pain or merely an artificially induced functional analogue of pain.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the functional state bear an appropriate relationship to actual or potential harm to the organism, rather than being produced by an arbitrary rewiring of the nervous system in the absence of any noxious stimulus.
passage: A functionalist analysis of pain holds that a subject has a pain if and only if the subject is in a functional state typically resulting from tissue damage, is disposed to avoid the stimulus, and is disposed to report discomfort. This proposal has the advantage of being neutral between different physical realisations of pain, and it accords well with the behavioural manifestations we commonly associate with painful experience. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed as follows. Suppose a patient undergoes a neurosurgical procedure in which certain nerve pathways are rerouted so that gentle stimulation of a particular area of entirely undamaged skin produces the same functional state that ordinarily results from tissue damage. When the area is touched, the patient withdraws from the stimulus and reports that something hurts. All three conditions of the definition are met: the functional state is of the type that typically results from tissue damage, the patient is disposed to avoid the stimulus, and she reports pain. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to assimilate this case to ordinary pain without qualification. No tissue damage has occurred or is threatened. The stimulus is harmless. The functional state has been produced by an artificial rerouting of nerve signals, not by any noxious input. It appears that the definition, by characterising pain solely in terms of functional role, cannot distinguish genuine pain — which bears an intimate connection to actual or potential bodily harm — from states that mimic pain's functional profile without any corresponding relation to harm.

definition: A subject S desires that P if and only if (i) S is disposed to act in ways that would bring about P, (ii) S would experience satisfaction if P were to obtain, and (iii) S would experience dissatisfaction if P were clearly unattainable.
conditions: S is disposed to act in ways that would bring about P
S would experience satisfaction if P were to obtain
S would experience dissatisfaction if P were clearly unattainable
counterexample: Consider a person who is disposed to eat large quantities of sugary food, who experiences satisfaction when she eats it, and who would experience dissatisfaction if deprived of it. All three conditions point towards a desire for sugary food. Yet suppose she is a recovering compulsive eater who sincerely and reflectively disavows any desire for sugar, regards her disposition as a pathological compulsion, and experiences her own satisfaction with disgust. One might observe that the definition classifies her compulsive urge as a desire, whereas she herself — and, arguably, a correct philosophical analysis — would distinguish the compulsion from a genuine desire.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S endorse or identify with the disposition to bring about P upon reflection, thereby conflating genuine desires with compulsions and addictive urges that the agent disavows.
passage: One might analyse desire by appeal to its characteristic functional role: a subject desires that P if the subject is disposed to act towards bringing about P, would feel satisfaction upon P's obtaining, and would feel dissatisfaction if P were unattainable. This analysis captures the motivational, hedonic, and aversive dimensions of desire. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider compulsive behaviour. Suppose a person is disposed to consume large quantities of sugary food, experiences satisfaction when she does so, and would experience genuine dissatisfaction if deprived of access to it. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied, and the analysis accordingly classifies her state as a desire for sugary food. Yet suppose further that she is a recovering compulsive eater who has, upon careful reflection, come to regard her disposition as a pathological compulsion rather than a genuine desire. She sincerely disavows any wish for sugar, finds her own episodes of satisfaction repugnant, and takes active steps to resist the impulse. One might observe that there is an important difference between a desire and a compulsion, even when the two are functionally indistinguishable in terms of behavioural disposition and hedonic response. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the functional profile of the state, cannot distinguish desires with which the agent identifies from compulsive urges that the agent reflectively repudiates.

definition: A mental state M is a conscious experience if and only if (i) M has representational content directed towards some object or state of affairs, (ii) the subject of M is aware of being in M, and (iii) M is available for verbal report by the subject.
conditions: M has representational content directed towards some object or state of affairs
The subject of M is aware of being in M
M is available for verbal report by the subject
counterexample: Consider a subject who, under experimental conditions, is presented with a visual stimulus in her peripheral field. She is unaware of the stimulus and cannot report seeing anything. Yet her subsequent behaviour — she adjusts her reach to avoid the object — reveals that some mental state with representational content directed towards the stimulus has occurred. The state has representational content and it influences behaviour, but the subject is not aware of it and cannot report it. Nevertheless, one might observe that this state possesses at least some of the marks of conscious experience: it represents a feature of the world and guides purposive action.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between full reflective awareness and the more basic phenomenal character of experience, thereby excluding states that possess genuine qualitative content but fall below the threshold of reportability.
passage: One might characterise conscious experience as a mental state with representational content, of which the subject is aware and which is available for verbal report. This proposal captures the introspective accessibility that many philosophers of mind have taken to be central to consciousness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the experimental study of vision. Suppose a subject is presented with a visual stimulus — a small disc of coloured light — in the far periphery of her visual field. She reports seeing nothing. She is, by her own account, entirely unaware of the stimulus. Yet when asked to reach for an object near the location of the disc, she adjusts the trajectory of her hand in a manner that reveals sensitivity to the disc's position and size. Some mental state with representational content directed towards the stimulus appears to have occurred, for the subject's motor behaviour is systematically influenced by properties of the disc. This state has representational content, yet the subject is not aware of being in it and cannot report it. The definition would exclude it from the class of conscious experiences. Yet one might observe that the exclusion is too swift. The state represents a feature of the external world and guides purposive action in a manner that goes beyond mere reflex. It appears that the definition, by requiring both awareness and reportability, draws the boundary of conscious experience in a way that may exclude states possessing genuine phenomenal character but lacking the higher-order accessibility that report demands.

definition: An agent S has an intention to perform an action A if and only if (i) S has a mental representation of A as something to be done by S, (ii) S is committed to performing A in the absence of overriding considerations, and (iii) S's representation of A plays a guiding role in S's practical reasoning about how to act.
conditions: S has a mental representation of A as something to be done by S
S is committed to performing A in the absence of overriding considerations
S's representation of A plays a guiding role in S's practical reasoning about how to act
counterexample: Consider a pianist who, in the course of performing a well-rehearsed sonata, executes a rapid passage requiring dozens of precisely coordinated finger movements per second. She does not mentally represent each movement as something to be done, nor does she deliberate about the individual finger placements during performance. Yet we should hesitate to deny that she intends to play the passage. Her performance is purposive, skilled, and responsive to error — if she strikes a wrong note, she corrects immediately. All three conditions fail for the individual movements, yet those movements are paradigmatically intentional.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to accommodate habitual and skilled action, in which intention operates at the level of an overarching goal rather than requiring discrete mental representation and deliberative guidance for each component movement.
passage: One might propose that intention consists in a mental representation of an action as something to be done, a commitment to performing it, and the guiding role of that representation in practical reasoning. This analysis captures the deliberative and forward-looking character of intention as it figures in philosophical discussions of rational agency. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the domain of skilled performance. Consider a concert pianist executing a rapid passage in a well-rehearsed sonata. The passage requires dozens of precisely coordinated finger movements per second. It appears that the pianist does not maintain a discrete mental representation of each movement as something to be done. She does not deliberate about individual finger placements during performance. The movements flow from deeply ingrained motor habits, and her conscious attention is directed not at the mechanics of finger placement but at the musical phrase as a whole. The individual movements fail to satisfy any of the three conditions. Yet we should hesitate to deny that they are intentional. The performance is purposive, responsive to error, and executed under the governance of a higher-order musical aim. If the pianist strikes a wrong note, she corrects immediately, demonstrating that the movements are not mere automatisms. It appears that the definition, by requiring explicit mental representation and deliberative guidance for each intended action, cannot accommodate the pervasive phenomenon of skilled action, in which intention operates at the level of an overarching goal and is realised through habitual routines that bypass the deliberative apparatus.

definition: A person P at time t2 is the same person as Q at time t1 if and only if (i) there is a continuous chain of psychological connections — memories, intentions, character traits — linking P at t2 to Q at t1, (ii) this chain is sustained by the normal biological processes of a single living brain, and (iii) P at t2 can, in principle, recall experiences had by Q at t1.
conditions: There is a continuous chain of psychological connections linking P at t2 to Q at t1
This chain is sustained by the normal biological processes of a single living brain
P at t2 can in principle recall experiences had by Q at t1
counterexample: Consider a person who undergoes gradual and complete replacement of neural tissue by functionally identical synthetic components over several decades, each replacement preserving the psychological connections, memories, and character traits of the preceding stage. At the end of the process, there is a continuous chain of psychological connections from the original person to the present one, and the present person can recall the original person's experiences. Yet condition (ii) is not satisfied, for the chain is no longer sustained by normal biological processes — the brain is entirely synthetic. Nevertheless, it would be difficult to deny that the person at the end of this gradual process is the same person as the one at its beginning.
passage: One might attempt to secure personal identity through time by requiring a continuous chain of psychological connections sustained by the normal biological processes of a single living brain, together with the capacity to recall earlier experiences. This proposal has the merit of combining the psychological continuity that many philosophers regard as essential with a biological grounding that prevents fanciful duplication scenarios. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the possibility of gradual neural replacement. Suppose that over several decades a person's neural tissue is replaced, neurone by neurone, with functionally identical synthetic components. Each replacement is so gradual and so precise that the psychological connections — memories, intentions, character traits — are perfectly preserved at every stage. The person notices no discontinuity. At the end of the process, the continuous chain of psychological connections is intact, and the person can recall experiences from any earlier period of her life. Yet the chain is no longer sustained by normal biological processes, for the brain is now entirely synthetic. Condition (ii) of the definition is not satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that it would be extraordinary to deny that this is the same person. The psychological continuity is unbroken, the memories are genuine, and the gradual character of the replacement seems to ensure that identity is preserved at every step. It appears that the definition, by insisting on biological substrates, draws a line that the gradual replacement case reveals to be arbitrary.

definition: A mental state E is an emotion if and only if (i) E involves a cognitive appraisal of some situation as bearing upon the subject's concerns, (ii) E has a characteristic phenomenal quality — that is, there is something it is like for the subject to be in E, and (iii) E motivates the subject towards a characteristic pattern of action.
conditions: E involves a cognitive appraisal of some situation as bearing upon the subject's concerns
E has a characteristic phenomenal quality
E motivates the subject towards a characteristic pattern of action
counterexample: Consider an infant of three months who, upon hearing a loud and sudden noise, displays a startle response: her body stiffens, her arms extend, and she begins to cry. There is plausibly something it is like for the infant to undergo this state, and the state motivates characteristic action — crying, clinging. Yet it is doubtful whether the infant performs a cognitive appraisal of the noise as bearing upon her concerns, for the concept of a concern presupposes a level of cognitive sophistication that a three-month-old arguably lacks. All the same, we should hesitate to deny that the infant experiences fear.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to accommodate pre-cognitive or sub-cognitive emotional responses — affective states that possess phenomenal quality and motivational force but arise prior to or independently of the kind of evaluative judgement that cognitive appraisal requires.
passage: A prominent approach to the analysis of emotion holds that an emotion involves a cognitive appraisal, a characteristic phenomenal quality, and a motivational orientation towards action. This tripartite analysis captures the evaluative, experiential, and behavioural dimensions that many philosophical accounts regard as essential. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be observed in the emotional lives of very young infants. Consider an infant of three months who hears a sudden, loud noise. She displays a pronounced startle response: her body stiffens, her arms extend outwards, and she begins to cry vigorously. There is, one might suppose, something it is like for the infant to undergo this state — a felt quality of alarm or distress. The state motivates a characteristic pattern of action: crying, seeking proximity to a caregiver, clinging. Two of the three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet it is far from clear that the infant performs a cognitive appraisal of the noise as bearing upon her concerns. Cognitive appraisal, as philosophers and psychologists typically understand it, involves the evaluation of a situation in relation to one's goals, values, or well-being — a process that presupposes concepts and evaluative capacities that a three-month-old arguably does not possess. Nevertheless, it would be peculiar to deny that the infant experiences something very like fear. It appears that the definition, by requiring cognitive appraisal as a necessary condition, cannot accommodate the pre-cognitive emotional responses that are among the earliest and most fundamental forms of affective experience.

definition: A subject S perceives a property F of an object O if and only if (i) O instantiates F, (ii) S has a sensory experience that represents O as having F, and (iii) S's experience is produced by the standard causal process appropriate to the relevant sense modality.
conditions: O instantiates F
S has a sensory experience that represents O as having F
S's experience is produced by the standard causal process appropriate to the relevant sense modality
counterexample: Consider a white wall illuminated by a red stage light. The wall instantiates the property of being white. Yet the subject's visual experience represents the wall as red, because the illumination alters the light reflected from the surface. Condition (i) is satisfied — the wall is white — and condition (iii) is satisfied — the experience is produced by the standard visual process of light striking the retina. But condition (ii) fails, for the experience represents the wall as red, not white. Yet many observers, particularly those familiar with stage lighting, will say that they perceive the wall's whiteness through the red illumination. They see it as a white wall under red light rather than as a red wall.
passage: One might analyse the perception of a property as requiring that the object instantiate the property, that the subject have an experience representing the object as possessing it, and that the experience be produced by the standard causal process for the relevant sense modality. This proposal appears to capture the veridicality and causal grounding that perception demands. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the familiar phenomenon of colour constancy under altered illumination. Consider a white wall bathed in red stage lighting. The wall instantiates the property of being white — its surface reflectance has not changed. The standard visual process is operative: light from the wall strikes the subject's retina through the normal optical pathway. Yet the subject's experience represents the wall not as white but as red, for the red illumination alters the spectral composition of the reflected light. Condition (ii) of the definition is not met. Nevertheless, many observers — particularly those experienced with stage lighting — will report that they see the wall as white, albeit under red light. They perceive the wall's whiteness through the distorting illumination, much as one perceives a circular coin as circular despite the elliptical retinal image it projects when viewed at an angle. It appears that the definition, by requiring that the experience represent the object as having the perceived property, cannot accommodate perceptual constancy — the pervasive capacity of perceivers to recover stable object properties through varying conditions of observation.

definition: A subject S desires that P if and only if (i) the thought of P's obtaining produces in S a positive hedonic response, (ii) S is disposed to take steps that would make P more likely, and (iii) S would choose P over its negation when presented with a forced choice between the two.
conditions: The thought of P's obtaining produces in S a positive hedonic response
S is disposed to take steps that would make P more likely
S would choose P over its negation when presented with a forced choice
counterexample: Consider a soldier who desires to perform his duty by holding a dangerous position under fire. The thought of holding the position does not produce a positive hedonic response — it fills him with dread. Yet he is disposed to take every step necessary to hold the position, and if presented with a forced choice between holding it and abandoning it, he would choose to hold it. Condition (i) is not satisfied, yet we should recognise that the soldier genuinely desires to do his duty, even though the prospect of doing so is deeply unpleasant.
passage: One might characterise desire by appeal to the hedonic response produced by the thought of its object, a behavioural disposition to promote its realisation, and a preference for its obtaining over its non-obtaining. This analysis has the attraction of bringing together the affective, the conative, and the preferential dimensions of desire. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers desires whose objects are onerous or frightening. Suppose a soldier stationed at a forward position recognises that it is his duty to hold the line against an advancing force. He desires to do his duty — genuinely, sincerely, and upon mature reflection. Yet the thought of holding the position does not produce a positive hedonic response. On the contrary, it fills him with dread and apprehension. He is, nevertheless, disposed to take every step necessary to hold the position, and if presented with a forced choice between holding it and abandoning it, he would choose to hold it without hesitation. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied, but condition (i) is not. The definition would therefore deny that the soldier desires to do his duty, a conclusion that one might find deeply counterintuitive. It appears that the definition, by requiring a positive hedonic response as a necessary condition, confuses the general category of desire with the narrower category of appetitive desire — desire for what is pleasant — and cannot accommodate the austere desires that arise from duty, principle, or moral commitment.

definition: A subject S remembers a fact F if and only if (i) S currently represents F as true, (ii) F is in fact true, (iii) S originally came to represent F as true through a reliable cognitive process, and (iv) there is a continuous causal chain from S's original representation of F to S's present representation.
conditions: S currently represents F as true
F is in fact true
S originally came to represent F as true through a reliable cognitive process
There is a continuous causal chain from S's original representation of F to S's present representation
counterexample: Consider a student who learns the date of the Battle of Hastings from a reliable textbook and retains this information through a continuous causal chain of memory traces. All four conditions are satisfied. Now suppose that during a period of confusion she briefly comes to doubt the date and considers an alternative, only to be re-convinced of the correct date by an unreliable source — a friend who happens to guess correctly. The causal chain from original learning to present representation passes through a link in which the information was sustained not by the original reliable process but by a lucky guess. All four conditions are formally met, yet one might observe that her present state is not a straightforward case of remembering.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the causal chain preserving the representation be sustained at every link by processes that are themselves reliable, rather than passing through a stage at which the connection to the original reliable acquisition is broken and coincidentally restored.
passage: One might propose that remembering a fact consists in currently representing it as true, its being true, one's having originally acquired it through a reliable cognitive process, and the existence of a continuous causal chain from the original to the present representation. This analysis combines veridicality with a causal-preservative requirement and a reliability condition on original acquisition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed as follows. Suppose a student learns the date of the Battle of Hastings — 1066 — from a thoroughly reliable history textbook. She retains this information in memory over several years. The causal chain from original learning to present representation is intact. Now suppose that during a period of examination anxiety, she briefly comes to doubt the date and considers several alternatives. Her doubt is resolved not by consulting the textbook again but by a casual remark from a friend who happens to guess correctly that the date is 1066. The friend has no historical training and is simply lucky. The student's present representation of the date is true, she originally acquired it through a reliable process, and there is a continuous causal chain linking original acquisition to present representation — the chain passes through the friend's remark but never breaks entirely. All four conditions are formally met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the student's present confidence in the date rests not on the original reliable acquisition but on a lucky guess. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the existence of a continuous causal chain, cannot distinguish cases in which the chain preserves the rational merit of the original acquisition from cases in which that merit is lost and coincidentally restored by an unreliable intermediary.

definition: An agent S acts freely in performing action A if and only if (i) S's action A proceeds from S's own desires and values, (ii) S would have refrained from A had S so desired, and (iii) S's desires and values were not implanted or manipulated by any external agent.
conditions: S's action A proceeds from S's own desires and values
S would have refrained from A had S so desired
S's desires and values were not implanted or manipulated by any external agent
counterexample: Consider a person raised from birth in a community that systematically and pervasively inculcates a particular set of values through education, social pressure, and cultural ritual, so thoroughly that she has never been exposed to any alternative. Her actions proceed from her own desires and values — she has fully internalised them. She would refrain from any action if she desired to, for no one coerces her. And no single external agent implanted her values; they arose from an entire culture. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that calling her free requires considerable qualification, for she has never had the opportunity to reflect upon her values from a standpoint outside the framework in which they were inculcated.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that S has had the opportunity to critically reflect upon and endorse the desires and values from which S acts, rather than merely having internalised values to which no alternative was ever presented.
passage: One might characterise free action as action that proceeds from the agent's own desires and values, that the agent would have refrained from had she so wished, and whose motivating desires and values were not implanted by an external agent. This analysis draws upon the compatibilist tradition and its emphasis on the agent's identification with her own motivational states. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the effects of pervasive cultural conditioning. Suppose a young woman is raised from birth in a closed community that systematically inculcates a particular set of values through education, social pressure, and elaborate cultural ritual. She has never been exposed to any alternative way of life. She has fully internalised the community's values and acts from them with conviction. She would refrain from any of her actions if she desired to — no one coerces her. And no single external agent implanted her values; they are the product of an entire culture rather than of any individual manipulator. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that her freedom is compromised in a way the definition cannot capture. She has never had the opportunity to reflect upon her values from a standpoint outside the framework in which they were formed. She cannot critically examine them because she has never encountered an alternative against which they might be measured. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that values not be implanted by an external agent, cannot distinguish free action grounded in reflective endorsement from action driven by values that the agent has never had the opportunity to question.

definition: A bodily movement M constitutes an intentional action if and only if (i) M is preceded by a conscious decision by the agent to perform M, (ii) the conscious decision is the proximate cause of M, and (iii) M conforms to the content of the decision — that is, the movement that occurs is the movement that was decided upon.
conditions: M is preceded by a conscious decision by the agent to perform M
The conscious decision is the proximate cause of M
M conforms to the content of the decision
counterexample: Consider a fluent speaker of French engaged in animated conversation. She produces hundreds of precisely articulated phonemes per minute, each requiring coordinated movements of the tongue, lips, jaw, and larynx. She does not consciously decide to produce each phoneme, nor does each articulatory movement arise from a discrete conscious decision. Yet her speech is paradigmatically intentional — it is purposive, meaningful, and responsive to the conversational context. The individual articulatory movements fail to satisfy condition (i), yet they are constituents of an intentional action.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the vast class of intentional actions whose component movements are executed automatically under the governance of a higher-level intention, without requiring a separate conscious decision for each constituent movement.
passage: One might analyse intentional action as a bodily movement preceded by a conscious decision, proximately caused by that decision, and conforming to its content. This analysis has the appeal of grounding intentional action in the agent's conscious mental life and of providing a clear criterion for distinguishing intentional from unintentional movements. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be found in the ordinary phenomenon of fluent speech. Consider a speaker of French engaged in animated conversation at a dinner party. She produces hundreds of precisely articulated phonemes per minute. Each phoneme requires a coordinated movement of the tongue, lips, jaw, and larynx — movements of extraordinary precision and complexity. Yet she does not consciously decide to produce each phoneme. She does not form a discrete intention for each articulatory movement. The individual movements arise not from conscious decisions but from deeply ingrained linguistic habits operating under the governance of her communicative intention. Condition (i) of the definition is not satisfied for the individual articulatory movements. Nevertheless, we should not hesitate to say that her speech is intentional action of the most paradigmatic sort. It is purposive, meaningful, responsive to the conversational context, and under the speaker's control in the sense that she could modify it at any moment. It appears that the definition, by requiring a conscious decision as the proximate cause of each movement, cannot account for the pervasive phenomenon of intentional action whose component movements are executed automatically under the guidance of a higher-level intention.

definition: Two subjects S1 and S2 have the same sensation if and only if (i) the physical stimulus producing the sensation is identical in both cases, (ii) the behavioural responses of S1 and S2 to the stimulus are indistinguishable, and (iii) S1 and S2 give the same verbal description of their experience when asked.
conditions: The physical stimulus producing the sensation is identical in both cases
The behavioural responses of S1 and S2 to the stimulus are indistinguishable
S1 and S2 give the same verbal description of their experience when asked
counterexample: Consider two subjects who each taste a particular wine. The physical stimulus — the chemical composition of the wine — is identical. Both describe the wine as 'dry with a hint of blackcurrant,' and both exhibit the same facial expressions and purchasing behaviour. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose that S1 is a trained sommelier who experiences a rich, highly differentiated array of flavour qualities, while S2, though she uses the same vocabulary, experiences a comparatively flat and undifferentiated taste. Their verbal reports coincide because S2 has learned the conventional descriptors without possessing the discriminatory acuity that gives those descriptors their full meaning for S1.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the phenomenal character — the qualitative feel — of the two sensations be the same, rather than merely that their external manifestations in behaviour and verbal report coincide.
passage: One might propose that two subjects have the same sensation when the physical stimulus is identical, their behavioural responses are indistinguishable, and their verbal descriptions agree. This proposal has the advantage of resting entirely upon publicly observable criteria, thereby avoiding the difficulties that attend any appeal to private, introspectible qualities. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the domain of gustatory experience. Consider two subjects who each taste a particular Bordeaux. The physical stimulus is identical — the same wine from the same bottle. Both describe it as dry with a hint of blackcurrant. Both exhibit similar facial expressions and are equally willing to purchase a case. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet suppose that the first subject is a trained sommelier with decades of tasting experience. She experiences a rich, finely differentiated array of flavour qualities — she can discriminate the contribution of the oak, the tannin structure, the length of the finish. The second subject, though she has learned the conventional vocabulary of wine description, experiences a comparatively flat and undifferentiated taste. She uses the same words but they do not, for her, correspond to the same wealth of qualitative detail. One might observe that the two sensations are not the same, despite the identity of stimulus, behaviour, and verbal report. It appears that the definition, by confining itself to publicly accessible criteria, cannot capture the qualitative character that distinguishes one sensation from another when that character is not reflected in behavioural or verbal differences.

definition: A state of consciousness C is genuinely unified if and only if (i) the subject of C experiences its contents as belonging to a single field of awareness, (ii) the contents of C are simultaneously accessible to the subject's faculty of attention, and (iii) the subject can report on the relations among the contents of C.
conditions: The subject of C experiences its contents as belonging to a single field of awareness
The contents of C are simultaneously accessible to the subject's faculty of attention
The subject can report on the relations among the contents of C
counterexample: Consider a patient who has undergone surgical division of the corpus callosum. When a word is presented to his left visual field, his left hand can select the corresponding object from behind a screen, yet he verbally denies seeing anything. His right hemisphere processes the word and guides action; his left hemisphere controls speech and reports no awareness. The subject cannot report on the relation between the visual content and the motor response, yet each hemisphere sustains a form of awareness with its own contents. One might observe that consciousness persists in both hemispheres despite the failure of the three conditions to hold across the whole person.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate cases in which the neural substrate of consciousness is divided, producing two streams of awareness within a single organism, neither of which satisfies the unity conditions with respect to the other.
passage: One might characterise the unity of consciousness as a state in which the subject experiences its contents as belonging to a single field, can attend to them simultaneously, and can report on their interrelations. This analysis captures the seamlessness of ordinary waking experience and the integrative character of normal conscious life. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is provided by the clinical phenomenon of the split brain. When the corpus callosum — the great fibre tract connecting the cerebral hemispheres — is surgically divided, the result is a patient whose two hemispheres sustain apparently independent streams of awareness. A word presented to the left visual field is processed by the right hemisphere, which can guide the left hand to select a corresponding object. Yet the left hemisphere, which controls speech, reports seeing nothing and denies that any selection has occurred. The subject cannot report on the relation between the visual content available to one hemisphere and the motor response guided by the other. The three conditions of the definition fail to hold across the whole person. Nevertheless, it would be difficult to deny that consciousness persists in both hemispheres: the right hemisphere perceives, recognises, and acts purposefully, while the left hemisphere maintains its own coherent field of awareness. It appears that the definition, by requiring a single unified field, cannot accommodate cases in which the biological substrate of consciousness is divided and yet each fragment sustains genuine awareness.

definition: An agent S intends to bring about a state of affairs P if and only if (i) S has formed a plan whose execution would produce P, (ii) S is prepared to execute the plan when the opportunity arises, and (iii) S regards P as a goal to be achieved rather than merely a foreseeable side-effect of S's action.
conditions: S has formed a plan whose execution would produce P
S is prepared to execute the plan when the opportunity arises
S regards P as a goal to be achieved rather than merely a foreseeable side-effect
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer who forms a plan to reach the summit and is fully prepared to execute it. She regards reaching the summit as her goal. Yet halfway through the ascent, deteriorating weather compels her to abandon the attempt and descend. She never withdraws her intention; the circumstances simply render execution impossible. At the moment of retreat, all three conditions remain satisfied — she has a plan, she is prepared to act, and she regards the summit as her goal — yet she is no longer in any meaningful sense intending to reach the summit. She is intending to survive.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the circumstances remain such that execution of the plan is feasible, so that an intention persists even when its realisation has become practically impossible and the agent has shifted to an entirely different course of action.
passage: One might propose that intending to bring about a state of affairs consists in having formed a plan for producing it, being prepared to execute the plan, and regarding the outcome as a goal rather than a mere side-effect. This analysis captures the purposive and forward-looking character of intention and distinguishes it from the foresight of unintended consequences. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when circumstances overtake the agent's plan. Consider a mountaineer who has formed a detailed plan for reaching a remote summit. She is prepared to execute it and regards the summit as her goal. Halfway through the ascent, a violent storm closes in, rendering further progress impossible. She begins a difficult descent to save her life. At this moment, the three conditions of the definition are still formally satisfied: she has a plan for reaching the summit, she was prepared to execute it, and she regards the summit as her goal — she has not renounced the ambition but merely postponed it. Yet it would be peculiar to say that she now intends to reach the summit. Her present intention is to descend safely. It appears that the definition, by confining itself to the agent's mental states and dispositional preparedness, cannot accommodate the role that external feasibility plays in the constitution of intention. One might observe that an intention is not merely a plan and a disposition but a commitment that is sensitive to the practical landscape in which it must be realised.

definition: A subject S is experiencing genuine grief if and only if (i) S has suffered the loss of a person or object to which S was emotionally attached, (ii) S feels a painful affect — sadness, heaviness, or desolation — in response to the loss, and (iii) S's painful affect persists over an extended period and is not merely a momentary pang.
conditions: S has suffered the loss of a person or object to which S was emotionally attached
S feels a painful affect in response to the loss
S's painful affect persists over an extended period
counterexample: Consider a man whose elderly father dies after a long and difficult illness. The man has been caring for his father for years and was deeply attached to him. Yet upon the father's death, the man feels not sadness but a profound and settled relief — relief that his father's suffering has ended, relief that the exhausting burden of care has been lifted. He does not feel the painful affect that condition (ii) requires, and it does not develop over subsequent weeks. Yet we should hesitate to deny that he grieves. His grief manifests instead as a quiet withdrawal from social life, an inability to concentrate, and an intermittent sense that the world has become less substantial.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that grief may manifest through affective states other than painful sadness — through numbness, relief, disorientation, or a diffuse sense of unreality — so that the requirement of a particular hedonic valence is too narrow.
passage: One might analyse grief as a persistent painful affect arising from the loss of an object of emotional attachment. This analysis captures the paradigmatic cases: the widow who weeps for months, the child bereft of a beloved companion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the complex affective responses to the death of a person whose final years were marked by prolonged suffering. Consider a man who has cared for his elderly father through a protracted and debilitating illness. He was deeply attached to his father and visited daily, attending to the most intimate requirements of care. When the father dies, the man feels not sadness but a profound and settled relief — relief that the suffering has ended, relief that the gruelling routine of care is over. He does not experience the painful affect that the definition requires. Yet one might observe that this man grieves. He withdraws from social life. He finds it difficult to concentrate on ordinary tasks. He experiences intermittent episodes of disorientation, as though something fundamental in the structure of his daily existence has been removed. These manifestations persist for months. It appears that the definition, by requiring a painful affect of a particular character, cannot accommodate the forms of grief that express themselves not as sadness but as numbness, relief, or a pervasive sense of absence. Grief, it seems, is more various in its phenomenology than the definition allows.

definition: A subject S perceives an object O if and only if (i) O exists and is present in S's environment, (ii) S has a sensory experience whose content represents O, and (iii) S's sensory experience is caused, by a normal causal chain, by O itself.
conditions: O exists and is present in S's environment
S has a sensory experience whose content represents O
S's sensory experience is caused by a normal causal chain originating in O
counterexample: Consider a birdwatcher who hears a distinctive call in the forest and forms a vivid sensory experience as of a golden oriole singing nearby. There is indeed a golden oriole in her vicinity, and the sound she hears is in fact caused by that bird through the normal causal chain of acoustic transmission. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet what she hears is actually the oriole's alarm call, which she has misidentified as a song. She perceives the bird, by the definition, yet her experience misrepresents a fundamental feature of what she perceives — the character of its vocalisation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the content of S's experience accurately represent the relevant properties of O, so that a subject may count as perceiving an object even when her experience profoundly mischaracterises it.
passage: One might characterise perception as a sensory experience whose content represents an existing object, caused through a normal causal chain by that object. This proposal captures the realist intuition that perception puts us in contact with mind-independent objects and the causal requirement that distinguishes perception from hallucination. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the domain of auditory experience. Consider a birdwatcher walking through a deciduous wood who hears a bright, fluting sound that she identifies as the song of a golden oriole. A golden oriole is indeed present in the canopy nearby. The sound she hears is caused, through the normal acoustic pathway, by that very bird. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet what the birdwatcher hears is not the oriole's song but its alarm call — a vocalisation with a quite different biological function and acoustic character, which she has misidentified owing to inexperience. She perceives the bird, by the definition, but her experience misrepresents a fundamental property of the sound it produces. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only that the experience represent the object and be appropriately caused by it, cannot distinguish perception that accurately characterises its object from perception that, while causally connected to the right source, profoundly misrepresents what it encounters. It appears that a satisfactory account of perception must attend not only to the causal link between object and experience but to the accuracy of the experiential content.

definition: A person P at time t2 is numerically identical to a person Q at time t1 if and only if (i) P possesses a continuous stream of memories linking t2 to t1, (ii) P's personality and character traits exhibit continuity with those of Q, and (iii) P would sincerely avow being the same person as Q.
conditions: P possesses a continuous stream of memories linking t2 to t1
P's personality and character traits exhibit continuity with those of Q
P would sincerely avow being the same person as Q
counterexample: Consider an elderly woman in the advanced stages of a progressive dementia. She possesses no memories of her earlier life, her personality and character have altered beyond recognition, and she does not avow being the person she once was — she does not even recognise her own name. None of the three conditions is satisfied. Yet her children, her physicians, and the law all treat her as the same person who, decades earlier, married, raised a family, and pursued a career. One might observe that denying her identity with that earlier person would have consequences that are both practically untenable and morally troubling.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to allow for biological or bodily continuity as a basis for personal identity, so that a person who has lost all psychological continuity through neurological disease is excluded from being identical with her former self despite unbroken physical continuity.
passage: One might propose that personal identity over time consists in a continuous stream of memories, continuity of personality and character, and the sincere avowal of sameness. This psychological-continuity analysis captures the intuition that what makes a person the same over time is the persistence of a distinctive mental life. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is provided by the phenomenon of advanced progressive dementia. Consider an elderly woman who, decades earlier, was a distinguished historian with a sharp wit, a deep love of music, and a formidable memory. She is now in the advanced stages of a neurodegenerative illness. She possesses no memories of her earlier life. Her personality has been transformed: the wit has vanished, the love of music has given way to indifference, and her character exhibits none of the traits by which she was formerly recognised. She does not avow being the person she once was, for she cannot understand the question. None of the three conditions of the definition is satisfied. Yet her children visit her, her physicians treat her medical history as continuous with that of the younger woman, and the law regards her as the same person who holds title to a house and a pension. One might observe that denying her identity with her former self would have consequences that are both practically unworkable and morally troubling — it would amount to declaring that the earlier person has died while a stranger inhabits her body. It appears that the definition, by resting identity entirely upon psychological continuity, cannot accommodate cases in which that continuity is destroyed while physical continuity persists.

definition: A subject S desires that P if and only if (i) S has a representational state whose content is that P obtains, (ii) S is disposed to experience satisfaction if P comes about, and (iii) S is disposed to experience frustration if P does not come about.
conditions: S has a representational state whose content is that P obtains
S is disposed to experience satisfaction if P comes about
S is disposed to experience frustration if P does not come about
counterexample: Consider a recovering addict who desires not to take the drug to which she was formerly addicted. She has a clear representational state whose content is that she remains abstinent. She is disposed to experience satisfaction upon maintaining her sobriety. She is disposed to experience frustration if she relapses. All three conditions are satisfied with respect to the desire for abstinence. Yet she simultaneously experiences a powerful craving for the drug — a desire in every phenomenological sense — whose satisfaction would produce not satisfaction but shame and self-reproach. The craving fails condition (ii): she is not disposed to experience satisfaction if the craving is fulfilled. Yet we should hesitate to deny that the craving is a desire.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate desires that the subject does not endorse and whose fulfilment would produce not satisfaction but distress, thereby conflating the motivational force of desire with the subject's evaluative attitude towards its object.
passage: One might analyse desire as a representational state whose content is that some state of affairs obtains, accompanied by dispositions to experience satisfaction upon its realisation and frustration upon its non-realisation. This analysis captures the motivational and affective dimensions that are commonly associated with desire. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the psychology of addiction. Consider a woman who has been sober for several months after a prolonged period of dependence upon a powerful narcotic. She desires, clearly and reflectively, to remain abstinent. This desire satisfies all three conditions: she represents abstinence as something to be maintained, she is disposed to feel satisfaction if she succeeds, and she is disposed to feel frustration if she fails. Yet she also experiences an intense craving for the drug — a state that possesses all the phenomenological marks of desire. The craving has representational content: it represents the taking of the drug as something to be done. It disposes her to action: she feels drawn towards the substance. But she is not disposed to experience satisfaction if the craving is fulfilled. On the contrary, she would feel profound shame and self-reproach. Condition (ii) is not met for the craving. Yet one might observe that denying the craving the status of a desire is deeply counterintuitive. It appears that the definition, by requiring a disposition to satisfaction, cannot accommodate the phenomenon of unwanted desire — desire that the subject recognises, opposes, and would be distressed to see fulfilled.

definition: A subject S has an episodic memory of an event E if and only if (i) S experienced E at some earlier time, (ii) S's present mental state includes a representation of E that preserves the phenomenal character of the original experience, and (iii) S is aware that the representation pertains to S's own past.
conditions: S experienced E at some earlier time
S's present mental state includes a representation of E that preserves the phenomenal character of the original experience
S is aware that the representation pertains to S's own past
counterexample: Consider a man who vividly recalls attending his fifth birthday party: the candles on the cake, the faces of the guests, the colour of the balloons. He is aware that this representation pertains to his own past. Yet careful investigation reveals that the man was not present at the party — he was ill and remained in bed. His vivid memory has been constructed over decades from family photographs, his mother's stories, and his own imaginative embellishment. He experienced no such event, yet his mental state has all the phenomenal marks of genuine episodic memory.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S's present representation be causally connected to the original experience in the right way, so that representations constructed from testimony, photographs, and imagination are indistinguishable from genuine episodic memories.
passage: One might characterise episodic memory as a present representation of a past event that preserves its phenomenal character and that the subject recognises as belonging to her own history. This analysis captures the distinctive first-personal and experiential quality that distinguishes episodic memory from mere factual recall. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the phenomenon of confabulated memory. Consider a man who, at the age of forty, vividly recalls his fifth birthday party. He remembers the candles flickering on the cake, the faces of the guests arranged around the table, the colour of the balloons that decorated the room. He is aware that this representation pertains to his own past and can locate it precisely in the chronology of his life. His mental state possesses the phenomenal richness and first-personal character that the definition demands. Yet careful investigation by a family member reveals that the man was not present at the party. He had fallen ill the previous day and spent his birthday in bed. His vivid memory has been constructed, over decades, from family photographs he has seen many times, stories told by his mother, and his own imaginative embellishment. Condition (i) is not satisfied — he did not experience the event — yet his mental state is indistinguishable, from the inside, from a genuine episodic memory. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the phenomenal character of the representation and the subject's self-attribution, cannot distinguish genuine memory from confabulation that has been elaborated over a lifetime into something experientially indistinguishable from the real thing.

definition: An agent S acts of S's own free will in performing action A if and only if (i) S deliberates among alternative courses of action before performing A, (ii) S's deliberation is not overridden by compulsion, coercion, or irresistible impulse, and (iii) S endorses A as the action S most wishes to perform upon reflection.
conditions: S deliberates among alternative courses of action before performing A
S's deliberation is not overridden by compulsion, coercion, or irresistible impulse
S endorses A as the action S most wishes to perform upon reflection
counterexample: Consider a man who deliberates carefully between two career paths, free from any compulsion or coercion, and reflectively endorses his choice to become a barrister. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet his deliberation has been decisively shaped by a hypnotic suggestion implanted during a therapeutic session years earlier, of which he is entirely unaware. The suggestion instilled a powerful preference for the legal profession that operates below the threshold of conscious reflection. He endorses his choice because the implanted preference has structured his evaluative landscape without his ever having the opportunity to examine it.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the processes underlying S's deliberation be transparent to S or at least not the product of manipulation that bypasses S's rational faculties, so that endorsement of a choice shaped by covert external influence counts as free.
passage: One might propose that acting of one's own free will consists in deliberating among alternatives, free from compulsion or irresistible impulse, and reflectively endorsing the chosen course of action. This analysis captures the deliberative and self-reflective dimensions of free agency and rightly excludes action performed under duress or overwhelming compulsion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when the processes that shape deliberation have themselves been manipulated. Consider a young man who deliberates carefully between two career paths — law and medicine. He weighs the advantages and disadvantages of each with admirable thoroughness. No one coerces him. No irresistible impulse drives him. After mature reflection, he endorses his choice to become a barrister as the course of action he most wishes to pursue. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet suppose that several years earlier, during a course of hypnotherapy for an unrelated anxiety, a suggestion was implanted that instilled a powerful and persistent preference for the legal profession. The man is entirely unaware of this suggestion. It operates below the threshold of conscious reflection, colouring his evaluation of the alternatives without his ever recognising its influence. He endorses his choice because the implanted preference has shaped his evaluative landscape from beneath. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the surface of deliberation and the sincerity of endorsement, cannot detect cases in which the deliberative process itself has been covertly structured by an external intervention that the agent has had no opportunity to examine.

definition: A subject S performs an action A voluntarily if and only if (i) S is aware of performing A at the time of its performance, (ii) S could have refrained from A had S so chosen, and (iii) A is performed in the absence of external physical compulsion.
conditions: S is aware of performing A at the time of its performance
S could have refrained from A had S so chosen
A is performed in the absence of external physical compulsion
counterexample: Consider a person suffering from a neurological condition that causes her hand to reach out and grasp nearby objects without her willing it. She is fully aware that her hand is grasping the object — she watches it happen and can describe the movement as it occurs. No external physical compulsion is present; the movement originates entirely within her own nervous system. She could, with considerable effort, refrain from the grasp by sitting on her hand. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the grasping is not voluntary — it is a pathological reflex over which she has no ordinary volitional control.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the action originate from the subject's own volitional processes — that it be initiated by a decision or intention of the agent rather than by a pathological neural mechanism, even if the agent is aware of it and could in principle suppress it.
passage: One might characterise voluntary action as action performed with awareness, in the absence of external compulsion, and with the capacity to refrain. This analysis captures the conditions that ordinarily distinguish what we do from what merely happens to us. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in certain neurological conditions. Consider a patient afflicted with a disorder that causes her hand to reach out and grasp any object placed within reach, independently of her will. She is fully aware of the movement — she watches her hand close around the object and can describe the grasping as it occurs. No external force compels the movement; it arises entirely from processes within her own nervous system. She can, with considerable muscular effort, suppress the reflex by sitting on her hand or gripping the arm of her chair. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied: she is aware of the action, she could refrain from it, and no external compulsion is present. Yet one might observe that the grasping is not voluntary in any ordinary sense. She does not decide to grasp the object; the movement is initiated by a pathological neural mechanism that bypasses her volitional processes. It appears that the definition, by attending to awareness, capacity to refrain, and absence of external compulsion, cannot distinguish genuine voluntary action from pathological movements that the subject is aware of and can with effort suppress but does not initiate.

definition: A sensation S is painful if and only if (i) S has a negative hedonic valence — that is, it is intrinsically unpleasant, (ii) S motivates the subject to take steps to terminate or avoid it, and (iii) the subject would prefer not to be in S, all else being equal.
conditions: S has a negative hedonic valence
S motivates the subject to take steps to terminate or avoid it
The subject would prefer not to be in S, all else being equal
counterexample: Consider a long-distance runner in the final mile of a race who experiences an intense burning sensation in her legs. The sensation has a negative hedonic valence — it is intrinsically unpleasant. It motivates her towards action — but the action it motivates is not termination but continuation, for she interprets the burn as a sign that she is pushing to her limit. She does not prefer to be without it; she actively seeks it as evidence that she is racing well. Conditions (i) is met, but (ii) and (iii) are not, despite the fact that the sensation is unambiguously painful.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish the intrinsic phenomenal character of pain from the subject's motivational and preferential response to it, so that a sensation which is experientially painful but motivationally sought or valued is excluded from the class of painful sensations.
passage: One might characterise painful sensation by its negative hedonic valence, its motivational tendency towards avoidance, and the subject's preference to be rid of it. This analysis brings together the phenomenal, motivational, and evaluative dimensions that are commonly associated with pain. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the context of endurance athletics. Consider a long-distance runner in the final mile of a competitive race. She experiences an intense burning sensation in the muscles of her legs — a sensation that is, by any ordinary reckoning, painful. The negative hedonic valence is present: the sensation is intrinsically unpleasant. Yet the motivational component diverges from what the definition predicts. The burning does not motivate her to stop running; on the contrary, she interprets it as confirmation that she is performing at her limit and presses harder. She does not prefer to be without the sensation. She has, in training and in previous races, come to associate this particular burn with peak effort, and she actively courts it. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are not satisfied. Nevertheless, it would be peculiar to deny that the sensation is painful. It hurts — that much is phenomenologically undeniable. One might observe that the definition, by tying the concept of pain to motivational avoidance and preference for its cessation, conflates the intrinsic character of a sensation with the subject's trained response to it. An experienced athlete may embrace what is painful precisely because it is painful, and the definition cannot accommodate this possibility.

definition: A mental state M of a subject S is a conscious state if and only if (i) S undergoes M, (ii) S is aware that S is undergoing M, and (iii) M has a determinate qualitative character for S.
conditions: S undergoes M
S is aware that S is undergoing M
M has a determinate qualitative character for S
counterexample: Suppose that a patient under light anaesthesia experiences acute pain in her left hand — a state with a vivid qualitative character — and is, in a certain attenuated sense, aware of this experience, as evidenced by her withdrawal reflex and elevated heart rate. Yet upon waking she has no recollection of the pain, no capacity to report it, and would sincerely deny that she felt anything during the procedure. We should hesitate to say that this was a conscious state in the fullest sense, for it lacked the sort of access that would make it available for verbal report, reasoning, or voluntary action.
missing_condition: The definition omits any requirement that the subject have access to the state in a manner that makes it available for report, reasoning, or the rational governance of behaviour — mere awareness and qualitative character are insufficient without this further condition of accessibility.
passage: One might propose that a mental state is conscious if and only if it is undergone by a subject who is aware of it and it possesses a determinate qualitative character. This analysis has considerable initial plausibility, for it captures the two features most commonly associated with consciousness: the subjective awareness of undergoing the state and the felt quality of the experience. Yet an instance that tells against the proposed definition may be constructed from the clinical literature on anaesthesia. Suppose a patient under light sedation experiences acute pain in her left hand. The state has, we may grant, a vivid qualitative character — it is, from the inside, as painful as any unsedated episode of comparable tissue damage. Moreover, the patient is in some attenuated sense aware of the experience: her autonomic nervous system responds with increased heart rate and a withdrawal reflex, suggesting that the pain is registered somewhere in her cognitive economy. Nevertheless, upon waking, the patient has no recollection of having felt anything whatsoever. She cannot report the pain, cannot reason about it, and would sincerely deny that any such experience occurred. It appears, then, that one should be reluctant to classify this as a conscious state in the fullest philosophical sense. What is missing is a condition of access — the requirement that the qualitative state be available to the subject for verbal report, deliberation, and the rational governance of voluntary action. Without such a condition, the proposed definition admits states that, whilst possessing qualitative character and a minimal form of awareness, fail to participate in the cognitive life of the subject in the manner characteristic of paradigmatic conscious experience.

definition: An agent A intends to perform an action X if and only if (i) A has formed a plan to do X, (ii) A has a desire that X be accomplished, and (iii) A believes that X is within A's power to perform.
conditions: A has formed a plan to do X
A has a desire that X be accomplished
A believes that X is within A's power to perform
counterexample: Consider a man who forms a plan to diet, desires to lose weight, and believes dieting is within his power. All three conditions are met. Yet when presented with a slice of cake, he eats it without hesitation. One might say that he intended to diet — he satisfies all three conditions — but that his intention was overridden by a contrary impulse. However, a stronger case reveals a deeper problem: consider a chess player who has planned a particular move, desires to make it, and believes she can — yet at the moment of action, moves a different piece absentmindedly. The original intention, though satisfying all conditions, was never translated into action, and the action she performed was unintended despite being deliberate in appearance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the plan, desire, and belief be operative at the moment of action — that they actually guide the agent's behaviour rather than merely coexisting in the agent's mind without exerting causal influence upon conduct.
passage: One might propose that an agent intends to perform an action when she has formed a plan to do it, desires that it be accomplished, and believes it is within her power. This analysis captures the deliberative character of intention: the formation of a plan directed towards a desired and achievable goal. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the phenomenology of absentminded action. Consider a chess player who has planned a particular move — advancing her knight to a strategically advantageous square. She desires to make this move and believes it is within her power. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet at the moment of action, she absentmindedly moves a different piece — her bishop — to a square she had not planned upon. Her original intention, though satisfying every stated condition, was not operative at the moment of action; it existed in her mind but failed to guide her hand. The move she actually made was unintended, despite appearing deliberate to an observer. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the existence of a plan, a desire, and a belief, without requiring that these mental states be operative at the moment of action — that they actually guide and control the agent's behaviour — cannot distinguish between a live intention that governs conduct and a dormant intention that coexists with contrary action.

definition: A person P₁ at time t₁ is the same person as P₂ at time t₂ if and only if (i) P₂ remembers experiences that P₁ had, (ii) there is psychological continuity between P₁ and P₂, and (iii) P₁ and P₂ share the same body.
conditions: P₂ remembers experiences that P₁ had
There is psychological continuity between P₁ and P₂
P₁ and P₂ share the same body
counterexample: Consider a person suffering from severe amnesia who cannot remember any experiences from before the onset of the condition. The first condition is violated — P₂ has no memories of P₁'s experiences. Psychological continuity may be partially maintained through personality traits and habits, but the memory link is severed. Yet the amnesiac is legally, socially, and intuitively regarded as the same person. She retains her name, her property, her obligations, and her relationships.
missing_condition: The definition makes memory a necessary condition of personal identity but fails to account for the persistence of identity through memory loss — it cannot accommodate amnesia, dreamless sleep, or any state in which experiential memories are absent whilst identity is preserved by other factors such as bodily continuity and character.
passage: One might define personal identity over time as requiring memory of past experiences, psychological continuity, and bodily sameness. This analysis combines the two most prominent philosophical traditions: the psychological criterion and the bodily criterion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the memory condition may be drawn from the phenomenon of amnesia. Consider a person who suffers a severe injury resulting in total retrograde amnesia. She cannot remember any experience from before the injury — not her childhood, not her education, not her marriage, not the events of yesterday. The first condition is plainly violated. Psychological continuity may be partially maintained through persistent personality traits, habitual dispositions, and emotional temperament, but the experiential memory link is completely severed. Yet the amnesiac is regarded — by the law, by her family, by her community, and by common intuition — as the same person she was before the injury. She retains her name, her property, her legal obligations, and her relationships. It appears that the definition, by making memory a necessary condition, cannot accommodate the persistence of personal identity through memory loss. One might observe that identity can survive the destruction of memory, suggesting that memory is a sufficient but not a necessary condition of personal identity.

definition: An action X is performed freely by an agent A if and only if (i) A could have done otherwise than X, (ii) A's performance of X was not determined by external compulsion, and (iii) A deliberated before performing X.
conditions: A could have done otherwise than X
A's performance of X was not determined by external compulsion
A deliberated before performing X
counterexample: Consider a person who witnesses a child drowning and leaps into the water to save her without a moment's deliberation. The action was not preceded by deliberation — it was immediate and spontaneous. The third condition is violated. Yet the action appears to be paradigmatically free: the rescuer was not compelled, could have refrained, and acted from her own compassionate character. We should hesitate to deny that this was a free action merely because it was not deliberated.
