definition: A system X is conscious if and only if (i) X processes information from its environment, (ii) X modifies its behaviour in response to that information, and (iii) X produces reports about its own internal states.
conditions: X processes information from its environment
X modifies its behaviour in response to that information
X produces reports about its own internal states
counterexample: Consider a modern thermostat equipped with a digital display and a verbal announcement system. The thermostat processes information from its environment — it measures the ambient temperature. It modifies its behaviour in response to that information — it activates heating or cooling systems when the temperature deviates from a set point. And it produces reports about its own internal states — it displays the message 'Currently cooling' or announces 'Temperature target reached.' All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet we should be most reluctant to ascribe consciousness to a thermostat. It lacks any inner experience, any felt quality, any subjective perspective on the world. Its information processing is entirely mechanical, and its self-reports are generated by simple programmed rules without any accompanying awareness.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the system's information processing be accompanied by subjective experience — by a felt, qualitative character that constitutes an inner perspective. Without this condition, the definition cannot distinguish genuinely conscious systems from mechanical devices that process information and generate self-reports without any accompanying awareness.
passage: One might propose that consciousness consists in the processing of environmental information, the modification of behaviour in response to that information, and the production of reports about one's own internal states. This functionalist characterisation captures the input-processing, behavioural-output, and self-monitoring features that many have associated with conscious systems. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from familiar technology. Consider a thermostat fitted with a digital display and a verbal announcement mechanism. The thermostat processes information from its environment: its sensor measures the ambient temperature with considerable precision. It modifies its behaviour in response to that information: when the temperature rises above a threshold, it activates the cooling system; when it falls below another, it engages the heater. And it produces reports about its own internal states: it displays messages such as 'Currently cooling' or announces 'Temperature target reached.' All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet one might observe that no reasonable person would ascribe consciousness to the thermostat. It has no inner life, no felt quality of warmth or cold, no subjective perspective upon the world. Its information processing is entirely mechanical, governed by simple conditional rules, and its self-reports are generated without any accompanying awareness of what they signify. It appears that the definition, by attending only to functional characteristics — information processing, behavioural modification, and self-reporting — fails to require the presence of subjective experience. One is led to conclude that consciousness involves something beyond functional organisation: a qualitative, felt dimension that no purely behavioural or functional criterion can capture.

definition: A person P at time t2 is the same person as Q at time t1 if and only if (i) P and Q share the same continuously existing body, (ii) there is spatiotemporal continuity between Q's body at t1 and P's body at t2, and (iii) the body in question has not undergone total replacement of its material constituents between t1 and t2.
conditions: P and Q share the same continuously existing body
There is spatiotemporal continuity between Q's body at t1 and P's body at t2
The body has not undergone total replacement of its material constituents between t1 and t2
counterexample: Consider the well-established biological fact that the material constituents of the human body are almost entirely replaced over a period of roughly seven to ten years through the ordinary processes of metabolism. A man at the age of forty shares the same continuously existing body as the boy he was at ten; there is unbroken spatiotemporal continuity between the boy's body and the man's. Yet the material constituents of the man's body are almost entirely different from those of the boy's body. The third condition is violated. Nevertheless, no one would deny that the man is the same person as the boy. The case reveals that requiring the persistence of material constituents is too strict a condition for personal identity.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that bodily identity is compatible with the gradual replacement of material constituents, provided the replacement proceeds through normal biological processes that maintain the body's functional and organisational continuity. A condition requiring continuity of organisation and function, rather than persistence of particular material constituents, is needed.
passage: One might propose that personal identity over time consists in the persistence of the same continuously existing body, spatiotemporal continuity of that body, and the retention of its material constituents. This bodily criterion of identity has the appeal of grounding personal identity in something concrete and observable. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from elementary biology. It is well established that the material constituents of the human body are almost entirely replaced over a period of approximately seven to ten years through the ordinary processes of metabolism: old cells die and are replaced by new ones, molecules are exchanged with the environment, and the atoms composing the body at one time are largely different from those composing it a decade later. Consider a man at the age of forty. He shares the same continuously existing body as the boy he was at ten; there is unbroken spatiotemporal continuity between the boy's body and the man's body. The first two conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet the material constituents of the man's body are almost entirely different from those of the boy's. The atoms that composed the boy have long since dispersed into the environment and been replaced by others. The third condition is violated. Nevertheless, no one would deny that the man is the same person as the boy he once was. It appears that the definition, by requiring the persistence of material constituents, sets a condition that is violated by every human life of sufficient duration. One is led to conclude that bodily identity does not require the retention of particular material constituents but rather the continuity of biological organisation and function through which the gradual replacement of constituents proceeds.

definition: A mental state M is a conscious experience if and only if (i) M has a qualitative character that is available to the subject from the first-person perspective, (ii) the subject is aware of being in state M at the time M occurs, and (iii) M is integrated into the subject's unified field of awareness such that it can influence other concurrent mental states.
conditions: M has a qualitative character available to the subject from the first-person perspective
The subject is aware of being in state M at the time M occurs
M is integrated into the subject's unified field of awareness such that it can influence other concurrent mental states
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man is absorbed in a difficult mathematical proof. He has been sitting in a room where a clock ticks steadily. He has qualitative auditory experience of the ticking, for he can report it if asked; he is aware of the sound at the time it occurs; and the ticking is integrated into his unified field of awareness, occasionally prompting annoyance that influences his concentration. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet upon reflection he realises he has not truly experienced the ticking as such but has rather been automatically registering a background stimulus. When someone enters the room and stops the clock, he notices its absence with a start, revealing that the ticking was processed as a mere sensory signal rather than as an experience with the richness we ordinarily attribute to conscious awareness.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the subject attend to M in a manner that constitutes genuine phenomenal engagement, as distinct from the mere automatic registration of a stimulus that satisfies the formal criteria of awareness and integration without possessing the full character of conscious experience.
passage: One might propose that a mental state qualifies as a conscious experience when it possesses qualitative character available from the first-person perspective, when the subject is aware of it at the time of its occurrence, and when it is integrated into the subject's unified field of awareness so as to be capable of influencing other concurrent mental states. This characterisation captures what we ordinarily mean when we distinguish conscious experiences from unconscious processes. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the familiar phenomenon of background perception. Suppose a man is absorbed in a difficult mathematical proof whilst sitting in a room where a clock ticks steadily. He has qualitative auditory experience of the ticking, for if questioned he can report the sound without hesitation. He is aware of the ticking at the time it occurs, as demonstrated by the fact that it occasionally provokes mild annoyance. And the ticking is integrated into his unified field of awareness, for the annoyance it causes influences his concentration upon the proof. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to say that the man genuinely experiences the ticking in the manner we associate with conscious awareness. When someone enters the room and stops the clock, he notices the absence with a start, as though a feature of his environment had changed without his having properly attended to it. It appears that the ticking was processed as a background signal, automatically registered yet lacking the fullness of phenomenal engagement that we ordinarily attribute to conscious experience. The definition, by requiring only qualitative character, concurrent awareness, and integrative influence, fails to distinguish between the genuine phenomenal richness of conscious experience and the mere automatic processing of stimuli that can mimic all three conditions without achieving the depth we intuitively demand.

definition: An agent A intends to perform action X if and only if (i) A has a mental representation of X as a future action to be performed by A, (ii) A believes that it is within A's power to bring about X, and (iii) A has formed a commitment to performing X that guides A's practical reasoning and planning.
conditions: A has a mental representation of X as a future action to be performed by A
A believes that it is within A's power to bring about X
A has formed a commitment to performing X that guides A's practical reasoning and planning
counterexample: Suppose a man mentally represents himself posting a letter tomorrow, believes it is within his power to do so, and has formed a commitment to posting the letter that guides his planning: he places the letter by the door, sets an alarm, and arranges his morning schedule accordingly. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the man has formed this commitment only because his wife asked him to do it, and he harbours a settled though unacknowledged desire to forget. He has arranged everything so as to post the letter, yet his planning is the product of social compliance rather than of genuine practical endorsement. When the alarm sounds, he finds that he has no inclination to carry out the action, and he realises that his so-called commitment was merely the appearance of intention produced by external pressure.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that A's commitment to X arise from A's own practical endorsement of the action, as distinguished from a merely externally induced disposition to plan for X that lacks the motivational quality characteristic of genuine intention.
passage: One might analyse intention as consisting in a mental representation of a future action, a belief in one's capacity to perform it, and a commitment to its performance that guides one's practical reasoning. This analysis captures the forward-looking, deliberative character that we take to be essential to intention. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from ordinary social life. Suppose a man mentally represents himself posting a letter on the morrow, believes it is well within his power, and has formed what appears to be a genuine commitment to the task: he places the letter by the front door, sets an alarm for the morning, and rearranges his schedule accordingly. All three conditions are manifestly satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the man has arranged all of this only because his wife insisted upon it, and that he harbours a settled though unacknowledged desire to forget entirely. His planning is the product of social compliance rather than of any genuine practical endorsement on his part. When the alarm sounds, he finds himself entirely without inclination to act, and he recognises that what he had taken for a commitment was merely the outward machinery of intention produced by external pressure. It appears that the definition, by requiring only a mental representation, a belief in capacity, and a commitment that guides planning, neglects the requirement that the commitment arise from the agent's own practical endorsement of the action. Without this further condition, the analysis cannot distinguish between genuine intention and the merely externally induced simulation of intention that social life so readily produces.

definition: A person P at time t2 is identical to a person Q at time t1 if and only if (i) P has continuous memory of experiences had by Q, (ii) P's body is spatiotemporally continuous with Q's body, and (iii) P exhibits the same fundamental character traits and dispositions as Q.
conditions: P has continuous memory of experiences had by Q
P's body is spatiotemporally continuous with Q's body
P exhibits the same fundamental character traits and dispositions as Q
counterexample: Suppose a woman suffers a severe illness that leaves her with complete amnesia regarding one year of her life, the year during which she committed a grave moral offence. Her memories before and after that year are fully intact and continuous, her body is spatiotemporally continuous throughout, and her character traits and dispositions remain fundamentally unchanged. All three conditions are satisfied, for the continuity of memory is unbroken except for the gap corresponding to the missing year, and one might argue that the memories on either side of the gap form a continuous chain. Yet the woman has no recollection whatever of having committed the offence, and she cannot connect her present self with the agent who performed it. If personal identity is what grounds moral responsibility, the analysis yields the uncomfortable conclusion that she is identical with the offender whilst being wholly unable to recognise herself as such.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the memory continuity include specific recollection of morally or practically significant actions, so that the identity relation grounds not merely a formal chain of memories but the capacity for the person to recognise herself as the author of her past deeds.
passage: One might propose that personal identity over time consists in continuous memory, bodily spatiotemporal continuity, and the persistence of fundamental character traits. This characterisation captures the psychological and physical threads that we ordinarily regard as binding a person's earlier and later selves. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be imagined as follows. Suppose a woman suffers a severe illness that erases precisely one year of her memory, the year during which she committed a grave moral offence. Her memories before and after that year remain fully intact and form a continuous chain, her body is spatiotemporally continuous throughout, and her character traits and dispositions are fundamentally unchanged. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to treat this as a straightforward case of identity, for the woman has no recollection whatever of having committed the offence. She cannot connect her present self with the agent who performed the act. If personal identity is what grounds our practices of moral responsibility and self-recognition, then the analysis yields the uncomfortable result that she is identical with the offender yet wholly incapable of recognising herself as such. It appears that the definition, by requiring only continuous memory, bodily continuity, and persistence of character, neglects the requirement that the memory continuity encompass specific recollection of morally and practically significant actions, so that the identity relation grounds not merely a formal chain but the capacity for genuine self-recognition as the author of one's past deeds.

definition: An agent A acts freely in performing action X if and only if (i) A could have done otherwise than X under the same circumstances, (ii) A's performance of X is caused by A's own deliberation and choice, and (iii) A's deliberation is not determined by factors external to A's rational faculties.
conditions: A could have done otherwise than X under the same circumstances
A's performance of X is caused by A's own deliberation and choice
A's deliberation is not determined by factors external to A's rational faculties
counterexample: Suppose a man deliberates about whether to donate money to a charity. He considers the arguments for and against, weighs his financial situation, and decides to donate. He could have done otherwise, for nothing constrained him. His action was caused by his own deliberation and choice. And his deliberation was not determined by factors external to his rational faculties, for no one coerced or manipulated him. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the man's rational faculties are themselves the product of a childhood in which he was systematically conditioned, through careful reward and punishment, to respond to charitable appeals with immediate generosity. His deliberation is genuinely his own, but the rational faculties that conduct it have been shaped so thoroughly by external conditioning that they could not have delivered any other verdict. He deliberates freely in the formal sense, yet the outcome was assured before deliberation began.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that A's rational faculties themselves be free from formative determination by external conditioning, so that the deliberation is not merely formally autonomous but conducted by faculties whose structure has not been predetermined to yield the very outcome that the agent believes she has freely chosen.
passage: One might define free action as action that the agent could have forgone, that is caused by the agent's own deliberation and choice, and whose deliberation is not determined by factors external to the agent's rational faculties. This characterisation captures the voluntariness, self-determination, and rational autonomy that we associate with freedom. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the domain of moral psychology. Suppose a man deliberates about whether to donate money to a charitable cause. He considers the arguments for and against, weighs his financial circumstances, and arrives at the decision to donate. He could have done otherwise, for nothing constrained him. His action was caused by his own deliberation. And no external factor determined his reasoning; he was neither coerced nor manipulated. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the man's rational faculties are themselves the product of a childhood during which he was systematically conditioned, through careful application of reward and punishment, to respond to charitable appeals with immediate generosity. His deliberation is, in the formal sense, entirely his own, yet the faculties that conduct it have been shaped so thoroughly by this external conditioning that they could not have produced any other verdict. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the agent could have done otherwise, that the action be caused by deliberation, and that the deliberation be free from external determination, fails to require that the rational faculties themselves be free from the kind of formative conditioning that predetermines their output. Without this further condition, we are led to count as free those actions whose outcomes were assured before deliberation began.

definition: A bodily movement B constitutes a genuine action if and only if (i) B is caused by a prior mental state of the agent, such as a desire or intention, (ii) B is performed voluntarily, in the sense that the agent could have refrained from B, and (iii) B is directed towards a goal that the agent represents to himself as the purpose of B.
conditions: B is caused by a prior mental state of the agent such as a desire or intention
B is performed voluntarily in the sense that the agent could have refrained from B
B is directed towards a goal that the agent represents to himself as the purpose of B
counterexample: Suppose a man desires to impress a dinner guest and forms the intention of reaching for the salt to season the guest's meal. He voluntarily extends his arm and his movement is directed towards the goal of seasoning the food. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the precise manner in which the desire causes the arm to extend is deviant: the desire makes him nervous, the nervousness causes a muscular spasm, and the spasm happens to propel his hand towards the salt shaker along exactly the trajectory he intended. The movement reaches the correct goal by a causal route that passes through physiological accident rather than through the normal exercise of motor control.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the causal chain connecting the agent's mental state to the bodily movement proceed through the normal channels of motor control and practical execution, rather than through an accidental or deviant causal route that happens to produce the intended result.
passage: One might propose that a bodily movement constitutes a genuine action when it is caused by a prior mental state of the agent, performed voluntarily, and directed towards a goal that the agent represents to himself as its purpose. This characterisation captures the intentional, voluntary, and purposive character that we ordinarily attribute to action. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomenon of deviant causation. Suppose a man desires to impress a dinner guest and forms the intention of reaching for the salt to season the guest's meal. He voluntarily extends his arm, and his movement is directed towards the acknowledged goal of seasoning the food. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the precise manner in which the desire causes the arm to extend is highly irregular. The desire makes the man nervous; the nervousness causes a muscular spasm; and the spasm happens, by physiological accident, to propel his hand towards the salt shaker along exactly the trajectory he intended. The movement arrives at the correct goal, but through a causal route that passes through involuntary physiological accident rather than through the normal exercise of motor control. It appears that the definition, by requiring only a causal connection to a mental state, voluntariness, and goal-directedness, fails to demand that the causal chain proceed through the normal channels by which agents execute their intentions. Without this requirement, we are compelled to count as genuine actions those movements that achieve their intended result by mere physiological coincidence.

definition: A mental state E is an emotion if and only if (i) E involves a felt qualitative character that is phenomenally distinct from mere sensation, (ii) E is directed towards an intentional object that the subject represents as the cause or occasion of E, and (iii) E disposes the subject towards characteristic patterns of behaviour relevant to the intentional object.
conditions: E involves a felt qualitative character phenomenally distinct from mere sensation
E is directed towards an intentional object that the subject represents as the cause or occasion of E
E disposes the subject towards characteristic patterns of behaviour relevant to the intentional object
counterexample: Suppose a woman takes a medication that produces, as a side effect, a state of intense agitation with a felt qualitative character phenomenally distinct from mere bodily sensation. She has recently quarrelled with her neighbour, and she represents the neighbour as the cause of her agitation, since the quarrel is the most salient recent event. The agitation disposes her towards characteristic behaviours relevant to the neighbour: she avoids him, speaks curtly, and feels an impulse to confront him. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the agitation is entirely pharmacological in origin and has nothing to do with the neighbour. The woman's representation of the neighbour as the cause is mistaken, and the behavioural dispositions are directed towards an object that is not in fact their source.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the intentional object the subject represents as the cause of E be in fact appropriately connected to the genesis of E, so that the emotion is not merely a pharmacologically or otherwise externally induced state that the subject misattributes to a convenient object.
passage: One might analyse emotion as a mental state possessing a felt qualitative character distinct from mere sensation, directed towards an intentional object that the subject represents as its cause, and disposing the subject towards characteristic patterns of behaviour relevant to that object. This analysis captures the phenomenal richness, intentional directedness, and behavioural import that we take to distinguish emotions from other mental states. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be imagined as follows. Suppose a woman takes a medication that produces, as an unintended side effect, a state of intense agitation possessing a qualitative character phenomenally distinct from mere bodily sensation. She has recently quarrelled with her neighbour, and because the quarrel is the most salient recent event in her life, she represents the neighbour as the cause of her agitation. The state disposes her towards characteristic behaviours relevant to the neighbour: she avoids him, speaks curtly when encounter is unavoidable, and feels an impulse to confront him about the quarrel. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to classify this as a genuine emotion directed at the neighbour, for the agitation is entirely pharmacological in origin and has nothing to do with him. The woman's representation of the neighbour as the cause is a confabulation prompted by the salience of the recent quarrel, and the behavioural dispositions are directed towards an object that is not in fact their source. It appears that the definition, by requiring only felt quality, intentional directedness, and behavioural disposition, neglects the requirement that the represented intentional object be appropriately connected to the actual genesis of the state.

definition: A subject S perceives an object O if and only if (i) O exists and is present in S's environment, (ii) S has a sensory experience that represents O as having certain properties, and (iii) S's sensory experience of O is caused, in the appropriate manner, by O itself.
conditions: O exists and is present in S's environment
S has a sensory experience that represents O as having certain properties
S's sensory experience of O is caused in the appropriate manner by O itself
counterexample: Suppose a man is looking at a red apple on a table. The apple exists and is present in his environment. He has a visual experience representing the apple as red and spherical. And his experience is caused, through the normal operation of light reflection and visual processing, by the apple itself. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet between the apple and the man's eyes there is a pane of tinted glass that shifts the apparent colour from red to orange. The man's experience represents the apple as orange, not red. The experience is caused by the apple and exists in the appropriate causal relation, yet it misrepresents the apple's colour. The man perceives the apple in the sense that the definition requires, yet his perception is systematically misleading.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S's sensory experience represent O's properties accurately, or at least that the causal process by which O produces the experience not introduce systematic distortion, so that the perception yields a veridical rather than a systematically misleading representation of the object.
passage: One might analyse perception as consisting in the existence of an object in the subject's environment, a sensory experience representing the object as possessing certain properties, and an appropriate causal connection between the object and the experience. This analysis captures the realist, representational, and causal features that we take to be central to perception. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be imagined as follows. Suppose a man is looking at a red apple resting upon a table. The apple exists and is present in his environment. He has a visual experience representing the apple as having a definite colour and shape. And his experience is caused, through the normal operation of light reflection and visual processing, by the apple itself. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, between the apple and the man's eyes there is a pane of tinted glass that shifts the apparent colour from red to orange. The man's experience represents the apple as orange, not red. The experience is caused by the apple through the appropriate causal pathway, yet the causal process introduces a systematic distortion. The man perceives the apple in the sense that the definition requires, yet his perception is systematically misleading. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the object's existence, a representing experience, and an appropriate causal connection, fails to demand that the causal process not introduce distortions that compromise the accuracy of the resulting representation.

definition: A subject S remembers an event E if and only if (i) E occurred in the past, (ii) S has a present mental representation that depicts E, and (iii) S's present representation of E is causally connected to S's original experience of E.
conditions: E occurred in the past
S has a present mental representation that depicts E
S's present representation of E is causally connected to S's original experience of E
counterexample: Suppose a woman experienced a particular sunset on holiday ten years ago. She now has a present mental representation depicting that sunset, and this representation is causally connected to her original experience through the normal processes of memory consolidation and retrieval. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet over the intervening decade, each time she recalled the sunset she unconsciously embellished the scene, adding a flock of birds that was never present and intensifying the colours beyond what she actually witnessed. Her present representation, though causally continuous with the original experience, now depicts a scene that significantly diverges from what actually occurred. She satisfies the definition of remembering, yet much of what she now represents never happened.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the present representation preserve the content of the original experience with sufficient fidelity, so that memory is not merely a causally connected representation but one that accurately reflects what was originally experienced.
passage: One might define memory as consisting in the past occurrence of an event, a present mental representation depicting that event, and a causal connection between the representation and the original experience. This characterisation captures the temporal, representational, and causal features that we ordinarily associate with remembering. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the well-documented phenomenon of memory distortion. Suppose a woman experienced a particular sunset on holiday ten years ago. She now has a vivid mental representation depicting the sunset, and this representation is causally connected to her original experience through the normal processes of memory consolidation and retrieval. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, over the intervening decade, each time she recalled the sunset she unconsciously embellished the scene, adding a flock of birds that was never present and intensifying the colours far beyond what she actually witnessed. Her present representation, though causally continuous with the original experience, now depicts a scene that significantly diverges from the event as it actually occurred. She satisfies the definition of remembering, yet much of what she now represents never happened. It appears that the definition, by requiring only past occurrence, present representation, and causal connection, neglects the requirement that the representation preserve the content of the original experience with sufficient fidelity. Without this condition, the analysis cannot distinguish genuine memory from the elaborate confabulations that the passage of time so readily produces.

definition: A creature C is conscious if and only if (i) C is capable of having states with qualitative phenomenal character, (ii) C exhibits flexible, context-sensitive behaviour that cannot be explained by simple stimulus-response mechanisms, and (iii) C can direct attention selectively to different features of its environment.
conditions: C is capable of having states with qualitative phenomenal character
C exhibits flexible context-sensitive behaviour not explainable by simple stimulus-response mechanisms
C can direct attention selectively to different features of its environment
counterexample: Suppose a sophisticated robot is designed so that its internal sensors generate states with qualitative phenomenal character, if indeed artificial states can have such character. The robot exhibits flexible, context-sensitive behaviour, adjusting its responses in complex ways that cannot be captured by simple stimulus-response rules. And it directs attention selectively, focusing its sensors on different environmental features depending on its current task. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet the robot has no unified subjective perspective: its various sensor systems operate in parallel without any integration into a single field of experience. There is no entity for whom it is like something to be that robot, despite the presence of phenomenal states, flexible behaviour, and selective attention.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that C's phenomenal states, flexible behaviour, and selective attention be unified in a single subjective perspective, so that there is an entity for whom these capacities collectively constitute a coherent experiential viewpoint on the world.
passage: One might propose that a creature is conscious when it possesses states with qualitative phenomenal character, exhibits flexible behaviour not reducible to simple stimulus-response mechanisms, and can direct attention selectively. This characterisation captures the phenomenal, behavioural, and attentional features that we commonly regard as marks of consciousness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be imagined from the domain of artificial systems. Suppose a sophisticated robot is designed so that its internal sensors generate states with qualitative phenomenal character, granting for the sake of argument that artificial states can possess such character. The robot exhibits flexible, context-sensitive behaviour, adjusting its responses in complex ways that defy description by simple stimulus-response rules. And it directs its sensors selectively towards different environmental features depending upon the demands of its current task. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the robot's various sensor systems operate entirely in parallel, without any integration into a single field of experience. There is no unified subjective perspective, no entity for whom it is like something to be that robot. The phenomenal states exist in disconnected streams, the flexible behaviour is produced by independent modules, and the selective attention operates without reference to a common experiential centre. It appears that the definition, by requiring only phenomenal character, flexible behaviour, and selective attention, fails to demand that these capacities be unified in a single subjective perspective. Without such unification, we have the components of consciousness without the coherent experiential viewpoint that we intuitively take consciousness to be.

definition: An agent A exercises free will in choosing between options X and Y if and only if (i) A is aware of both X and Y as genuine alternatives, (ii) A deliberates about the merits and demerits of each option, and (iii) A's choice is not the product of external compulsion.
conditions: A is aware of both X and Y as genuine alternatives
A deliberates about the merits and demerits of each option
A's choice is not the product of external compulsion
counterexample: Suppose a man is choosing between two career paths. He is aware of both as genuine alternatives, deliberates at length about the merits and demerits of each, and his choice is not the product of any external compulsion. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the man suffers from a severe and unrecognised phobia that makes one of the options psychologically intolerable, though he cannot articulate why. His deliberation proceeds as though both options are genuinely available, but the phobia ensures that his choice is a foregone conclusion. He exercises what appears to be free will whilst being internally compelled by a psychological condition of which he is unaware.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that A's deliberation be free from internal psychological compulsions that effectively predetermine the outcome, so that the choice is genuinely open not only in the sense of external freedom but in the sense of internal psychological availability of the alternatives.
passage: One might analyse free will as the exercise of choice between alternatives of which the agent is aware, about which the agent deliberates, and which is not produced by external compulsion. This analysis captures the awareness, reflectiveness, and voluntariness that we associate with the exercise of free will. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the domain of abnormal psychology. Suppose a man is choosing between two career paths. He is aware of both as genuine alternatives, deliberates at length about the merits of each, and his choice is entirely free from external compulsion. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, the man suffers from a severe and unrecognised phobia that renders one of the options psychologically intolerable, though he cannot articulate why he finds it so repellent. His deliberation proceeds as though both options were genuinely available, yet the phobia ensures that his choice is a foregone conclusion from the outset. He exercises what appears to be free will whilst being internally compelled by a condition of which he is entirely unaware. It appears that the definition, by requiring only awareness of alternatives, deliberation, and the absence of external compulsion, fails to require that the agent's deliberation be free from internal psychological compulsions that effectively predetermine the outcome. Without this requirement, the analysis counts as free those choices whose results were fixed before deliberation began by forces operating beneath the threshold of the agent's self-awareness.

definition: A mental state is conscious if and only if (i) the subject is aware of the state, (ii) the state has qualitative character, and (iii) the subject can report on the state's content.
conditions: the subject is aware of the state
the state has qualitative character
the subject can report on the state's content
counterexample: Consider a case in which a patient under partial anaesthesia experiences vivid qualitative states — sharp colours and a dull ache — and is demonstrably aware of them, as evidenced by involuntary physiological responses, yet owing to the pharmacological suppression of the speech and motor centres, she is wholly unable to report on the content of these states. The state possesses qualitative character and the subject is aware of it, yet the third condition fails. One would hesitate to deny that such a state is conscious merely because the avenue of report is blocked.
missing_condition: The definition conflates consciousness with the capacity for verbal or behavioural report, failing to distinguish between the presence of a conscious state and the ability to communicate its content.
passage: It has long been supposed that consciousness admits of a tolerably neat characterisation: a mental state is conscious provided the subject is aware of it, the state bears some qualitative character, and the subject is able to report upon its content. One might observe that these three conditions appear, at first blush, to capture what we ordinarily mean when we speak of a state as being conscious. Yet we should hesitate before pronouncing the analysis complete. Let us imagine a patient who, under the influence of a partial anaesthetic, retains vivid qualitative experience — she sees colours, feels a dull ache in her limbs — and whose autonomic responses confirm that she is aware of these states. Nevertheless, the pharmacological agent has suppressed those motor and linguistic capacities upon which any report must depend. She cannot speak, gesture, or write, and thus she satisfies the first two conditions whilst failing the third. It appears, however, that her states remain conscious in the fullest sense; the impediment lies not in consciousness but in the channel of expression. We are led to conclude that the capacity for report is not, after all, a necessary condition of consciousness, but rather a frequent concomitant whose absence does not extinguish the conscious character of the state.

definition: An agent intends to perform an action if and only if (i) the agent has a desire directed toward the action's outcome, (ii) the agent believes the action will bring about that outcome, and (iii) the agent has formed a plan to execute the action.
conditions: the agent has a desire directed toward the action's outcome
the agent believes the action will bring about that outcome
the agent has formed a plan to execute the action
counterexample: Suppose that a man desires to post a letter, believes that walking to the postbox will achieve this, and has formed a detailed plan to do so after lunch. Yet while crossing the street, he is startled by a loud noise and, in his fright, drops the letter into a drain. He possessed all three conditions at the relevant moment, yet his posting of the letter — which never occurred — cannot be said to have been an intended action. The conditions were met, but the intention was never carried through to completion owing to an interrupting cause entirely external to his deliberative structure.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the agent's desire, belief, and plan must causally guide the execution of the action in the right way, rather than being present merely at the moment of planning.
passage: One familiar analysis holds that intention consists in a conjunction of desire, belief, and plan: the agent desires a certain outcome, believes that a particular action will secure it, and has formed a plan for carrying the action out. It might be supposed that when these three elements converge, we have before us a genuine case of intention. Yet one may construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Let us imagine a man who, desiring to post a letter, believing that a walk to the postbox will achieve this end, and having laid a careful plan to do so after his midday meal, sets out upon his errand. As he crosses the road, a sudden loud report startles him, and in his alarm he drops the letter through the grating of a drain. Here all three conditions were satisfied at the moment of his departure, and yet the posting of the letter was never performed. The desire, the belief, and the plan were genuinely present, but an intervening cause disrupted the causal pathway from deliberation to action. We are thus brought to recognise that intention requires not merely the co-presence of these mental states but their playing the appropriate causal role in guiding behaviour to its completion.

definition: A person at time t2 is identical to a person at time t1 if and only if (i) the person at t2 has continuous memory of the experiences of the person at t1, (ii) the person at t2 possesses the same psychological character traits as the person at t1, and (iii) the person at t2 regards herself as the same individual as the person at t1.
conditions: the person at t2 has continuous memory of the experiences of the person at t1
the person at t2 possesses the same psychological character traits as the person at t1
the person at t2 regards herself as the same individual as the person at t1
counterexample: Consider a case in which a woman suffers a severe fever that obliterates all recollection of the first thirty years of her life. She retains her temperament, her characteristic habits of thought, and her self-regarding judgement that she is the same person she has always been. She satisfies the second and third conditions, yet fails the first entirely, possessing no continuous memory of her earlier experiences. It appears extravagant to deny that she is the same person merely because a pathological event has severed the thread of memory.
missing_condition: The definition makes continuous memory a necessary condition of personal identity, overlooking the possibility that identity may persist through bodily or psychological continuity even when memory is wholly disrupted.
passage: It has been a common proposal that personal identity across time rests upon three pillars: continuity of memory, sameness of psychological character, and the subject's own self-regarding judgement of identity. So stated, the analysis appears to capture the ordinary conviction that one remains the same person through the passage of years, provided one remembers one's past, retains one's disposition, and takes oneself to be the selfsame individual. Yet we should examine whether these conditions are truly necessary. Suppose that a woman of sixty suffers a catastrophic fever which destroys all recollection of her life before the age of thirty. She emerges from the illness with her temperament intact, her habits of reasoning unchanged, and a firm conviction that she is the person she has always been. The first condition is plainly unsatisfied: she has no continuous memory reaching back to her earlier experiences. Nevertheless, it appears that we should be reluctant to pronounce her a different person. Her friends recognise her; her behaviour conforms to patterns established decades earlier; and she herself entertains no doubt of her identity. One is thus led to suspect that memory, though ordinarily a reliable index of identity, is not among its necessary conditions, and that the analysis must be revised to accommodate those cases in which identity persists despite the wholesale loss of recollection.

definition: An agent acts freely if and only if (i) the agent could have acted otherwise under identical circumstances, (ii) the action proceeds from the agent's own deliberation, and (iii) no external force compels the action.
conditions: the agent could have acted otherwise under identical circumstances
the action proceeds from the agent's own deliberation
no external force compels the action
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man deliberates at length about whether to donate to a charitable cause and concludes, after careful reflection, that he ought to do so. No external force compels him. Yet unbeknownst to him, a neuroscientist has implanted a device that would have triggered the same decision had his natural deliberation led elsewhere. He satisfies the second and third conditions and, in the actual sequence of events, his action proceeds from his own reasoning. Whether he could have acted otherwise is doubtful, since the device would have intervened. Yet one might hesitate to deny that he acted freely, for the device played no actual role.
missing_condition: The definition demands the ability to do otherwise as a necessary condition, overlooking cases in which an agent's action issues from genuine deliberation and yet alternative possibilities are foreclosed by a dormant mechanism that never actually operates.
passage: A familiar philosophical proposal maintains that free action requires three things: that the agent could have acted otherwise in identical circumstances, that the action springs from the agent's own deliberation, and that no external force compels the behaviour in question. So stated, the conditions appear to constitute a plausible joint account of what it is to act freely. Yet one may devise a scenario that calls the first condition into question. Let us imagine a man who, after protracted deliberation, decides to make a generous donation to a charitable society. No external compulsion bears upon him; his reasoning is his own; and he arrives at his conclusion through the ordinary exercise of his reflective capacities. Unknown to him, however, a device has been surgically implanted in his brain by a neuroscientist, designed to trigger the very same decision should his natural processes of thought tend in any other direction. As it happens, the device remains dormant, for his deliberation leads him independently to the conclusion the device was designed to enforce. The first condition — the ability to have done otherwise — appears unsatisfied, for had his reasoning gone differently the device would have intervened. Yet one is strongly inclined to say that the man acted freely, since his choice flowed entirely from his own reflective judgement. We are thus led to suspect that the capacity to do otherwise, though often present in free action, may not be among its necessary conditions.

definition: A bodily movement constitutes an action if and only if (i) it is caused by the agent's intention, (ii) the agent is aware of performing the movement, and (iii) the movement is voluntary.
conditions: it is caused by the agent's intention
the agent is aware of performing the movement
the movement is voluntary
counterexample: Suppose that a pianist, in the course of performing a difficult passage, executes a series of rapid finger movements that she has practised to the point of automaticity. She intends to play the passage and the movements are voluntary, yet at the moment of execution she is not consciously aware of each individual finger movement. Nevertheless, each movement is unquestionably an action — indeed, a highly skilled one.
missing_condition: The definition requires concurrent awareness of the movement, overlooking habitual or highly practised actions in which the agent's intention operates through well-established motor routines without moment-by-moment conscious monitoring.
passage: A natural analysis of bodily action might hold that a movement counts as an action when it is caused by the agent's intention, the agent is aware of performing it, and it is voluntary. These three conditions seem, at first glance, to capture the distinction between mere bodily happenings — a twitch, a reflex — and genuine actions for which the agent may be held responsible. Yet one may observe that the second condition is more problematic than it initially appears. Let us imagine a concert pianist who has practised a demanding passage many thousands of times. When she performs it on stage, her fingers move with extraordinary precision, executing dozens of discrete movements per second. She intends to play the passage; the movements are wholly voluntary; and the result is a performance of the highest order. Yet she is not, at the moment of execution, consciously aware of each individual finger movement. Her attention is directed to the phrasing of the musical line, not to the mechanics of her hands. It appears, however, that each finger movement is an action — indeed, a supremely accomplished one — notwithstanding the absence of moment-by-moment awareness. We are thus led to recognise that concurrent awareness is not a necessary condition of action and that many of our most competent performances proceed through well-rehearsed motor routines that operate beneath the threshold of explicit consciousness.

definition: A mental state is an emotion if and only if (i) it involves a felt qualitative experience, (ii) it is directed toward an intentional object, and (iii) it disposes the subject to characteristic behavioural responses.
conditions: it involves a felt qualitative experience
it is directed toward an intentional object
it disposes the subject to characteristic behavioural responses
counterexample: Suppose that a man, upon receiving news of a distant relative's death, feels a sudden pang of sadness directed toward the loss of that relative and is disposed to weep. Yet before any behavioural response can manifest, he is distracted by an urgent demand upon his attention and the state passes without any outward expression whatever. The state satisfies the first two conditions — qualitative feeling and intentional directedness — and was disposed toward a behavioural response that was never actualised. If we insist upon the third condition strictly, we may doubt whether the state qualifies; yet it appears plainly to have been an emotion, albeit a fleeting one.
missing_condition: The definition requires a behavioural disposition but does not accommodate the possibility that an emotion may be genuine even when the disposition is present only momentarily and is extinguished before any characteristic behaviour occurs.
passage: One widely canvassed analysis of emotion holds that a mental state is an emotion when it involves a qualitative feel, is directed toward an intentional object, and disposes the subject to characteristic behavioural responses — flight in the case of fear, tears in the case of grief, and so forth. The conditions appear to discriminate emotions from other mental states such as moods, which may lack a definite object, and sensations, which need not dispose the subject to any particular behaviour. Yet we should inquire whether all three conditions are genuinely necessary. Let us imagine a man who learns by telegram of the death of a distant relative. He feels a sharp pang of sadness directed unmistakably toward the loss of that person and is, for a brief instant, disposed to weep. Yet at that very moment a colleague bursts into the room with an urgent request, and the man's attention is wholly diverted. The state of sadness passes before any tear is shed or any characteristic behaviour is manifested. It appears, however, that the man experienced a genuine, if fleeting, emotion. The qualitative feel was present, the intentional object was clearly specified, and there existed a momentary disposition to behaviour that was interrupted before it could issue in action. We are led to wonder whether the behavioural-disposition condition, taken strictly, is too demanding, and whether an adequate analysis should allow that an emotion may be genuine even when its characteristic behavioural expression is forestalled.

definition: A subject perceives an object if and only if (i) the object exists in the external world, (ii) the subject has a sensory experience that represents the object, and (iii) the sensory experience is caused by the object in an appropriate manner.
conditions: the object exists in the external world
the subject has a sensory experience that represents the object
the sensory experience is caused by the object in an appropriate manner
counterexample: Suppose that a man looks at a red apple on a table. The apple exists, and it causes light to enter his eyes; but the light is reflected off a cleverly placed mirror in such a way that he sees the apple as being two feet to the left of its actual position. He has a sensory experience of the apple, the apple exists, and the experience is caused by the apple — yet the causal chain is deviant, passing through the mirror in a way that produces a systematically misleading spatial representation. One would hesitate to say he genuinely perceives the apple where he takes it to be.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the experience be caused by the object 'in an appropriate manner' but does not specify what counts as appropriate, leaving it unable to exclude deviant causal chains that produce systematically inaccurate representations.
passage: A common analysis of perception holds that a subject perceives an object when the object exists, the subject has a sensory experience representing it, and the experience is caused by the object in an appropriate manner. The three conditions appear to distinguish veridical perception from hallucination — where no object exists — and from cases of mere coincidence, where an experience happens to match an object with which it has no causal connexion. Yet the phrase 'in an appropriate manner' conceals a difficulty that the definition does not resolve. Let us imagine a man who looks toward a table upon which a red apple rests. Light from the apple enters the room, but before reaching his eyes it is reflected off a mirror positioned behind him at a peculiar angle, so that the visual image he receives places the apple two feet to the left of its true location. The apple exists; the man has a sensory experience that represents an apple; and the experience is caused by the apple, since the apple is the ultimate source of the light. Yet the causal route is so deviant that the resulting perception is spatially misleading — the man reaches for the apple and grasps empty air. It appears that we should hesitate to call this genuine perception, for the causal mechanism has introduced systematic error. We are brought to recognise that the requirement of appropriate causation is doing essential work in the analysis, and yet the definition provides no criterion by which appropriate causal chains may be distinguished from deviant ones. Until this gap is filled, the analysis remains incomplete.

definition: A subject remembers an event if and only if (i) the event occurred, (ii) the subject has a present mental representation of the event, and (iii) the present representation was caused by the subject's original experience of the event.
conditions: the event occurred
the subject has a present mental representation of the event
the present representation was caused by the subject's original experience of the event
counterexample: Suppose that a woman witnessed a railway accident in her youth. Over the decades her genuine recollection fades entirely, but she later reads a vivid newspaper account of the same accident and forms a new mental representation that is, in all its details, accurate. The event occurred; she has a present representation of it; but the representation was caused not by her original experience but by her reading of the newspaper. She satisfies the first two conditions but fails the third, and yet she sincerely believes herself to be remembering the event, and her representation is entirely accurate.
missing_condition: The definition requires an unbroken causal link to the original experience, failing to address cases where an accurate representation of a genuinely experienced event is causally sustained by an intervening source rather than by the original perceptual encounter.
passage: A standard philosophical account of memory holds that a subject remembers an event when the event actually occurred, the subject presently represents it in thought, and this representation was caused by the subject's original experience of the event. The analysis is designed to exclude confabulation (where no event occurred), imagination (where no causal link to the past obtains), and mere information-retention (where the causal link may pass through testimony rather than experience). Yet one may observe a difficulty with the third condition. Let us imagine a woman who, as a child, witnessed a collision between two railway carriages. For many years she carried a vivid recollection of the scene. In time, however, the memory faded until nothing of it remained. Years later, she happens upon a newspaper account of the very accident she witnessed. The article is richly detailed, and upon reading it she forms a mental representation of the event that is, in every particular, accurate. She sincerely takes this representation to be a memory. The event occurred; she has a present representation of it; but the causal source of that representation is the newspaper, not her original perceptual experience. The third condition is unsatisfied. Yet the peculiarity of the case lies in the fact that she did experience the event, and her representation happens to correspond perfectly with what she saw. We are led to wonder whether the causal condition, as formulated, draws the line in the right place, for it excludes a case that is, in many respects, more memory-like than the definition allows.

definition: A creature is conscious if and only if (i) there is something it is like to be that creature, (ii) the creature can distinguish between itself and its environment, and (iii) the creature exhibits purposive behaviour directed toward goals.
conditions: there is something it is like to be that creature
the creature can distinguish between itself and its environment
the creature exhibits purposive behaviour directed toward goals
counterexample: Consider a case in which a sophisticated robot is programmed to distinguish between itself and its surroundings by means of sensors and to exhibit purposive behaviour — navigating obstacles, seeking power sources, avoiding damage. The second and third conditions are satisfied. Yet it is far from obvious that there is something it is like to be the robot; the machine may operate entirely without qualitative inner experience. If the first condition is not met, the robot is not conscious, despite satisfying the other two conditions. Conversely, if we stipulate that there is something it is like to be the robot, all three conditions are met, and we should have to call it conscious — a conclusion many would find premature.
missing_condition: The definition provides no criterion for determining whether the first condition — that there is something it is like to be the creature — obtains, rendering the analysis untestable in cases where purposive behaviour and self-environment discrimination are present but the existence of qualitative experience is doubtful.
passage: A tempting characterisation of consciousness proposes three conditions: that there is something it is like to be the creature in question, that the creature can distinguish between itself and its environment, and that it exhibits purposive behaviour directed toward goals. The first condition is intended to capture the qualitative, subjective aspect of consciousness; the second and third are behavioural indicators that might serve as evidence for its presence. Yet one may observe that the analysis faces a significant difficulty. Let us imagine a robot of considerable sophistication, equipped with an array of sensors that enable it to discriminate its own body from its surroundings, and programmed to pursue goals — navigating a room, locating a charging station, avoiding obstacles that might damage its chassis. The second and third conditions are plainly satisfied. Whether the first condition is met, however, is a question to which the analysis itself provides no answer. Is there something it is like to be this robot? The machine's behaviour is indistinguishable, in relevant respects, from that of a simple organism, and yet we may have no ground for attributing qualitative experience to it. The definition, then, includes a condition — the presence of subjective experience — for which it supplies no criterion of application, and this renders it inadequate as a tool for adjudicating the difficult cases it was presumably designed to address.

definition: An agent possesses free will if and only if (i) the agent's choices are determined by her own reasons and desires, (ii) the agent is not externally compelled, and (iii) the agent endorses, upon reflection, the desires that move her to act.
conditions: the agent's choices are determined by her own reasons and desires
the agent is not externally compelled
the agent endorses, upon reflection, the desires that move her to act
counterexample: Suppose that a man is addicted to a narcotic substance. His desire for the drug is emphatically his own; no external force compels him; and, in a peculiar twist, he reflectively endorses his addiction, sincerely judging that the pleasure of the drug makes his life worth living and that he would not wish to be otherwise. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might hesitate to say that he acts freely, for his endorsement of the desire is itself a product of the addiction's distortion of his evaluative capacities.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the agent's reflective endorsement be formed under conditions free from the distorting influence of the very desires being endorsed, leaving it unable to exclude cases in which addiction or compulsion corrupts the endorsing judgement itself.
passage: One influential account holds that free will obtains when an agent's choices flow from her own reasons and desires, she is free from external compulsion, and she reflectively endorses the desires that move her. The third condition is meant to distinguish the free agent from the unwilling addict — the person who acts on a desire she wishes she did not have. Yet one may construct a case which reveals a gap in this analysis. Let us imagine a man addicted to a powerful narcotic. His desire for the substance is undeniably his own; no one forces it upon him. Moreover, unlike the stereotypical unwilling addict, he sincerely endorses his addiction upon reflection. He regards the pleasure of the drug as the chief good in his life and would not wish to be rid of the desire even if he could. All three conditions are met. Yet one is inclined to doubt that he possesses free will in the fullest sense, for there is reason to suspect that his reflective endorsement has itself been corrupted by the very addiction it purports to evaluate. The drug has so reorganised his evaluative landscape that he can no longer assess the desire from a standpoint independent of its influence. We are thus led to recognise that the analysis requires not merely reflective endorsement but endorsement formed under conditions that are themselves free from the distorting influence of the desires in question — a condition the definition, as stated, does not impose.

definition: A person persists through time if and only if (i) there is spatiotemporal continuity of her body, (ii) her brain preserves functional continuity, and (iii) her personality and character remain recognisably the same.
conditions: there is spatiotemporal continuity of her body
her brain preserves functional continuity
her personality and character remain recognisably the same
counterexample: Suppose that a young man of gentle and scholarly temperament suffers a severe head injury, after which his personality is radically transformed: he becomes impulsive, coarse, and indifferent to the pursuits he once loved. His body is spatiotemporally continuous and his brain, though damaged, preserves its basic functional capacities — he can reason, speak, and remember. The first two conditions are satisfied but the third is not, for his personality and character are no longer recognisably the same. Yet his family, his physicians, and the law all regard him as the same person. One would be hard pressed to maintain that he has ceased to exist.
missing_condition: The definition makes continuity of personality and character a necessary condition of personal persistence, failing to accommodate cases of radical personality change — through injury, illness, or conversion — in which personal identity is preserved despite a thoroughgoing alteration of character.
passage: It has sometimes been proposed that personal persistence through time requires the conjunction of three conditions: bodily spatiotemporal continuity, functional continuity of the brain, and recognisable sameness of personality and character. This analysis has the merit of combining physical and psychological criteria, and in the ordinary case — where a person changes gradually and retains the broad outlines of her temperament — it delivers plausible verdicts. Yet we should consider whether the third condition can bear the weight placed upon it. Let us imagine a young man of mild and studious disposition who suffers a traumatic injury to the head. Upon recovery, his personality has been profoundly altered: he is now rash, boorish, and utterly indifferent to the books and ideas that once absorbed him. His body is the same body, traceable through an unbroken spatiotemporal path; his brain, though scarred, retains its functional capacities — he speaks, reasons, and remembers his past. But his personality and character are no longer recognisably those of the person he was before the injury. On the proposed analysis, the third condition is unsatisfied, and we should have to conclude that the original person no longer persists. Yet this conclusion appears intolerably strong. His mother recognises him; the law holds him accountable for his former debts; and he himself, for all his altered temperament, recalls his earlier life. We are led to suspect that continuity of personality, though normally attendant upon personal persistence, is not among its necessary conditions.
